subject: Mathematics
question_html: One bag contains 4 white and 5 black balls. Another bag contains 6 white and 7 black balls. A ball is transferred from first bag to the second bag and then a ball is drawn from the second bag. Find the probability that the ball drawn is white.
question_text: One bag contains 4 white and 5 black balls. Another bag contains 6 white and 7 black balls. A ball is transferred from first bag to the second bag and then a ball is drawn from the second bag. Find the probability that the ball drawn is white.
explanation: A white ball can be drawn in two mutually exclusive ways: By transferring a black ball from first to second bag, then drawing a white ball By transferring a white ball from first to second bag, then drawing a white ball Consider the following events: E 1 = A black ball is transferred from first to second bag E 2 = A white ball is transferred from first to second bag A = A white ball is drawn Therefore, we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex} Now, {tex}P\left(\frac {A}{ E_{1}}\right)=\frac{6}{14}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac {A}{E_{2}}\right)=\frac{7}{14}{/tex} Using the law of total probability, we get Required probability = P(A) = P(E 1 )P( {tex}\frac {A}{E_1}{/tex} ) + P(E 2 )P( {tex}\frac {A}{E_2}{/tex} ) {tex}=\frac{5}{9} \times \frac{6}{14}+\frac{4}{9} \times \frac{7}{14}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{30}{126}+\frac{28}{126}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{58}{126}=\frac{29}{63}{/tex}
solution: A white ball can be drawn in two mutually exclusive ways: By transferring a black ball from first to second bag, then drawing a white ball By transferring a white ball from first to second bag, then drawing a white ball Consider the following events: E 1 = A black ball is transferred from first to second bag E 2 = A white ball is transferred from first to second bag A = A white ball is drawn Therefore, we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex} Now, {tex}P\left(\frac {A}{ E_{1}}\right)=\frac{6}{14}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac {A}{E_{2}}\right)=\frac{7}{14}{/tex} Using the law of total probability, we get Required probability = P(A) = P(E 1 )P( {tex}\frac {A}{E_1}{/tex} ) + P(E 2 )P( {tex}\frac {A}{E_2}{/tex} ) {tex}=\frac{5}{9} \times \frac{6}{14}+\frac{4}{9} \times \frac{7}{14}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{30}{126}+\frac{28}{126}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{58}{126}=\frac{29}{63}{/tex}
solution_html:
A white ball can be drawn in two mutually exclusive ways:

  1. By transferring a black ball from first to second bag, then drawing a white ball
  2. By transferring a white ball from first to second bag, then drawing a white ball

Consider the following events:
E1 = A black ball is transferred from first to second bag
E2 = A white ball is transferred from first to second bag
A = A white ball is drawn
Therefore, we have,
P(E1) = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
P(E2) = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}P\left(\frac {A}{ E_{1}}\right)=\frac{6}{14}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac {A}{E_{2}}\right)=\frac{7}{14}{/tex}
Using the law of total probability, we get
Required probability = P(A) = P(E1)P({tex}\frac {A}{E_1}{/tex}) + P(E2)P({tex}\frac {A}{E_2}{/tex})
{tex}=\frac{5}{9} \times \frac{6}{14}+\frac{4}{9} \times \frac{7}{14}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{30}{126}+\frac{28}{126}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{58}{126}=\frac{29}{63}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: For the vacancy advertised in the newspaper, 3000 candidates submitted their applications. From the data it was revealed that two third of the total applicants were females and other were males. The selection for the job was done through a written test. The performance of the applicants indicates that the probability of a male getting a distinction in written test is 0.4 and that a female getting a distinction is 0.35. Find the probability that the candidate chosen at random will have a distinction in the written test.
question_text: For the vacancy advertised in the newspaper, 3000 candidates submitted their applications. From the data it was revealed that two third of the total applicants were females and other were males. The selection for the job was done through a written test. The performance of the applicants indicates that the probability of a male getting a distinction in written test is 0.4 and that a female getting a distinction is 0.35. Find the probability that the candidate chosen at random will have a distinction in the written test.
explanation: Let {tex}{E}_{1}{/tex} : The applicant is a male {tex}{E}_{2}{/tex} :The applicant is a female {tex}A{/tex} :The candidate chosen will have distinction in the written test. {tex}P\left(E_{1}\right)=\frac{1}{3}, P\left(E_{2}\right)=\frac{2}{3},{/tex} {tex} P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right)=0.4, P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right)=0.35{/tex} {tex}\therefore P(A)=P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3} \times 0.4+\frac{2}{3} \times 0.35{/tex} {tex}=\frac{11}{30}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}{E}_{1}{/tex} : The applicant is a male {tex}{E}_{2}{/tex} :The applicant is a female {tex}A{/tex} :The candidate chosen will have distinction in the written test. {tex}P\left(E_{1}\right)=\frac{1}{3}, P\left(E_{2}\right)=\frac{2}{3},{/tex} {tex} P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right)=0.4, P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right)=0.35{/tex} {tex}\therefore P(A)=P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3} \times 0.4+\frac{2}{3} \times 0.35{/tex} {tex}=\frac{11}{30}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}{E}_{1}{/tex} : The applicant is a male
{tex}{E}_{2}{/tex}:The applicant is a female
{tex}A{/tex}:The candidate chosen will have distinction in the written test.
{tex}P\left(E_{1}\right)=\frac{1}{3}, P\left(E_{2}\right)=\frac{2}{3},{/tex} {tex} P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right)=0.4, P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right)=0.35{/tex}
{tex}\therefore P(A)=P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right){/tex}{tex}+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{3} \times 0.4+\frac{2}{3} \times 0.35{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{11}{30}{/tex}

question_html:
Assertion (A): If A and B are two events such that {tex}{P}({A} \cap {B})=0{/tex}, then A and B are independent events.
Reason {tex}(R){/tex}: Two events are independent if the occurrence of one does not effect the occurrence of the other.
question_text: Assertion (A): If A and B are two events such that {tex}{P}({A} \cap {B})=0{/tex} , then A and B are independent events. Reason {tex}(R){/tex} : Two events are independent if the occurrence of one does not effect the occurrence of the other.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: In a village of 8000 people, 3000 go out of the village to work and 4000 are women. It is noted that {tex}30 \%{/tex} of women go out of the village to work. What is the probability that a randomly chosen individual is either a woman or a person working outside the village?
question_text: In a village of 8000 people, 3000 go out of the village to work and 4000 are women. It is noted that {tex}30 \%{/tex} of women go out of the village to work. What is the probability that a randomly chosen individual is either a woman or a person working outside the village?
explanation: {tex}{A}={A}{/tex} randomly chosen person is a woman {tex}B=A{/tex} randomly chosen person works outside village. {tex}P(A)=\frac{4000}{8000}=\frac{1}{2}, {/tex} {tex}P(B)=\frac{3000}{8000}=\frac{3}{8}, {/tex} {tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{1200}{8000}=\frac{3}{20}{/tex} Required probability {tex}=P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{3}{8}-\frac{3}{20}=\frac{29}{40} {/tex}
solution: {tex}{A}={A}{/tex} randomly chosen person is a woman {tex}B=A{/tex} randomly chosen person works outside village. {tex}P(A)=\frac{4000}{8000}=\frac{1}{2}, {/tex} {tex}P(B)=\frac{3000}{8000}=\frac{3}{8}, {/tex} {tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{1200}{8000}=\frac{3}{20}{/tex} Required probability {tex}=P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{3}{8}-\frac{3}{20}=\frac{29}{40} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{A}={A}{/tex} randomly chosen person is a woman
{tex}B=A{/tex} randomly chosen person works outside village.
{tex}P(A)=\frac{4000}{8000}=\frac{1}{2}, {/tex} {tex}P(B)=\frac{3000}{8000}=\frac{3}{8}, {/tex} {tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{1200}{8000}=\frac{3}{20}{/tex}
Required probability {tex}=P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{3}{8}-\frac{3}{20}=\frac{29}{40} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
The probability that a student buys a colouring book is 0.7 and that she buys a box of colours is {tex}0.2{/tex}. The probability that she buys a colouring book, given that she buys a box of colours, is {tex}0 \cdot 3{/tex}. Find the probability that the student:

  1. Buys both the colouring book and the box of colours.
  2. Buys a box of colours given that she buys the colouring book.
question_text: The probability that a student buys a colouring book is 0.7 and that she buys a box of colours is {tex}0.2{/tex} . The probability that she buys a colouring book, given that she buys a box of colours, is {tex}0 \cdot 3{/tex} . Find the probability that the student: Buys both the colouring book and the box of colours. Buys a box of colours given that she buys the colouring book.
explanation: Let A be the event of buying colouring book and {tex}B{/tex} be the event of buying coloured box. {tex}P(A)=0.7, P(B)=0.2, P(A / B)=0.3{/tex} {tex}{P}\left(\frac{{A}}{{B}}\right)=\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{{P}({B})} \Rightarrow 0.3=\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{0.2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P(A \cap B)=0.06 \text { or } \frac{3}{50}{/tex} {tex}{P}\left(\frac{{B}}{{A}}\right)=\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{{P}({A})}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.06}{0.7}=\frac{3}{35} \text { or } 0.086{/tex}
solution: Let A be the event of buying colouring book and {tex}B{/tex} be the event of buying coloured box. {tex}P(A)=0.7, P(B)=0.2, P(A / B)=0.3{/tex} {tex}{P}\left(\frac{{A}}{{B}}\right)=\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{{P}({B})} \Rightarrow 0.3=\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{0.2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P(A \cap B)=0.06 \text { or } \frac{3}{50}{/tex} {tex}{P}\left(\frac{{B}}{{A}}\right)=\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{{P}({A})}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.06}{0.7}=\frac{3}{35} \text { or } 0.086{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A be the event of buying colouring book and {tex}B{/tex} be the event of buying coloured box.
{tex}P(A)=0.7, P(B)=0.2, P(A / B)=0.3{/tex}

  1. {tex}{P}\left(\frac{{A}}{{B}}\right)=\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{{P}({B})} \Rightarrow 0.3=\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{0.2}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow P(A \cap B)=0.06 \text { or } \frac{3}{50}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{P}\left(\frac{{B}}{{A}}\right)=\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{{P}({A})}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{0.06}{0.7}=\frac{3}{35} \text { or } 0.086{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A bank offers loan to its customers on different types of interest namely, fixed rate, floating rate and variable rate. From the past data with the bank, it is known that a customer avails loan on fixed rate, floating rate or variable rate with probabilities {tex}10 \%, 20 \%{/tex} and {tex}70 \%{/tex} respectively. A customer after availing loan can pay the loan or default on loan repayment. The bank data suggests that the probability that a person defaults on loan after availing it at fixed rate, floating rate and variable rate is {tex}5 \%, 3 \%{/tex} and {tex}1 \%{/tex} respectively.
Based on the above information, answer the following :

  1. What is the probability that a customer after availing the loan will default on the loan repayment? (2)
  2. A customer after availing the loan, defaults on loan repayment. What is the probability that he availed the loan at a variable rate of interest? (2)
question_text: A bank offers loan to its customers on different types of interest namely, fixed rate, floating rate and variable rate. From the past data with the bank, it is known that a customer avails loan on fixed rate, floating rate or variable rate with probabilities {tex}10 \%, 20 \%{/tex} and {tex}70 \%{/tex} respectively. A customer after availing loan can pay the loan or default on loan repayment. The bank data suggests that the probability that a person defaults on loan after availing it at fixed rate, floating rate and variable rate is {tex}5 \%, 3 \%{/tex} and {tex}1 \%{/tex} respectively. Based on the above information, answer the following : What is the probability that a customer after availing the loan will default on the loan repayment? (2) A customer after availing the loan, defaults on loan repayment. What is the probability that he availed the loan at a variable rate of interest? (2)
explanation: {tex}E_1{/tex} : customer avails loan on fixed rate {tex}E_2{/tex} : customer avails loan on floating rate {tex}E_3{/tex} : customer avails loan on variable rate A:the person defaults on the loan {tex} {P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{1}{10}, {P}\left(E_2\right)=\frac{2}{10}, {P}\left(E_3\right)=\frac{7}{10} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right) =\frac{5}{100}, {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{3}{100}, {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)=\frac{1}{100} {/tex} {tex}{P}(A) ={P}\left(E_1\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) {/tex} {tex}\cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)+{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{10} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \times \frac{3}{100}+\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{1}{100} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{18}{1000} \text { or } \frac{9}{500} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(E_3 \mid A\right) {/tex} {tex}=\frac{{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{{P}\left(E_1\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)+{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{1}{100}}{\frac{18}{1000}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{7}{18} {/tex}
solution: {tex}E_1{/tex} : customer avails loan on fixed rate {tex}E_2{/tex} : customer avails loan on floating rate {tex}E_3{/tex} : customer avails loan on variable rate A:the person defaults on the loan {tex} {P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{1}{10}, {P}\left(E_2\right)=\frac{2}{10}, {P}\left(E_3\right)=\frac{7}{10} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right) =\frac{5}{100}, {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{3}{100}, {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)=\frac{1}{100} {/tex} {tex}{P}(A) ={P}\left(E_1\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) {/tex} {tex}\cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)+{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{10} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \times \frac{3}{100}+\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{1}{100} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{18}{1000} \text { or } \frac{9}{500} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(E_3 \mid A\right) {/tex} {tex}=\frac{{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{{P}\left(E_1\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)+{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{1}{100}}{\frac{18}{1000}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{7}{18} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}E_1{/tex} : customer avails loan on fixed rate
{tex}E_2{/tex} : customer avails loan on floating rate
{tex}E_3{/tex} : customer avails loan on variable rate
A:the person defaults on the loan
{tex} {P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{1}{10}, {P}\left(E_2\right)=\frac{2}{10}, {P}\left(E_3\right)=\frac{7}{10} {/tex}
{tex} {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right) =\frac{5}{100}, {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{3}{100}, {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)=\frac{1}{100} {/tex}

  1. {tex}{P}(A) ={P}\left(E_1\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) {/tex}{tex}\cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)+{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right) {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{10} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \times \frac{3}{100}+\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{1}{100} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{18}{1000} \text { or } \frac{9}{500} {/tex}
  2. {tex} {P}\left(E_3 \mid A\right) {/tex} {tex}=\frac{{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{{P}\left(E_1\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)+{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{1}{100}}{\frac{18}{1000}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{7}{18} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Three persons viz. Amber, Bonzi and Comet are manufacturing cars which run on petrol and on battery as well. Their production share in the market is {tex}60 \%, 30 \%{/tex} and {tex}10 \%{/tex} respectively. Of their respective production capacities, {tex}20 \%, 10 \%{/tex} and {tex}5 \%{/tex} cars respectively are electric (or battery operated). Based on the above, answer the following:

  1. (a) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is an electric car? (2)

OR

  1. (b) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is a petrol car? (2)
  2. A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Comet? (1)
  3. A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Amber or Bonzi? (1)
question_text: Three persons viz. Amber, Bonzi and Comet are manufacturing cars which run on petrol and on battery as well. Their production share in the market is {tex}60 \%, 30 \%{/tex} and {tex}10 \%{/tex} respectively. Of their respective production capacities, {tex}20 \%, 10 \%{/tex} and {tex}5 \%{/tex} cars respectively are electric (or battery operated). Based on the above, answer the following: (a) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is an electric car? (2) OR (b) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is a petrol car? (2) A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Comet? (1) A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Amber or Bonzi? (1)
explanation: (i) (a) Let {tex}{A}={Amber}{/tex} manufactures the car {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is electric {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{155}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{31}{200}{/tex} OR (b) Let {tex}{A}={/tex} Amber manufactures the car {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is a petrol car {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{90}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{95}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{845}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{169}{200}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{E}\right)=\frac{P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{P(E)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{50}{10000}}{\frac{1550}{10000}}=\frac{1}{31}{/tex} {tex}{P}\left(\frac{{A} \text { or } {B}}{{E}}\right)=1-{P}\left(\frac{{C}}{{E}}\right)=1-\frac{1}{31}=\frac{30}{31}{/tex}
solution: (i) (a) Let {tex}{A}={Amber}{/tex} manufactures the car {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is electric {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{155}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{31}{200}{/tex} OR (b) Let {tex}{A}={/tex} Amber manufactures the car {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is a petrol car {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{90}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{95}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{845}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{169}{200}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{E}\right)=\frac{P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{P(E)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{50}{10000}}{\frac{1550}{10000}}=\frac{1}{31}{/tex} {tex}{P}\left(\frac{{A} \text { or } {B}}{{E}}\right)=1-{P}\left(\frac{{C}}{{E}}\right)=1-\frac{1}{31}=\frac{30}{31}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. (i) (a) Let {tex}{A}={Amber}{/tex} manufactures the car
    {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car
    {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car
    {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is electric
    {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{155}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{31}{200}{/tex}

OR

  1. (b) Let {tex}{A}={/tex} Amber manufactures the car
    {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car
    {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car
    {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is a petrol car
    {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{90}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{95}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{845}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{169}{200}{/tex}
  2. {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{E}\right)=\frac{P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{P(E)}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\frac{50}{10000}}{\frac{1550}{10000}}=\frac{1}{31}{/tex}
  3. {tex}{P}\left(\frac{{A} \text { or } {B}}{{E}}\right)=1-{P}\left(\frac{{C}}{{E}}\right)=1-\frac{1}{31}=\frac{30}{31}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Two dice are thrown. Defined are the following two events A and B: {tex}{A}=\{(x, {y}): x+{y}=9\}, {B}=\{(x, {y}): x \neq 3\}{/tex}, where {tex}(x, {y}){/tex} denote a point in the sample space.
Check if events A and B are independent or mutually exclusive.
question_text: Two dice are thrown. Defined are the following two events A and B: {tex}{A}=\{(x, {y}): x+{y}=9\}, {B}=\{(x, {y}): x \neq 3\}{/tex} , where {tex}(x, {y}){/tex} denote a point in the sample space. Check if events A and B are independent or mutually exclusive.
explanation: A = {(3, 6), (4, 5), (5, 4), (6, 3) {tex}P(A)=\frac{4}{36}=\frac{1}{9}, P(B)=\frac{30}{36}=\frac{5}{6}{/tex} {tex}{P}({A} \cap {B})=\frac{3}{36}=\frac{1}{12}{/tex} {tex}P(A) \times P(B)=\frac{5}{54} \neq P(A \cap B){/tex} Therefore, A and B are not independent. A and B are not mutually exclusive as {tex}A \cap B \neq \varnothing{/tex}
solution: A = {(3, 6), (4, 5), (5, 4), (6, 3) {tex}P(A)=\frac{4}{36}=\frac{1}{9}, P(B)=\frac{30}{36}=\frac{5}{6}{/tex} {tex}{P}({A} \cap {B})=\frac{3}{36}=\frac{1}{12}{/tex} {tex}P(A) \times P(B)=\frac{5}{54} \neq P(A \cap B){/tex} Therefore, A and B are not independent. A and B are not mutually exclusive as {tex}A \cap B \neq \varnothing{/tex}
solution_html:
A = {(3, 6), (4, 5), (5, 4), (6, 3)
{tex}P(A)=\frac{4}{36}=\frac{1}{9}, P(B)=\frac{30}{36}=\frac{5}{6}{/tex}
{tex}{P}({A} \cap {B})=\frac{3}{36}=\frac{1}{12}{/tex}
{tex}P(A) \times P(B)=\frac{5}{54} \neq P(A \cap B){/tex}
Therefore, A and B are not independent.
A and B are not mutually exclusive as {tex}A \cap B \neq \varnothing{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
In a city, a survey was conducted among residents about their preferred mode of commuting. It was found that {tex}50 \%{/tex} people preferred using public transport, 35\% preferred using a bicycle and {tex}20 \%{/tex} use both public transport and a bicycle. If a person is selected at random, find the probability that:

  1. The person uses only public transport.
  2. The person uses a bicycle, given that they also use the public transport.
  3. The person uses neither public transport nor a bicycle.
question_text: In a city, a survey was conducted among residents about their preferred mode of commuting. It was found that {tex}50 \%{/tex} people preferred using public transport, 35\% preferred using a bicycle and {tex}20 \%{/tex} use both public transport and a bicycle. If a person is selected at random, find the probability that: The person uses only public transport. The person uses a bicycle, given that they also use the public transport. The person uses neither public transport nor a bicycle.
explanation: Let {tex}T{/tex} : Person uses public transport {tex}B{/tex} : Person uses a bicycle Given {tex}P(T)=\frac{50}{100}, P(B)=\frac{35}{100},{/tex} {tex} P(T \cap B)=\frac{20}{100}{/tex} {tex}P({/tex} only {tex}T)=P(T)-P(T \cap B){/tex} {tex}=\frac{50}{100}-\frac{20}{100}=\frac{30}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(B \mid T)=\frac{P(T \cap B)}{P(T)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{20}{100}}{\frac{50}{100}}=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}P\left(T^{\prime} \cap B^{\prime}\right)=1-P(T \cup B){/tex} {tex}=1-[P(T)+P(B)-P(T \cap B)]{/tex} {tex}=1-\left[\frac{50}{100}+\frac{35}{100}-\frac{20}{100}\right]{/tex} {tex}=1-\frac{65}{100}=\frac{35}{100}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}T{/tex} : Person uses public transport {tex}B{/tex} : Person uses a bicycle Given {tex}P(T)=\frac{50}{100}, P(B)=\frac{35}{100},{/tex} {tex} P(T \cap B)=\frac{20}{100}{/tex} {tex}P({/tex} only {tex}T)=P(T)-P(T \cap B){/tex} {tex}=\frac{50}{100}-\frac{20}{100}=\frac{30}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(B \mid T)=\frac{P(T \cap B)}{P(T)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{20}{100}}{\frac{50}{100}}=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}P\left(T^{\prime} \cap B^{\prime}\right)=1-P(T \cup B){/tex} {tex}=1-[P(T)+P(B)-P(T \cap B)]{/tex} {tex}=1-\left[\frac{50}{100}+\frac{35}{100}-\frac{20}{100}\right]{/tex} {tex}=1-\frac{65}{100}=\frac{35}{100}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}T{/tex}: Person uses public transport
{tex}B{/tex}: Person uses a bicycle
Given {tex}P(T)=\frac{50}{100}, P(B)=\frac{35}{100},{/tex} {tex} P(T \cap B)=\frac{20}{100}{/tex}

  1. {tex}P({/tex}only {tex}T)=P(T)-P(T \cap B){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{50}{100}-\frac{20}{100}=\frac{30}{100}{/tex}
  2. {tex}P(B \mid T)=\frac{P(T \cap B)}{P(T)}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\frac{20}{100}}{\frac{50}{100}}=\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
  3. {tex}P\left(T^{\prime} \cap B^{\prime}\right)=1-P(T \cup B){/tex}
    {tex}=1-[P(T)+P(B)-P(T \cap B)]{/tex}
    {tex}=1-\left[\frac{50}{100}+\frac{35}{100}-\frac{20}{100}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=1-\frac{65}{100}=\frac{35}{100}{/tex}

question_html: Consider the experiment of tossing a coin. If the coin shows head, toss it again; but if it shows a tail, then throw a die. Find the conditional probability of the event A: 'the die shows a number greater than 3' given that B: 'there is at least one tail'.
question_text: Consider the experiment of tossing a coin. If the coin shows head, toss it again; but if it shows a tail, then throw a die. Find the conditional probability of the event A: 'the die shows a number greater than 3' given that B: 'there is at least one tail'.
explanation: Let {tex}A{/tex} :The die shows a number greater than 3 and {tex}B{/tex} :There is at least one tail {tex}{P}(B \cap A)=P(T 4, T 5, T 6)=\frac{3}{12}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}P(B)=P(H T, T 1, T 2, T 3, T 4, T 5, T 6){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{6}{12}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}P(A \mid B)=\frac{{P}(B \cap A)}{P(B)}=\frac{1 / 4}{3 / 4}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}A{/tex} :The die shows a number greater than 3 and {tex}B{/tex} :There is at least one tail {tex}{P}(B \cap A)=P(T 4, T 5, T 6)=\frac{3}{12}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}P(B)=P(H T, T 1, T 2, T 3, T 4, T 5, T 6){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{6}{12}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}P(A \mid B)=\frac{{P}(B \cap A)}{P(B)}=\frac{1 / 4}{3 / 4}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}A{/tex} :The die shows a number greater than 3
and {tex}B{/tex} :There is at least one tail
{tex}{P}(B \cap A)=P(T 4, T 5, T 6)=\frac{3}{12}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}P(B)=P(H T, T 1, T 2, T 3, T 4, T 5, T 6){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{6}{12}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}P(A \mid B)=\frac{{P}(B \cap A)}{P(B)}=\frac{1 / 4}{3 / 4}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Two persons are competing for a position on the Managing Committee of an organisation. The probabilities that the first and the second person will be appointed are 0.5 and 0.6 respectively. Also, if the first person gets appointed, then the probability of introducing waste treatment plant is {tex}0. 7{/tex} and the corresponding probability is {tex}0.4{/tex}, if the second person gets appointed.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. What is the probability that the waste treatment plant is introduced?
  2. After the selection, if the waste treatment plant is introduced, what is the probability that the first person had introduced it?
question_text: Two persons are competing for a position on the Managing Committee of an organisation. The probabilities that the first and the second person will be appointed are 0.5 and 0.6 respectively. Also, if the first person gets appointed, then the probability of introducing waste treatment plant is {tex}0. 7{/tex} and the corresponding probability is {tex}0.4{/tex} , if the second person gets appointed. Based on the above information, answer the following questions: What is the probability that the waste treatment plant is introduced? After the selection, if the waste treatment plant is introduced, what is the probability that the first person had introduced it?
explanation: {tex}E_{1}{/tex} : Event that the first person is appointed. {tex}E_{2}{/tex} : Event that the second person is appointed. A: Event that the waste treatment plant is introducted. Here, {tex}{P}\left(E_{1}\right)=0.5, P\left(E_{2}\right)=0.6{/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right)=0.7, P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right)=0.4{/tex} {tex}P{/tex} (waste treatment plant is introducted) {tex}=P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=(0.5)(0.7)+(0.6)(0.4){/tex} {tex}=0.35+0.24=0.59{/tex} {tex} P\left(E_1 \mid A\right) =\frac{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{(0.5)(0.7)}{(0.5)(0.7)+(0.6)(0.4)} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{0.35}{0.59}=\frac{35}{59}{/tex}
solution: {tex}E_{1}{/tex} : Event that the first person is appointed. {tex}E_{2}{/tex} : Event that the second person is appointed. A: Event that the waste treatment plant is introducted. Here, {tex}{P}\left(E_{1}\right)=0.5, P\left(E_{2}\right)=0.6{/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right)=0.7, P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right)=0.4{/tex} {tex}P{/tex} (waste treatment plant is introducted) {tex}=P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=(0.5)(0.7)+(0.6)(0.4){/tex} {tex}=0.35+0.24=0.59{/tex} {tex} P\left(E_1 \mid A\right) =\frac{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{(0.5)(0.7)}{(0.5)(0.7)+(0.6)(0.4)} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{0.35}{0.59}=\frac{35}{59}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}E_{1}{/tex}: Event that the first person is appointed.
{tex}E_{2}{/tex}: Event that the second person is appointed.
A: Event that the waste treatment plant is introducted.
Here, {tex}{P}\left(E_{1}\right)=0.5, P\left(E_{2}\right)=0.6{/tex}
{tex}P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right)=0.7, P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right)=0.4{/tex}

  1. {tex}P{/tex} (waste treatment plant is introducted)
    {tex}=P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=(0.5)(0.7)+(0.6)(0.4){/tex}
    {tex}=0.35+0.24=0.59{/tex}
  2. {tex} P\left(E_1 \mid A\right) =\frac{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{(0.5)(0.7)}{(0.5)(0.7)+(0.6)(0.4)} {/tex}
    {tex}= \frac{0.35}{0.59}=\frac{35}{59}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Some students are having a misconception while comparing decimals. For example, a student may mention that {tex}78.56 \gt 78.9{/tex} as {tex}7856 \gt 789{/tex}. In order to assess this concept, a decimal comparison test was administered to the students of class VI through the following question : In the recently held Sports Day in the school, 5 students participated in a javelin throw competition. The distances to which they have thrown the javelin are shown below in the table :

Name of student Distance of javelin (in meters)
Ajay {tex}47 .7{/tex}
Bijoy {tex}47 .07{/tex}
Kartik {tex}43.09{/tex}
Dinesh {tex}43 . 9{/tex}
Devesh {tex}45.2{/tex}

The students were asked to identify who has thrown the javelin the farthest.
Based on the test attempted by the students, the teacher concludes that {tex}40 \%{/tex} of the students have the misconception in the concept of decimal comparison and the rest do not have the misconception. {tex}80 \%{/tex} of the students having misconception answered Bijoy as the correct answer in the paper. {tex}90 \%{/tex} of the students who are identified with not having misconception, did not answer Bijoy as their answer.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions :

  1. What is the probability of a student not having misconception but still answers Bijoy in the test? (1)
  2. What is the probability that a randomly selected student answers Bijoy as his answer in the test? (1)
    1. What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is having misconception? (2)

      OR

    2.  What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is amongst students who do not have the misconception? (2)
question_text: Some students are having a misconception while comparing decimals. For example, a student may mention that {tex}78.56 \gt 78.9{/tex} as {tex}7856 \gt 789{/tex} . In order to assess this concept, a decimal comparison test was administered to the students of class VI through the following question : In the recently held Sports Day in the school, 5 students participated in a javelin throw competition. The distances to which they have thrown the javelin are shown below in the table : Name of student Distance of javelin (in meters) Ajay {tex}47 .7{/tex} Bijoy {tex}47 .07{/tex} Kartik {tex}43.09{/tex} Dinesh {tex}43 . 9{/tex} Devesh {tex}45.2{/tex} The students were asked to identify who has thrown the javelin the farthest. Based on the test attempted by the students, the teacher concludes that {tex}40 \%{/tex} of the students have the misconception in the concept of decimal comparison and the rest do not have the misconception. {tex}80 \%{/tex} of the students having misconception answered Bijoy as the correct answer in the paper. {tex}90 \%{/tex} of the students who are identified with not having misconception, did not answer Bijoy as their answer. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions : What is the probability of a student not having misconception but still answers Bijoy in the test? (1) What is the probability that a randomly selected student answers Bijoy as his answer in the test? (1) What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is having misconception? (2) OR What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is amongst students who do not have the misconception? (2)
explanation: Let {tex}E_1{/tex} : Student has a misconception {tex}E_2{/tex} : Student does not have misconception A: Student answered Bijoy as correct {tex} \therefore P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{40}{100}, {P}\left(E_2\right)=\frac{60}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(A \mid E_1\right)=\frac{80}{100}, P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_1\right)=\frac{20}{100}, P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_2\right)=\frac{90}{100} {/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}P(A)={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right){/tex} {tex} =\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{38}{100} \text { or } \frac{19}{50} {/tex} {tex}P\left(E_1 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)}{P(A)}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{16}{19} {/tex} OR {tex}P\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{3}{19} {/tex}
solution: Let {tex}E_1{/tex} : Student has a misconception {tex}E_2{/tex} : Student does not have misconception A: Student answered Bijoy as correct {tex} \therefore P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{40}{100}, {P}\left(E_2\right)=\frac{60}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(A \mid E_1\right)=\frac{80}{100}, P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_1\right)=\frac{20}{100}, P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_2\right)=\frac{90}{100} {/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}P(A)={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right){/tex} {tex} =\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{38}{100} \text { or } \frac{19}{50} {/tex} {tex}P\left(E_1 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)}{P(A)}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{16}{19} {/tex} OR {tex}P\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{3}{19} {/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}E_1{/tex} : Student has a misconception
{tex}E_2{/tex} : Student does not have misconception
A: Student answered Bijoy as correct
{tex} \therefore P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{40}{100}, {P}\left(E_2\right)=\frac{60}{100} {/tex}
{tex} P\left(A \mid E_1\right)=\frac{80}{100}, P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100} {/tex}
{tex} P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_1\right)=\frac{20}{100}, P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_2\right)=\frac{90}{100} {/tex}

  1. {tex}P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
  2. {tex}P(A)={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right){/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{38}{100} \text { or } \frac{19}{50} {/tex}
    1. {tex}P\left(E_1 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)}{P(A)}{/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{16}{19} {/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}P\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)}{/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{3}{19} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A shop selling electronic items sells smartphones of only three reputed companies {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C because chances of their manufacturing a defective smartphone are only {tex}5 \%, 4 \%{/tex} and {tex}2 \%{/tex} respectively. In his inventory he has 25% smartphones from company A, 35% smartphones from company B and {tex}40 \%{/tex} smartphones from company C.
A person buys a smartphone from this shop.

  1. Find the probability that it was defective. (2)
  2. What is the probability that this defective smartphone was manufactured by company B? (2)
question_text: A shop selling electronic items sells smartphones of only three reputed companies {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C because chances of their manufacturing a defective smartphone are only {tex}5 \%, 4 \%{/tex} and {tex}2 \%{/tex} respectively. In his inventory he has 25% smartphones from company A, 35% smartphones from company B and {tex}40 \%{/tex} smartphones from company C. A person buys a smartphone from this shop. Find the probability that it was defective. (2) What is the probability that this defective smartphone was manufactured by company B? (2)
explanation: {tex}{P}({/tex} defective smartphone) {tex}=0.25 \times 0.05+0.35 \times 0.04+0.40 \times 0.02{/tex} {tex}=0.0345{/tex} {tex}{P}({~B} / \text { Defective}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.35 \times 0.04}{0.25 \times 0.05+0.35 \times 0.04+0.40 \times 0.02}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{140}{345} \text { or } \frac{28}{69}{/tex}
solution: {tex}{P}({/tex} defective smartphone) {tex}=0.25 \times 0.05+0.35 \times 0.04+0.40 \times 0.02{/tex} {tex}=0.0345{/tex} {tex}{P}({~B} / \text { Defective}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.35 \times 0.04}{0.25 \times 0.05+0.35 \times 0.04+0.40 \times 0.02}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{140}{345} \text { or } \frac{28}{69}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}{P}({/tex}defective smartphone) {tex}=0.25 \times 0.05+0.35 \times 0.04+0.40 \times 0.02{/tex}
    {tex}=0.0345{/tex}
  2. {tex}{P}({~B} / \text { Defective}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.35 \times 0.04}{0.25 \times 0.05+0.35 \times 0.04+0.40 \times 0.02}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{140}{345} \text { or } \frac{28}{69}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Four students of class XII are given a problem to solve independently. Their chances of solving the problem respectively are {tex}\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}. Find the probability that at most one of them will solve the problem.
question_text: Four students of class XII are given a problem to solve independently. Their chances of solving the problem respectively are {tex}\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} . Find the probability that at most one of them will solve the problem.
explanation: {tex}P(\text { at most one of them will solve the problem }){/tex} {tex}=P(\text { none of them solves the problem }){/tex} {tex}+P(\text { only one of them solves the problem }){/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3}\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{5}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{19}{45}{/tex}
solution: {tex}P(\text { at most one of them will solve the problem }){/tex} {tex}=P(\text { none of them solves the problem }){/tex} {tex}+P(\text { only one of them solves the problem }){/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3}\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{5}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{19}{45}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}P(\text { at most one of them will solve the problem }){/tex}
{tex}=P(\text { none of them solves the problem }){/tex} {tex}+P(\text { only one of them solves the problem }){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3}\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{5}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{19}{45}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A person is Head of two independent selection committees I and II. If the probability of making a wrong selection in committee I is 0.03 and that in committee II is {tex}0.01{/tex}, then find the probability that the person makes the correct decision of selection:

  1. in both committees
  2. in only one committee
question_text: A person is Head of two independent selection committees I and II. If the probability of making a wrong selection in committee I is 0.03 and that in committee II is {tex}0.01{/tex} , then find the probability that the person makes the correct decision of selection: in both committees in only one committee
explanation: {tex}{P}({/tex} correct decision in both committees) {tex}=(1-0.03) \cdot(1-0.01)=0.9603{/tex} {tex}{P}({/tex} correct decision in one committee) {tex}=0.03 .(1-0.01)+(1-0.03) .0 .01 =0.0394{/tex}
solution: {tex}{P}({/tex} correct decision in both committees) {tex}=(1-0.03) \cdot(1-0.01)=0.9603{/tex} {tex}{P}({/tex} correct decision in one committee) {tex}=0.03 .(1-0.01)+(1-0.03) .0 .01 =0.0394{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}{P}({/tex}correct decision in both committees) {tex}=(1-0.03) \cdot(1-0.01)=0.9603{/tex}
  2. {tex}{P}({/tex}correct decision in one committee){tex}=0.03 .(1-0.01)+(1-0.03) .0 .01 =0.0394{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A gardener wanted to plant vegetables in his garden. Hence he bought 10 seeds of brinjal plant, 12 seeds of cabbage plant and 8 seeds of radish plant. The shopkeeper assured him of germination probabilities of brinjal, cabbage and radish to be {tex}25 \%, 35 \%{/tex} and {tex}40 \%{/tex} respectively. But before he could plant the seeds, they got mixed up in the bag and he had to sow them randomly.

Based upon the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Calculate the probability of a randomly chosen seed to germinate. (2)
  2. What is the probability that it is a cabbage seed, given that the chosen seed germinates? (2)
question_text: A gardener wanted to plant vegetables in his garden. Hence he bought 10 seeds of brinjal plant, 12 seeds of cabbage plant and 8 seeds of radish plant. The shopkeeper assured him of germination probabilities of brinjal, cabbage and radish to be {tex}25 \%, 35 \%{/tex} and {tex}40 \%{/tex} respectively. But before he could plant the seeds, they got mixed up in the bag and he had to sow them randomly. Based upon the above information, answer the following questions: Calculate the probability of a randomly chosen seed to germinate. (2) What is the probability that it is a cabbage seed, given that the chosen seed germinates? (2)
explanation: Let A: Event that chosen seed germinates. B: Event that Brinjal seed is chosen. C: Event that Cabbage seed is chosen. R: Event that Radish seed is chosen. {tex}P(B)=\frac{10}{30} ; P(C)=\frac{12}{30} ; P(R)=\frac{8}{30} ;{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)=\frac{25}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)=\frac{35}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right)=\frac{40}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(A) =P(B) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}+P(R) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{30} \times \frac{25}{100}+\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}+\frac{8}{30} \times \frac{40}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{990}{3000} \text { or } \frac{33}{100}{/tex} (a) {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{A}\right)=\frac{P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)}{P(B) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)+P(R) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right)} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}}{\frac{990}{3000}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{42}{99} \text { or } \frac{14}{33}{/tex}
solution: Let A: Event that chosen seed germinates. B: Event that Brinjal seed is chosen. C: Event that Cabbage seed is chosen. R: Event that Radish seed is chosen. {tex}P(B)=\frac{10}{30} ; P(C)=\frac{12}{30} ; P(R)=\frac{8}{30} ;{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)=\frac{25}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)=\frac{35}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right)=\frac{40}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(A) =P(B) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}+P(R) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{30} \times \frac{25}{100}+\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}+\frac{8}{30} \times \frac{40}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{990}{3000} \text { or } \frac{33}{100}{/tex} (a) {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{A}\right)=\frac{P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)}{P(B) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)+P(R) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right)} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}}{\frac{990}{3000}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{42}{99} \text { or } \frac{14}{33}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A: Event that chosen seed germinates.
B: Event that Brinjal seed is chosen.
C: Event that Cabbage seed is chosen.
R: Event that Radish seed is chosen.
{tex}P(B)=\frac{10}{30} ; P(C)=\frac{12}{30} ; P(R)=\frac{8}{30} ;{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)=\frac{25}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)=\frac{35}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right)=\frac{40}{100}{/tex}

  1. {tex}P(A) =P(B) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}+P(R) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{10}{30} \times \frac{25}{100}+\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}+\frac{8}{30} \times \frac{40}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{990}{3000} \text { or } \frac{33}{100}{/tex}
  2. (a){tex}P\left(\frac{C}{A}\right)=\frac{P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)}{P(B) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)+P(R) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right)} {/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}}{\frac{990}{3000}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{42}{99} \text { or } \frac{14}{33}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Based upon the results of regular medical check-ups in a hospital, it was found that out of 1000 people, 700 were very healthy, 200 maintained average health and 100 had a poor health record.
Let {tex}{A}_{1}{/tex}: People with good health,
{tex}{A}_{2}{/tex}: People with average health,
and {tex}{A}_{3}{/tex}: People with poor health.
During a pandemic, the data expressed that the chances of people contracting the disease from category {tex}{A}_{1}, {~A}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}{A}_{3}{/tex} are {tex}25 \%, 35 \%{/tex} and {tex}50 \%{/tex}, respectively.
Based upon the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. A person was tested randomly. What is the probability that he/she has contracted the disease? (2)
  2. Given that the person has not contracted the disease, what is the probability that the person is from category {tex}{A}_{2}{/tex}? (2)
question_text: Based upon the results of regular medical check-ups in a hospital, it was found that out of 1000 people, 700 were very healthy, 200 maintained average health and 100 had a poor health record. Let {tex}{A}_{1}{/tex} : People with good health, {tex}{A}_{2}{/tex} : People with average health, and {tex}{A}_{3}{/tex} : People with poor health. During a pandemic, the data expressed that the chances of people contracting the disease from category {tex}{A}_{1}, {~A}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}{A}_{3}{/tex} are {tex}25 \%, 35 \%{/tex} and {tex}50 \%{/tex} , respectively. Based upon the above information, answer the following questions: A person was tested randomly. What is the probability that he/she has contracted the disease? (2) Given that the person has not contracted the disease, what is the probability that the person is from category {tex}{A}_{2}{/tex} ? (2)
explanation: Let A: Person contracted the disease {tex} {P}({~A}) ={P}\left({~A}_1\right) \cdot {P}\left({~A} \mid {A}_1\right){/tex} {tex}+{P}\left({~A}_2\right) \cdot {P}\left({~A} \mid {A}_2\right)+{P}\left({~A}_3\right) \cdot {P}\left({~A} \mid {A}_3\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{7}{10}\left(\frac{{2 5}}{{1 0 0}}\right)+\frac{{2}}{{1 0}}\left(\frac{{3 5}}{{1 0 0}}\right)+\frac{{1}}{{1 0}}\left(\frac{{5 0}}{{1 0 0}}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{{2 9 5}}{{1 0 0 0}}={0 . 2 9 5} \text { or }\left(\frac{{5 9}}{{2 0 0}}\right) {/tex} {tex} {P}\left({A}_2 \mid \overline{{A}}\right) =\frac{{P}\left({A}_2\right) \cdot {P}\left(\overline{{A}} / {A}_2\right)}{{P}\left({A}_1\right) \cdot {P}\left(\overline{{A}} / {A}_1\right)+{P}\left({A}_2\right) \cdot {P}\left(\overline{{A}} / {A}_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{{2}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{6 5}}{{1 0 0}}}{\frac{{7}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{7 5}}{{1 0 0}}+\frac{{2}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{6 5}}{{1 0 0}}+\frac{{1}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{5 0}}{{1 0 0}}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{{2} \times {1 3}}{{7} \times {1 5}+{2} \times {1 3}+{1} \times {1 0}}=\frac{{2 6}}{{1 4 1}} {/tex}
solution: Let A: Person contracted the disease {tex} {P}({~A}) ={P}\left({~A}_1\right) \cdot {P}\left({~A} \mid {A}_1\right){/tex} {tex}+{P}\left({~A}_2\right) \cdot {P}\left({~A} \mid {A}_2\right)+{P}\left({~A}_3\right) \cdot {P}\left({~A} \mid {A}_3\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{7}{10}\left(\frac{{2 5}}{{1 0 0}}\right)+\frac{{2}}{{1 0}}\left(\frac{{3 5}}{{1 0 0}}\right)+\frac{{1}}{{1 0}}\left(\frac{{5 0}}{{1 0 0}}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{{2 9 5}}{{1 0 0 0}}={0 . 2 9 5} \text { or }\left(\frac{{5 9}}{{2 0 0}}\right) {/tex} {tex} {P}\left({A}_2 \mid \overline{{A}}\right) =\frac{{P}\left({A}_2\right) \cdot {P}\left(\overline{{A}} / {A}_2\right)}{{P}\left({A}_1\right) \cdot {P}\left(\overline{{A}} / {A}_1\right)+{P}\left({A}_2\right) \cdot {P}\left(\overline{{A}} / {A}_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{{2}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{6 5}}{{1 0 0}}}{\frac{{7}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{7 5}}{{1 0 0}}+\frac{{2}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{6 5}}{{1 0 0}}+\frac{{1}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{5 0}}{{1 0 0}}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{{2} \times {1 3}}{{7} \times {1 5}+{2} \times {1 3}+{1} \times {1 0}}=\frac{{2 6}}{{1 4 1}} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let A: Person contracted the disease
    {tex} {P}({~A}) ={P}\left({~A}_1\right) \cdot {P}\left({~A} \mid {A}_1\right){/tex}{tex}+{P}\left({~A}_2\right) \cdot {P}\left({~A} \mid {A}_2\right)+{P}\left({~A}_3\right) \cdot {P}\left({~A} \mid {A}_3\right) {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{7}{10}\left(\frac{{2 5}}{{1 0 0}}\right)+\frac{{2}}{{1 0}}\left(\frac{{3 5}}{{1 0 0}}\right)+\frac{{1}}{{1 0}}\left(\frac{{5 0}}{{1 0 0}}\right) {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{{2 9 5}}{{1 0 0 0}}={0 . 2 9 5} \text { or }\left(\frac{{5 9}}{{2 0 0}}\right) {/tex}
  2. {tex} {P}\left({A}_2 \mid \overline{{A}}\right) =\frac{{P}\left({A}_2\right) \cdot {P}\left(\overline{{A}} / {A}_2\right)}{{P}\left({A}_1\right) \cdot {P}\left(\overline{{A}} / {A}_1\right)+{P}\left({A}_2\right) \cdot {P}\left(\overline{{A}} / {A}_2\right)} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{\frac{{2}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{6 5}}{{1 0 0}}}{\frac{{7}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{7 5}}{{1 0 0}}+\frac{{2}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{6 5}}{{1 0 0}}+\frac{{1}}{{1 0}} \times \frac{{5 0}}{{1 0 0}}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{{2} \times {1 3}}{{7} \times {1 5}+{2} \times {1 3}+{1} \times {1 0}}=\frac{{2 6}}{{1 4 1}} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: E and F are two independent events such that P({tex}\bar{E}{/tex}) = 0.6 and P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F) = 0.6. Find P(F) and P({tex}\bar{E}{/tex} {tex}\cup{/tex} {tex}\bar{F}{/tex}).
question_text: E and F are two independent events such that P( {tex}\bar{E}{/tex} ) = 0.6 and P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F) = 0.6. Find P(F) and P( {tex}\bar{E}{/tex} {tex}\cup{/tex} {tex}\bar{F}{/tex} ).
explanation: {tex} P(\bar{E})=0.6 \Rightarrow P(E)=0.4 {/tex} {tex} P(E \cup F)=P(E)+P(F)-P(E \cap F) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 0.6=0.4+P(F)-0.4 P(F) \Rightarrow P(F)=\frac{1}{3} {/tex} {tex} P(\bar{E} \cup \bar{F})=1-P(E \cap F) {/tex} {tex}=1-0.4 \times \frac{1}{3}=\frac{13}{15}{/tex}
solution: {tex} P(\bar{E})=0.6 \Rightarrow P(E)=0.4 {/tex} {tex} P(E \cup F)=P(E)+P(F)-P(E \cap F) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 0.6=0.4+P(F)-0.4 P(F) \Rightarrow P(F)=\frac{1}{3} {/tex} {tex} P(\bar{E} \cup \bar{F})=1-P(E \cap F) {/tex} {tex}=1-0.4 \times \frac{1}{3}=\frac{13}{15}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} P(\bar{E})=0.6 \Rightarrow P(E)=0.4 {/tex}
{tex} P(E \cup F)=P(E)+P(F)-P(E \cap F) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 0.6=0.4+P(F)-0.4 P(F) \Rightarrow P(F)=\frac{1}{3} {/tex}
{tex} P(\bar{E} \cup \bar{F})=1-P(E \cap F) {/tex}
{tex}=1-0.4 \times \frac{1}{3}=\frac{13}{15}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The chances of {tex}{P}, {Q}{/tex} and R getting selected as CEO of a company are in the ratio {tex}4: 1: 2{/tex} respectively. The probabilities for the company to increase its profits from the previous year under the new CEO, P, Q or R are {tex}0 \cdot 3,0 \cdot 8{/tex} and {tex}0 \cdot 5{/tex} respectively. If the company increased the profits from the previous year, find the probability that it is due to the appointment of {tex}R{/tex} as CEO.
question_text: The chances of {tex}{P}, {Q}{/tex} and R getting selected as CEO of a company are in the ratio {tex}4: 1: 2{/tex} respectively. The probabilities for the company to increase its profits from the previous year under the new CEO, P, Q or R are {tex}0 \cdot 3,0 \cdot 8{/tex} and {tex}0 \cdot 5{/tex} respectively. If the company increased the profits from the previous year, find the probability that it is due to the appointment of {tex}R{/tex} as CEO.
explanation: Let {tex}E_1: P{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex} , {tex}E_2: Q{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex} , {tex}E_3: R{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex} {tex}A{/tex} : company increase profits from previous year here, {tex}{P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{4}{7}, {P}\left(E_3\right)=\frac{1}{7}, {P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{2}{7}{/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_1\right) =0.3, {/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=0.8, {/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_3\right)=0.5{/tex} {tex}P\left(E_3 \mid A\right) {/tex} {tex}=\frac{P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)+P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{2}{7} \times 0.5}{\frac{4}{7} \times 0.3+\frac{1}{7} \times 0.8+\frac{2}{7} \times 0.5}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}E_1: P{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex} , {tex}E_2: Q{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex} , {tex}E_3: R{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex} {tex}A{/tex} : company increase profits from previous year here, {tex}{P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{4}{7}, {P}\left(E_3\right)=\frac{1}{7}, {P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{2}{7}{/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_1\right) =0.3, {/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=0.8, {/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_3\right)=0.5{/tex} {tex}P\left(E_3 \mid A\right) {/tex} {tex}=\frac{P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)+P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{2}{7} \times 0.5}{\frac{4}{7} \times 0.3+\frac{1}{7} \times 0.8+\frac{2}{7} \times 0.5}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}E_1: P{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex},
{tex}E_2: Q{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex},
{tex}E_3: R{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex}
{tex}A{/tex} : company increase profits from previous year here, {tex}{P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{4}{7}, {P}\left(E_3\right)=\frac{1}{7}, {P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{2}{7}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(A \mid E_1\right) =0.3, {/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=0.8, {/tex}{tex}P\left(A \mid E_3\right)=0.5{/tex}
{tex}P\left(E_3 \mid A\right) {/tex}{tex}=\frac{P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)+P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{2}{7} \times 0.5}{\frac{4}{7} \times 0.3+\frac{1}{7} \times 0.8+\frac{2}{7} \times 0.5}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
According to recent research, air turbulence has increased in various regions around the world due to climate change. Turbulence makes flights bumpy and often delays the flights.
Assume that, an airplane observes severe turbulence, moderate turbulence or light turbulence with equal probabilities. Further, the chance of an airplane reaching late to the destination are 55%, 37% and 17% due to severe, moderate and light turbulence respectively.

On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find the probability that an airplane reached its destination late. (2)
  2. If the airplane reached its destination late, find the probability that it was due to moderate turbulence. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: According to recent research, air turbulence has increased in various regions around the world due to climate change. Turbulence makes flights bumpy and often delays the flights. Assume that, an airplane observes severe turbulence, moderate turbulence or light turbulence with equal probabilities. Further, the chance of an airplane reaching late to the destination are 55%, 37% and 17% due to severe, moderate and light turbulence respectively. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions: Find the probability that an airplane reached its destination late. (2) If the airplane reached its destination late, find the probability that it was due to moderate turbulence. (2)
explanation: Let A denote the event of airplane reaching its destination late E 1 = severe turbulence E 2 = moderate turbulence E 3 = light turbulence {tex} {P}({A}) ={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)+P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{55}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{17}{100} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{109}{100}\right)=\frac{109}{300} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}}{\frac{109}{300}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{37}{109} {/tex}
solution: Let A denote the event of airplane reaching its destination late E 1 = severe turbulence E 2 = moderate turbulence E 3 = light turbulence {tex} {P}({A}) ={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)+P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{55}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{17}{100} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{109}{100}\right)=\frac{109}{300} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}}{\frac{109}{300}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{37}{109} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let A denote the event of airplane reaching its destination late
    E1 = severe turbulence
    E2 = moderate turbulence
    E3 = light turbulence
    {tex} {P}({A}) ={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)+P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right) {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{55}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{17}{100} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{109}{100}\right)=\frac{109}{300} {/tex}
  2. {tex} {P}\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}}{\frac{109}{300}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{37}{109} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Airplanes are by far the safest mode of transportation when the number of transported passengers are measured against personal injuries and fatality totals.

Previous records state that the probability of an airplane crash is {tex}0.00001 \%{/tex}. Further, there are {tex}95 \%{/tex} chances that there will be survivors after a plane crash. Assume that in case of no crash, all travellers survive.
Let {tex}{E}_1{/tex} be the event that there is a plane crash and {tex}{E}_2{/tex} be the event that there is no crash. Let A be the event that passengers survive after the journey.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find the probability that the airplane will not crash. (1)
  2. {tex} \text { Find } {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_1\right)+{P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_2\right) \text {. }{/tex} (1)
    1. Find P(A). (2)
      OR
    2. Find {tex}P\left(E_2 \mid A\right) \text {. }{/tex} (2)
question_text: Airplanes are by far the safest mode of transportation when the number of transported passengers are measured against personal injuries and fatality totals. Previous records state that the probability of an airplane crash is {tex}0.00001 \%{/tex} . Further, there are {tex}95 \%{/tex} chances that there will be survivors after a plane crash. Assume that in case of no crash, all travellers survive. Let {tex}{E}_1{/tex} be the event that there is a plane crash and {tex}{E}_2{/tex} be the event that there is no crash. Let A be the event that passengers survive after the journey. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions: Find the probability that the airplane will not crash. (1) {tex} \text { Find } {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_1\right)+{P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_2\right) \text {. }{/tex} (1) Find P(A). (2) OR Find {tex}P\left(E_2 \mid A\right) \text {. }{/tex} (2)
explanation: {tex} \text { } P\left(E_2\right)=1-0.0000001 {/tex} {tex} =0.9999999 {/tex} {tex} P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(A / E_2\right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{95}{100}+1=\frac{195}{100}{/tex} {tex} P(A)=P\left(E_1\right) \times P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \times P\left(A / E_2\right){/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{10000000} \times \frac{95}{100}+\frac{9999999}{10000000} \times 1 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{95+999999900}{1000000000}=\frac{999999995}{1000000000} {/tex} {tex} P\left(E_2 / A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_2\right) \times P\left(A / E_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \times P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \times P\left(A / E_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{9999999}{10000000}}{\frac{999999995}{1000000000}}=\frac{999999900}{999999995} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \text { } P\left(E_2\right)=1-0.0000001 {/tex} {tex} =0.9999999 {/tex} {tex} P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(A / E_2\right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{95}{100}+1=\frac{195}{100}{/tex} {tex} P(A)=P\left(E_1\right) \times P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \times P\left(A / E_2\right){/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{10000000} \times \frac{95}{100}+\frac{9999999}{10000000} \times 1 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{95+999999900}{1000000000}=\frac{999999995}{1000000000} {/tex} {tex} P\left(E_2 / A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_2\right) \times P\left(A / E_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \times P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \times P\left(A / E_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{9999999}{10000000}}{\frac{999999995}{1000000000}}=\frac{999999900}{999999995} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} \text { } P\left(E_2\right)=1-0.0000001 {/tex}
    {tex} =0.9999999 {/tex}
  2. {tex} P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(A / E_2\right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{95}{100}+1=\frac{195}{100}{/tex}
    1. {tex} P(A)=P\left(E_1\right) \times P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \times P\left(A / E_2\right){/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{1}{10000000} \times \frac{95}{100}+\frac{9999999}{10000000} \times 1 {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{95+999999900}{1000000000}=\frac{999999995}{1000000000} {/tex}
    2. {tex} P\left(E_2 / A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_2\right) \times P\left(A / E_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \times P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \times P\left(A / E_2\right)} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{\frac{9999999}{10000000}}{\frac{999999995}{1000000000}}=\frac{999999900}{999999995} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: It is known that {tex}20 \%{/tex} of the students in a school have above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance and {tex}80 \%{/tex} of the students are irregular. Past year results show that {tex}80 \%{/tex} of students who have above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance and {tex}20 \%{/tex} of irregular students get 'A' grade in their annual examination. At the end of a year, a student is chosen at random from the school and is found to have an 'A' grade. What is the probability that the student is irregular?
question_text: It is known that {tex}20 \%{/tex} of the students in a school have above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance and {tex}80 \%{/tex} of the students are irregular. Past year results show that {tex}80 \%{/tex} of students who have above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance and {tex}20 \%{/tex} of irregular students get 'A' grade in their annual examination. At the end of a year, a student is chosen at random from the school and is found to have an 'A' grade. What is the probability that the student is irregular?
explanation: Let B : Student getting 'A' grade {tex}{E}_1{/tex} : Student having above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance {tex}{E}_2{/tex} : Student being irregular {tex} P\left(E_1\right)= \frac{20}{100} ; {/tex} {tex}P\left(E_2\right)=\frac{80}{100} P\left(B \mid E_1\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{80}{100} ; P\left(B \mid E_2\right)=\frac{20}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(E_2 \mid A\right) =\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(B \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{0.8 \times 0.2}{0.2 \times 0.8+0.8 \times 0.2}=\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
solution: Let B : Student getting 'A' grade {tex}{E}_1{/tex} : Student having above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance {tex}{E}_2{/tex} : Student being irregular {tex} P\left(E_1\right)= \frac{20}{100} ; {/tex} {tex}P\left(E_2\right)=\frac{80}{100} P\left(B \mid E_1\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{80}{100} ; P\left(B \mid E_2\right)=\frac{20}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(E_2 \mid A\right) =\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(B \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{0.8 \times 0.2}{0.2 \times 0.8+0.8 \times 0.2}=\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
solution_html:
Let B : Student getting 'A' grade
{tex}{E}_1{/tex} : Student having above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance
{tex}{E}_2{/tex} : Student being irregular
{tex} P\left(E_1\right)= \frac{20}{100} ; {/tex}{tex}P\left(E_2\right)=\frac{80}{100} P\left(B \mid E_1\right){/tex}{tex}=\frac{80}{100} ; P\left(B \mid E_2\right)=\frac{20}{100} {/tex}
{tex} P\left(E_2 \mid A\right) =\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(B \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{0.8 \times 0.2}{0.2 \times 0.8+0.8 \times 0.2}=\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
