subject: Mathematics
question_html: A card from a well shuffled deck of 52 playing cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, a card is drawn at random and is found to be a King. Find the probability of the lost card being a King.
question_text: A card from a well shuffled deck of 52 playing cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, a card is drawn at random and is found to be a King. Find the probability of the lost card being a King.
explanation: Let {tex}{E}_{1}{/tex} be the event of lost card is King, {tex}{E}_{2}{/tex} be the event of lost card not a King and A be the event of drawing a King from remaining 51 cards. so, {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)=\frac{1}{13}, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{12}{13}, {/tex} {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)=\frac{3}{51}, {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{51}{/tex} Now, Required probability is {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1} \mid {A}\right){/tex} , {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1} \mid {A}\right)=\frac{{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)}=\frac{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{3}{51}}{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{3}{51}+\frac{12}{13} \times \frac{4}{51}}=\frac{1}{17}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}{E}_{1}{/tex} be the event of lost card is King, {tex}{E}_{2}{/tex} be the event of lost card not a King and A be the event of drawing a King from remaining 51 cards. so, {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)=\frac{1}{13}, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{12}{13}, {/tex} {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)=\frac{3}{51}, {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{51}{/tex} Now, Required probability is {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1} \mid {A}\right){/tex} , {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1} \mid {A}\right)=\frac{{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)}=\frac{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{3}{51}}{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{3}{51}+\frac{12}{13} \times \frac{4}{51}}=\frac{1}{17}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}{E}_{1}{/tex} be the event of lost card is King,
{tex}{E}_{2}{/tex} be the event of lost card not a King and
A be the event of drawing a King from remaining 51 cards.
so, {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)=\frac{1}{13}, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{12}{13}, {/tex} {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)=\frac{3}{51}, {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{51}{/tex}
Now, Required probability is {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1} \mid {A}\right){/tex},
{tex}{P}\left({E}_{1} \mid {A}\right)=\frac{{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)}=\frac{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{3}{51}}{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{3}{51}+\frac{12}{13} \times \frac{4}{51}}=\frac{1}{17}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A departmental store sends bills to charge its customers once a month. Past experience shows that {tex}70 \%{/tex} of its customers pay their first month bill in time. The store also found that the customer who pays the bill in time has the probability of 0.8 of paying in time next month and the customer who doesn't pay in time has the probability of 0.4 of paying in time the next month.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Let {tex}{E}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{E}_{2}{/tex} respectively denote the event of customer paying or not paying the first month bill in time.
    Find {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right), {P}\left({E}_{2}\right){/tex}. (1)
  2. Let A denotes the event of customer paying second month's bill in time, then find {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right){/tex} and {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right){/tex}. (1)
    1. Find the probability of customer paying second month's bill in time. (2)

      OR

    2. Find the probability of customer paying first month's bill in time if it is found that customer has paid the second month's bill in time. (2)
question_text: A departmental store sends bills to charge its customers once a month. Past experience shows that {tex}70 \%{/tex} of its customers pay their first month bill in time. The store also found that the customer who pays the bill in time has the probability of 0.8 of paying in time next month and the customer who doesn't pay in time has the probability of 0.4 of paying in time the next month. Based on the above information, answer the following questions: Let {tex}{E}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{E}_{2}{/tex} respectively denote the event of customer paying or not paying the first month bill in time. Find {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right), {P}\left({E}_{2}\right){/tex} . (1) Let A denotes the event of customer paying second month's bill in time, then find {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right){/tex} and {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right){/tex} . (1) Find the probability of customer paying second month's bill in time. (2) OR Find the probability of customer paying first month's bill in time if it is found that customer has paid the second month's bill in time. (2)
explanation: {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)=\frac{7}{10}=0.7, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{3}{10}=0.3{/tex} {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)=0.8, {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=0.4{/tex} {tex}{P}({A})={P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right){/tex} = 0.7 {tex}\times 0.8+0.3 \times 0.4=0.68{/tex} or {tex}\frac{17}{25}{/tex} OR {tex}{P}({A})=\frac{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)}=\frac{14}{17}{/tex}
solution: {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)=\frac{7}{10}=0.7, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{3}{10}=0.3{/tex} {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)=0.8, {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=0.4{/tex} {tex}{P}({A})={P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right){/tex} = 0.7 {tex}\times 0.8+0.3 \times 0.4=0.68{/tex} or {tex}\frac{17}{25}{/tex} OR {tex}{P}({A})=\frac{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)}=\frac{14}{17}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)=\frac{7}{10}=0.7, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{3}{10}=0.3{/tex}
  2. {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)=0.8, {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=0.4{/tex}
    1. {tex}{P}({A})={P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right){/tex} = 0.7 {tex}\times 0.8+0.3 \times 0.4=0.68{/tex} or {tex}\frac{17}{25}{/tex}
      OR
    2. {tex}{P}({A})=\frac{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)}=\frac{14}{17}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Rohit, Jaspreet and Alia appeared for an interview for three vacancies in the same post. The probability of Rohit's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}, Jaspreet's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and Alia's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}. The event of selection is independent of each other.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. What is the probability that at least one of them is selected? (1)
  2. Find {tex}{P}({G} \mid \overline{{H}}){/tex} where G is the event of Jaspreet's selection and {tex}\overline{{H}}{/tex} denotes the event that Rohit is not selected. (1)
    1. Find the probability that exactly one of them is selected. (2)

      OR

    2. Find the probability that exactly two of them are selected. (2)
question_text: Rohit, Jaspreet and Alia appeared for an interview for three vacancies in the same post. The probability of Rohit's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} , Jaspreet's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and Alia's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} . The event of selection is independent of each other. Based on the above information, answer the following questions: What is the probability that at least one of them is selected? (1) Find {tex}{P}({G} \mid \overline{{H}}){/tex} where G is the event of Jaspreet's selection and {tex}\overline{{H}}{/tex} denotes the event that Rohit is not selected. (1) Find the probability that exactly one of them is selected. (2) OR Find the probability that exactly two of them are selected. (2)
explanation: Given {tex}{P}({/tex} Rohit {tex})=\frac{1}{5}, {P}({/tex} Jaspreet {tex})=\frac{1}{3}, {P}({/tex} Alia {tex})=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} P (atleast one of them is selected) {tex}=1-{P}{/tex} (no one is selected) {tex}=1-\left(\frac{4}{5} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}\right)=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}{P}({G} \mid \overline{{H}})=\frac{P(G \cap \bar{H})}{P(\bar{H})}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} P (exactly one of them selected) {tex}={P}({R}) \times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) {/tex} {tex}\times {P}({~J}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) \times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}({~A}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{6+12+8}{60}=\frac{13}{30}{/tex} OR P (exactly two of them selected) {tex}={P}({R}) \times {P}({~J}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}({R}) {/tex} {tex}\times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}({~A})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) \times {P}({~J}) \times {P}({~A}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3+2+4}{60}=\frac{3}{20}{/tex}
solution: Given {tex}{P}({/tex} Rohit {tex})=\frac{1}{5}, {P}({/tex} Jaspreet {tex})=\frac{1}{3}, {P}({/tex} Alia {tex})=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} P (atleast one of them is selected) {tex}=1-{P}{/tex} (no one is selected) {tex}=1-\left(\frac{4}{5} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}\right)=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}{P}({G} \mid \overline{{H}})=\frac{P(G \cap \bar{H})}{P(\bar{H})}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} P (exactly one of them selected) {tex}={P}({R}) \times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) {/tex} {tex}\times {P}({~J}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) \times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}({~A}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{6+12+8}{60}=\frac{13}{30}{/tex} OR P (exactly two of them selected) {tex}={P}({R}) \times {P}({~J}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}({R}) {/tex} {tex}\times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}({~A})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) \times {P}({~J}) \times {P}({~A}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3+2+4}{60}=\frac{3}{20}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given {tex}{P}({/tex}Rohit{tex})=\frac{1}{5}, {P}({/tex}Jaspreet{tex})=\frac{1}{3}, {P}({/tex}Alia{tex})=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

  1. P (atleast one of them is selected) {tex}=1-{P}{/tex} (no one is selected) {tex}=1-\left(\frac{4}{5} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}\right)=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{P}({G} \mid \overline{{H}})=\frac{P(G \cap \bar{H})}{P(\bar{H})}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
    1. P (exactly one of them selected)
      {tex}={P}({R}) \times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) {/tex} {tex}\times {P}({~J}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) \times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}({~A}){/tex}{tex}=\frac{6+12+8}{60}=\frac{13}{30}{/tex}

      OR

    2. P (exactly two of them selected)
      {tex}={P}({R}) \times {P}({~J}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}({R}) {/tex} {tex}\times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}({~A})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) \times {P}({~J}) \times {P}({~A}){/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{3+2+4}{60}=\frac{3}{20}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: It is known that {tex}20 \%{/tex} of the students in a school have above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance and {tex}80 \%{/tex} of the students are irregular. Past year results show that {tex}80 \%{/tex} of students who have above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance and {tex}20 \%{/tex} of irregular students get '{tex}A{/tex}' grade in their annual examination. At the end of a year, a student is chosen at random from the school and is found to have an 'A' grade. What is the probability that the student is irregular?
question_text: It is known that {tex}20 \%{/tex} of the students in a school have above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance and {tex}80 \%{/tex} of the students are irregular. Past year results show that {tex}80 \%{/tex} of students who have above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance and {tex}20 \%{/tex} of irregular students get ' {tex}A{/tex} ' grade in their annual examination. At the end of a year, a student is chosen at random from the school and is found to have an 'A' grade. What is the probability that the student is irregular?
explanation: Let B : Student getting 'A' grade {tex}{E}_1{/tex} : Student having above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance {tex}{E}_2{/tex} : Student being irregular {tex} P\left(E_1\right)= \frac{20}{100} ; {/tex} {tex}P\left(E_2\right)=\frac{80}{100} P\left(B \mid E_1\right)=\frac{80}{100} ; {/tex} {tex}P\left(B \mid E_2\right)=\frac{20}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(E_2 \mid B\right) =\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(B \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{0.8 \times 0.2}{0.2 \times 0.8+0.8 \times 0.2}=\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
solution: Let B : Student getting 'A' grade {tex}{E}_1{/tex} : Student having above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance {tex}{E}_2{/tex} : Student being irregular {tex} P\left(E_1\right)= \frac{20}{100} ; {/tex} {tex}P\left(E_2\right)=\frac{80}{100} P\left(B \mid E_1\right)=\frac{80}{100} ; {/tex} {tex}P\left(B \mid E_2\right)=\frac{20}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(E_2 \mid B\right) =\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(B \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{0.8 \times 0.2}{0.2 \times 0.8+0.8 \times 0.2}=\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
solution_html:
Let B : Student getting 'A' grade
{tex}{E}_1{/tex}: Student having above {tex}90 \%{/tex} attendance
{tex}{E}_2{/tex}: Student being irregular
{tex} P\left(E_1\right)= \frac{20}{100} ; {/tex}{tex}P\left(E_2\right)=\frac{80}{100} P\left(B \mid E_1\right)=\frac{80}{100} ; {/tex}{tex}P\left(B \mid E_2\right)=\frac{20}{100} {/tex}
{tex} P\left(E_2 \mid B\right) =\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(B \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(B \mid E_2\right)} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{0.8 \times 0.2}{0.2 \times 0.8+0.8 \times 0.2}=\frac{1}{2} {/tex}

question_text: Assertion (A): If R and S are two events such that {tex}{P}({R} \mid {S})=1{/tex} and {tex}{P}({S}) \gt 0{/tex} , then {tex}{S} \subset {R}{/tex} . Reason {tex}(R):{/tex} If two events A and B are such that {tex}{P}({A} \cap {B})={P}({B}){/tex} , then {tex}{A} \subset {B}{/tex} .

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Bag I contains 3 red and 4 black balls, Bag II contains 5 red and 2 black balls. Two balls are transferred at random from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn at random from Bag II. Find the probability that the drawn ball is red in colour.
question_text: Bag I contains 3 red and 4 black balls, Bag II contains 5 red and 2 black balls. Two balls are transferred at random from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn at random from Bag II. Find the probability that the drawn ball is red in colour.
explanation: E 1 : Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Red. E 2 : Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Black. E 3 : Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Red and Black. A: Ball drawn from Bag II is Red {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{7}, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{2}{7}, {P}\left({E}_{3}\right)=\frac{4}{7}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{7}{9}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=\frac{5}{9}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{3}\right)=\frac{6}{9}{/tex} {tex}{P}({~A})=\frac{1}{7} \cdot \frac{7}{9}+\frac{2}{7} \cdot \frac{5}{9}+\frac{4}{7} \cdot \frac{6}{9}=\frac{41}{63}{/tex}
solution: E 1 : Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Red. E 2 : Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Black. E 3 : Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Red and Black. A: Ball drawn from Bag II is Red {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{7}, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{2}{7}, {P}\left({E}_{3}\right)=\frac{4}{7}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{7}{9}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=\frac{5}{9}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{3}\right)=\frac{6}{9}{/tex} {tex}{P}({~A})=\frac{1}{7} \cdot \frac{7}{9}+\frac{2}{7} \cdot \frac{5}{9}+\frac{4}{7} \cdot \frac{6}{9}=\frac{41}{63}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1: Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Red.
E2: Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Black.
E3: Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Red and Black.
A: Ball drawn from Bag II is Red
{tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{7}, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{2}{7}, {P}\left({E}_{3}\right)=\frac{4}{7}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{7}{9}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=\frac{5}{9}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{3}\right)=\frac{6}{9}{/tex}
{tex}{P}({~A})=\frac{1}{7} \cdot \frac{7}{9}+\frac{2}{7} \cdot \frac{5}{9}+\frac{4}{7} \cdot \frac{6}{9}=\frac{41}{63}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Out of two bags, bag A contains 2 white and 3 red balls and bag B contains 4 white and 5 red balls. One ball is drawn at random from one of the bags and is found to be red. Find the probability that it was drawn from bag B.
question_text: Out of two bags, bag A contains 2 white and 3 red balls and bag B contains 4 white and 5 red balls. One ball is drawn at random from one of the bags and is found to be red. Find the probability that it was drawn from bag B.
explanation: Let A, E 1 and E 2 denote the events that the ball is red, chosen from bag A and chosen from bag B, respectively. {tex}\therefore {/tex} P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}P(\frac {A}{E_{1}})=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{5}{9}{/tex} Using Bayes' theorem, we get Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{2}}{ A})=\frac{P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(\frac {A }{E_{2}}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(\frac {A}{E_{1}}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(\frac {A }{E_{2}}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{9}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{9}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{25}{52}{/tex}
solution: Let A, E 1 and E 2 denote the events that the ball is red, chosen from bag A and chosen from bag B, respectively. {tex}\therefore {/tex} P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}P(\frac {A}{E_{1}})=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{5}{9}{/tex} Using Bayes' theorem, we get Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{2}}{ A})=\frac{P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(\frac {A }{E_{2}}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(\frac {A}{E_{1}}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(\frac {A }{E_{2}}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{9}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{9}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{25}{52}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A, E1 and E2 denote the events that the ball is red,  chosen from bag A and chosen from bag B, respectively.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} P(E1) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
P(E2) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}P(\frac {A}{E_{1}})=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
Using Bayes' theorem, we get
Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{2}}{ A})=\frac{P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(\frac {A }{E_{2}}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(\frac {A}{E_{1}}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(\frac {A }{E_{2}}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{9}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{9}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{25}{52}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: In answering a question on a multiple choice test, a student either knows the answer or guesses. Let {tex}\frac { 3 } { 5 }{/tex} be the probability that he knows the answer and {tex}\frac { 2 } { 5 }{/tex} be the probability that he guesses.  Assuming that a student who guesses at the answer will be correct with probability {tex}\frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}. What is the probability that the student knows the answer, given that he answered it correctly?
question_text: In answering a question on a multiple choice test, a student either knows the answer or guesses. Let {tex}\frac { 3 } { 5 }{/tex} be the probability that he knows the answer and {tex}\frac { 2 } { 5 }{/tex} be the probability that he guesses. Assuming that a student who guesses at the answer will be correct with probability {tex}\frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} . What is the probability that the student knows the answer, given that he answered it correctly?
explanation: Let E 1 : Event that the student knows the answer E 2 : Event that the student guesses the answer E: Event that the answer is correct Here, E 1 and E 2 are mutually exclusive and exhaustive events. {tex}\therefore \quad P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 3 } { 5 } \text { and } P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 2 } { 5 }{/tex} Now, {tex}P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 1 } } \right) = P{/tex} (the student answered correctly, given the knows the answer)=1 {tex}P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 2 } } \right) = P{/tex} (the student answered correctly, given he gusses) {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} The probability that the student knows the answer given that he answered it correctly is given by {tex}P \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E } \right){/tex} By using Baye's theorem, we get {tex}P \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E } \right) = \frac { P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 1 } } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 1 } } \right) P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 2 } } \right) P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 \times \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { 1 \times \frac { 3 } { 5 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 2 } { 5 } } = \frac { \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { \frac { 3 } { 5 } + \frac { 2 } { 15 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { \frac { 15 + 10 } { 15 } } = \frac { 3 \times 3 } { 25 } = \frac { 9 } { 25 }{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 : Event that the student knows the answer E 2 : Event that the student guesses the answer E: Event that the answer is correct Here, E 1 and E 2 are mutually exclusive and exhaustive events. {tex}\therefore \quad P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 3 } { 5 } \text { and } P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 2 } { 5 }{/tex} Now, {tex}P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 1 } } \right) = P{/tex} (the student answered correctly, given the knows the answer)=1 {tex}P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 2 } } \right) = P{/tex} (the student answered correctly, given he gusses) {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} The probability that the student knows the answer given that he answered it correctly is given by {tex}P \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E } \right){/tex} By using Baye's theorem, we get {tex}P \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E } \right) = \frac { P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 1 } } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 1 } } \right) P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 2 } } \right) P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 \times \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { 1 \times \frac { 3 } { 5 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 2 } { 5 } } = \frac { \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { \frac { 3 } { 5 } + \frac { 2 } { 15 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { \frac { 15 + 10 } { 15 } } = \frac { 3 \times 3 } { 25 } = \frac { 9 } { 25 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1: Event that the student knows the answer
E2: Event that the student guesses the answer
E: Event that the answer is correct
Here, E1 and E2 are mutually exclusive and exhaustive events.
{tex}\therefore \quad P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 3 } { 5 } \text { and } P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 2 } { 5 }{/tex}
Now,{tex}P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 1 } } \right) = P{/tex}(the student answered correctly, given the knows the answer)=1
{tex}P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 2 } } \right) = P{/tex}(the student answered correctly, given he gusses){tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
The probability that the student knows the answer given that he answered it correctly is
given by {tex}P \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E } \right){/tex}
By using Baye's theorem, we get
{tex}P \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E } \right) = \frac { P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 1 } } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 1 } } \right) P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( \frac { E } { E _ { 2 } } \right) P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 \times \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { 1 \times \frac { 3 } { 5 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 2 } { 5 } } = \frac { \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { \frac { 3 } { 5 } + \frac { 2 } { 15 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { \frac { 15 + 10 } { 15 } } = \frac { 3 \times 3 } { 25 } = \frac { 9 } { 25 }{/tex}

question_html: A and B throw a die alternatively till one of them gets a 6 and wins the game. Find their respective probabilities of winning, if A starts first.
question_text: A and B throw a die alternatively till one of them gets a 6 and wins the game. Find their respective probabilities of winning, if A starts first.
explanation: S denote the success(getting a 6) and F denotes the failure(not getting a 6) {tex}P(S) = \frac{1}{6},P(F) = \frac{5}{6}{/tex} P(A win in the first throw) = (S) {tex} = \frac{1}{6}{/tex} P(A win in the 3rd throw) {tex} = {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^2} \times \frac{1}{6}{/tex} P(A win in the fifth throw) {tex} = {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^4} \times \frac{1}{6}{/tex} P (A win) {tex} = \frac{1}{6} + {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^2} \times \frac{1}{6} + {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^4} \times \frac{1}{6} + ........{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{6}}}{{1 - \frac{{25}}{{36}}}}{/tex} {tex}\left[ {u\sin g\;s = \frac{a}{{1 - r}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{6}{{11}}{/tex}
solution: S denote the success(getting a 6) and F denotes the failure(not getting a 6) {tex}P(S) = \frac{1}{6},P(F) = \frac{5}{6}{/tex} P(A win in the first throw) = (S) {tex} = \frac{1}{6}{/tex} P(A win in the 3rd throw) {tex} = {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^2} \times \frac{1}{6}{/tex} P(A win in the fifth throw) {tex} = {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^4} \times \frac{1}{6}{/tex} P (A win) {tex} = \frac{1}{6} + {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^2} \times \frac{1}{6} + {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^4} \times \frac{1}{6} + ........{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{6}}}{{1 - \frac{{25}}{{36}}}}{/tex} {tex}\left[ {u\sin g\;s = \frac{a}{{1 - r}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{6}{{11}}{/tex}
solution_html:
S denote the success(getting a 6) and F denotes the failure(not getting a 6)
{tex}P(S) = \frac{1}{6},P(F) = \frac{5}{6}{/tex}
P(A win in the first throw) = (S) {tex} = \frac{1}{6}{/tex}
P(A win in the 3rd throw) {tex} = {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^2} \times \frac{1}{6}{/tex}
P(A win in the fifth throw) {tex} = {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^4} \times \frac{1}{6}{/tex}
P (A win) {tex} = \frac{1}{6} + {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^2} \times \frac{1}{6} + {\left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)^4} \times \frac{1}{6} + ........{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{6}}}{{1 - \frac{{25}}{{36}}}}{/tex} {tex}\left[ {u\sin g\;s = \frac{a}{{1 - r}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{6}{{11}}{/tex}

question_html: A fair coin and an unbiased die are tossed. Let A be the event, Head appears on the coin and B be the event, 3 comes on the die. Find whether A and B are independent events or not.
question_text: A fair coin and an unbiased die are tossed. Let A be the event, Head appears on the coin and B be the event, 3 comes on the die . Find whether A and B are independent events or not.
explanation: Let A be the sample space of given experiment. S = { (H, 1), (H, 2) , (H, 3), (H, 4) , (H, 5) , (H, 6) , (T, 1), (T , 2), (T, 3), (T, 4), (T, 5), (T, 6) } A : Head appear on the coin B : 3 appear on the dice A = {(H, 1), (H, 2), (H, 3), (H, 4), (H, 5), (H, 6)} B = {(H, 3), (T, 3)} {tex}A \cap B = \left\{ {(H,3)} \right\}{/tex} {tex}P(A) = \frac{6}{{12}} = \frac{1}{2},p(B) = \frac{2}{{12}} = \frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}P(A \cap B) = \frac{1}{{12}}{/tex} {tex}P(A) \times P(B) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{12}}{/tex} {tex} = P\left( {A \cap B} \right){/tex} Hence A and B are independent events.
solution: Let A be the sample space of given experiment. S = { (H, 1), (H, 2) , (H, 3), (H, 4) , (H, 5) , (H, 6) , (T, 1), (T , 2), (T, 3), (T, 4), (T, 5), (T, 6) } A : Head appear on the coin B : 3 appear on the dice A = {(H, 1), (H, 2), (H, 3), (H, 4), (H, 5), (H, 6)} B = {(H, 3), (T, 3)} {tex}A \cap B = \left\{ {(H,3)} \right\}{/tex} {tex}P(A) = \frac{6}{{12}} = \frac{1}{2},p(B) = \frac{2}{{12}} = \frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}P(A \cap B) = \frac{1}{{12}}{/tex} {tex}P(A) \times P(B) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{12}}{/tex} {tex} = P\left( {A \cap B} \right){/tex} Hence A and B are independent events.
solution_html:
Let A be the sample space of given experiment.
S = { (H, 1), (H, 2) , (H, 3), (H, 4) , (H, 5) , (H, 6) , (T, 1), (T , 2), (T, 3), (T, 4), (T, 5), (T, 6) }
A : Head appear on the coin
B : 3 appear on the dice
A = {(H, 1), (H, 2), (H, 3), (H, 4), (H, 5), (H, 6)}
B = {(H, 3), (T, 3)}
{tex}A \cap B = \left\{ {(H,3)} \right\}{/tex}
{tex}P(A) = \frac{6}{{12}} = \frac{1}{2},p(B) = \frac{2}{{12}} = \frac{1}{6}{/tex}
{tex}P(A \cap B) = \frac{1}{{12}}{/tex}
{tex}P(A) \times P(B) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{6}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{{12}}{/tex}
{tex} = P\left( {A \cap B} \right){/tex}
Hence A and B are independent events.

question_html: Two events E and F are independent. If P(E) = 0.3, P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F)=0.5 then P(E|F) - P(F|E) equals
question_text: Two events E and F are independent. If P(E) = 0.3, P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F)=0.5 then P(E|F) - P(F|E) equals

options.explanation: Here P(E) = 0.3, P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F)=0.5 Let P(F) = x {tex}\therefore P(E \cup F)=P(E)+P(F)-P(E \cap F){/tex} {tex}=P(E)+P(F)-P(E) \cdot P(F){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 0.5 = 0.3 + x - 0.3x {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{0.5-0.3}{0.7}=\frac{2}{7}=P(F){/tex} {tex}\therefore P(E / F)-P(F / E)=\frac{P(E \cap F)}{P(F)}-\frac{P(F \cap E)}{P(E)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{P(E \cap F) \cdot P(E)-P(F \cap E) \cdot P(F)}{P(E) \cdot P(F)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{P(E \cap F)[P(E)-P(F)]}{P(E \cap F)}=P(E)-P(F){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{10}-\frac{2}{7}=\frac{21-20}{70}=\frac{1}{70}{/tex}

explanation: Here P(E) = 0.3, P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F)=0.5 Let P(F) = x {tex}\therefore P(E \cup F)=P(E)+P(F)-P(E \cap F){/tex} {tex}=P(E)+P(F)-P(E) \cdot P(F){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 0.5 = 0.3 + x - 0.3x {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{0.5-0.3}{0.7}=\frac{2}{7}=P(F){/tex} {tex}\therefore P(E / F)-P(F / E)=\frac{P(E \cap F)}{P(F)}-\frac{P(F \cap E)}{P(E)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{P(E \cap F) \cdot P(E)-P(F \cap E) \cdot P(F)}{P(E) \cdot P(F)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{P(E \cap F)[P(E)-P(F)]}{P(E \cap F)}=P(E)-P(F){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{10}-\frac{2}{7}=\frac{21-20}{70}=\frac{1}{70}{/tex}

question_text: There are different types of Yoga which involve the usage of different poses of Yoga Asanas, Meditation and Pranayam as shown in the figure below: The Venn diagram below represents the probabilities of three different types of Yoga, A, B and C performed by the people of a society. explanation: {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text C}{\text B}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P(\text C \cap \text B)}{\text P(\text B)}{/tex} P(B) = 0.09 + 0.04 + 0.23 = 0.36 {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text C}{\text B}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.23}{0.36}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{23}{36}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text C}{\text B}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P(\text C \cap \text B)}{\text P(\text B)}{/tex} P(B) = 0.09 + 0.04 + 0.23 = 0.36 {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text C}{\text B}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.23}{0.36}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{23}{36}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text C}{\text B}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P(\text C \cap \text B)}{\text P(\text B)}{/tex}
P(B) = 0.09 + 0.04 + 0.23 = 0.36
{tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text C}{\text B}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.23}{0.36}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{23}{36}{/tex}

options.explanation: {tex} P(\bar{A})=\frac{1}{2}, P(\bar{B})=\frac{2}{3} \text { and } P(A \cap B)=\frac{1}{4} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow P(A)=\frac{1}{2} ; P(B)=\frac{1}{3} {/tex} We have, {tex}P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B){/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{7}{12} {/tex} {tex} P\left(\frac{\bar{A}}{\bar{B}}\right)=\frac{P(\bar{A} \cap \bar{B})}{P(\bar{B})} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{P(\overline{A \cup B})}{P(\bar{B})} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1-P(A \cup B)}{P(\bar{B})} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1-\frac{7}{12}}{\frac{2}{3}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{5}{8} {/tex}

explanation: {tex} P(\bar{A})=\frac{1}{2}, P(\bar{B})=\frac{2}{3} \text { and } P(A \cap B)=\frac{1}{4} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow P(A)=\frac{1}{2} ; P(B)=\frac{1}{3} {/tex} We have, {tex}P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B){/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{7}{12} {/tex} {tex} P\left(\frac{\bar{A}}{\bar{B}}\right)=\frac{P(\bar{A} \cap \bar{B})}{P(\bar{B})} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{P(\overline{A \cup B})}{P(\bar{B})} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1-P(A \cup B)}{P(\bar{B})} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1-\frac{7}{12}}{\frac{2}{3}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{5}{8} {/tex}

question_html: A and B are independent events such that P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} {tex}\bar {\text B}{/tex}) = {tex}\frac 14{/tex} and P({tex}\bar {\text A}{/tex} {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = {tex}\frac 16{/tex}. Find P(A) and P(B).
question_text: A and B are independent events such that P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} {tex}\bar {\text B}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac 14{/tex} and P( {tex}\bar {\text A}{/tex} {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = {tex}\frac 16{/tex} . Find P(A) and P(B).
explanation: P(A) {tex}\text P(\bar {\text B}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}){/tex} P(B) = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} Let P(A) = x P(B) = y x(1 - y) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} , (1 - x) y = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y = {tex}\frac{1}{12}{/tex} eliminating y, we get 12x 2 - 13x + 3 = 0 gives x = {tex}\frac{1}{3}, \frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\left.\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})=\frac{1}{4} \\ \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})=\frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})=\frac{2}{3} \end{array}\right\}{/tex} /////////////////////
solution: P(A) {tex}\text P(\bar {\text B}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}){/tex} P(B) = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} Let P(A) = x P(B) = y x(1 - y) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} , (1 - x) y = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y = {tex}\frac{1}{12}{/tex} eliminating y, we get 12x 2 - 13x + 3 = 0 gives x = {tex}\frac{1}{3}, \frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\left.\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})=\frac{1}{4} \\ \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})=\frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})=\frac{2}{3} \end{array}\right\}{/tex} /////////////////////
solution_html:
P(A) {tex}\text P(\bar {\text B}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}){/tex} P(B) = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
Let P(A) = x P(B) = y
x(1 - y) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}, (1 - x) y = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y = {tex}\frac{1}{12}{/tex}
eliminating y, we get 12x2 - 13x + 3 = 0
gives x = {tex}\frac{1}{3}, \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\left.\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})=\frac{1}{4} \\ \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})=\frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})=\frac{2}{3} \end{array}\right\}{/tex}
/////////////////////

subject: Mathematics
question_html: There are two coins. One of them is a biased coin such that P (head): P (tail) is 1 : 3 and the other coin is a fair coin. A coin is selected at random and tossed once. If the coin showed head, then find the probability that it is a biased coin.
question_text: There are two coins. One of them is a biased coin such that P (head): P (tail) is 1 : 3 and the other coin is a fair coin. A coin is selected at random and tossed once. If the coin showed head, then find the probability that it is a biased coin.
explanation: E 1 = Biased coin is selected {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} E 2 = Fair coin is selected {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} A = Head appeared on tossing a selected coin. {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} , {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} By Bayes' Theorem {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\text E_2\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_2}\right)} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4}}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} ///////////////////////////
solution: E 1 = Biased coin is selected {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} E 2 = Fair coin is selected {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} A = Head appeared on tossing a selected coin. {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} , {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} By Bayes' Theorem {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\text E_2\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_2}\right)} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4}}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} ///////////////////////////
solution_html:
E1 = Biased coin is selected {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E1) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
E2 = Fair coin is selected {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E2) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
A = Head appeared on tossing a selected coin.
{tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
By Bayes' Theorem {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\text E_2\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_2}\right)} {/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4}}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
///////////////////////////

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Two fair dice are thrown simultaneously. If X denotes the number of sixes, find the mean of X.
question_text: Two fair dice are thrown simultaneously. If X denotes the number of sixes, find the mean of X.
explanation: Here X can take values 0, 1 or 2 P(X = 0) = {tex}\frac{5}{6} \times \frac{5}{6}=\frac{25}{36}{/tex} P(X = 1) = {tex}2\left(\frac{5}{6} \times \frac{1}{6}\right)=\frac{10}{36}{/tex} P(X = 2) = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{36}{/tex} Mean of X = {tex}\left(0 \times \frac{25}{36}\right)+\left(1 \times \frac{10}{36}\right){/tex} + {tex}\left(2 \times \frac{1}{36}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{12}{36}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution: Here X can take values 0, 1 or 2 P(X = 0) = {tex}\frac{5}{6} \times \frac{5}{6}=\frac{25}{36}{/tex} P(X = 1) = {tex}2\left(\frac{5}{6} \times \frac{1}{6}\right)=\frac{10}{36}{/tex} P(X = 2) = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{36}{/tex} Mean of X = {tex}\left(0 \times \frac{25}{36}\right)+\left(1 \times \frac{10}{36}\right){/tex} + {tex}\left(2 \times \frac{1}{36}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{12}{36}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here X can take values 0, 1 or 2
P(X = 0) = {tex}\frac{5}{6} \times \frac{5}{6}=\frac{25}{36}{/tex}
P(X = 1) = {tex}2\left(\frac{5}{6} \times \frac{1}{6}\right)=\frac{10}{36}{/tex}
P(X = 2) = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{36}{/tex}
Mean of X = {tex}\left(0 \times \frac{25}{36}\right)+\left(1 \times \frac{10}{36}\right){/tex} + {tex}\left(2 \times \frac{1}{36}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{12}{36}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
A building contractor undertakes a job to construct 4 flats on a plot along with parking area. Due to strike the probability of many construction workers not being present for the job is 0.65. The probability that many are not present and still the work gets completed on time is 0.35. The probability that work will be completed on time when all workers are present is 0.80.
Let: E1: represent the event when many workers were not present for the job;
E2: represent the event when all workers were present; and
E: represent completing the construction work on time.

  1. What is the probability that all the workers are present for the job? (1)
  2. What is the probability that construction will be completed on time? (1)
  3. What is the probability that many workers are not present given that the construction work is completed on time? (2)
    OR
    What is the probability that all workers were present given that the construction job was completed on time? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A building contractor undertakes a job to construct 4 flats on a plot along with parking area. Due to strike the probability of many construction workers not being present for the job is 0.65. The probability that many are not present and still the work gets completed on time is 0.35. The probability that work will be completed on time when all workers are present is 0.80. Let: E 1 : represent the event when many workers were not present for the job; E 2 : represent the event when all workers were present; and E: represent completing the construction work on time. What is the probability that all the workers are present for the job? (1) What is the probability that construction will be completed on time? (1) What is the probability that many workers are not present given that the construction work is completed on time? (2) OR What is the probability that all workers were present given that the construction job was completed on time? (2)
explanation: P(E 2 ) = 1 - P(E 1 ) = 1 – 0.65 = 0.35 P(E) = P(E 1 ) {tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_1}\right){/tex} + P(E 2 ) {tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_2}\right){/tex} = 0.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.35 + 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 = 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.45 = 0.51 {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)+\text P\left(\text E_2\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.65 \times 0.35}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.45 OR {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}{E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.35 \times 0.8}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.55
solution: P(E 2 ) = 1 - P(E 1 ) = 1 – 0.65 = 0.35 P(E) = P(E 1 ) {tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_1}\right){/tex} + P(E 2 ) {tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_2}\right){/tex} = 0.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.35 + 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 = 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.45 = 0.51 {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)+\text P\left(\text E_2\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.65 \times 0.35}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.45 OR {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}{E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.35 \times 0.8}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.55
solution_html:
1. P(E2) = 1 - P(E1) = 1 – 0.65 = 0.35

  2. P(E) = P(E1){tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_1}\right){/tex} + P(E2){tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_2}\right){/tex}
    = 0.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.35 + 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8
    = 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.45
    = 0.51

  3. {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)+\text P\left(\text E_2\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.65 \times 0.35}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.45
    OR
    {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}{E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.35 \times 0.8}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.55

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Recent studies suggest that roughly 12% of the world population is left handed.

Depending upon the parents, the chances of having a left handed child are as follows:

  1. When both father and mother are left handed:
    Chances of left handed child is 24%.
  2. When father is right handed and mother is left handed:
    Chances of left handed child is 22%.
  3. When father is left handed and mother is right handed:
    Chances of left handed child is 17%.
  4. When both father and mother are right handed:
    Chances of left handed child is 9%.

Assuming that P(A) = P(B) = P(C) = P(D) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} and L denotes the event that child is left handed.

  1. Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text C}\right){/tex}. (1)
  2. Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\bar {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex}. (1)
  3. Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text L}\right){/tex}. (2)
    OR
    Find the probability that a randomly selected child is left handed given that exactly one of the parents is left handed. (2)
question_text: Recent studies suggest that roughly 12% of the world population is left handed. Depending upon the parents, the chances of having a left handed child are as follows: When both father and mother are left handed: Chances of left handed child is 24%. When father is right handed and mother is left handed: Chances of left handed child is 22%. When father is left handed and mother is right handed: Chances of left handed child is 17%. When both father and mother are right handed: Chances of left handed child is 9%. Assuming that P(A) = P(B) = P(C) = P(D) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} and L denotes the event that child is left handed. Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text C}\right){/tex} . (1) Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\bar {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} . (1) Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text L}\right){/tex} . (2) OR Find the probability that a randomly selected child is left handed given that exactly one of the parents is left handed. (2)
explanation: {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text C}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac {17}{100}{/tex} {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\bar {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} = 1 - {tex}\text P\left(\frac { {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac {24}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {76}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {19}{25}{/tex} {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text L}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{24}{100}}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{24}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{17}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{9}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{24}{72}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Probability that a randomly selected child is left-handed given that exactly one of the parents is left-handed. = {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text B \cup \text C}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{22}{100}+\frac{17}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{39}{100}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text C}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac {17}{100}{/tex} {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\bar {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} = 1 - {tex}\text P\left(\frac { {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac {24}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {76}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {19}{25}{/tex} {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text L}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{24}{100}}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{24}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{17}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{9}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{24}{72}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Probability that a randomly selected child is left-handed given that exactly one of the parents is left-handed. = {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text B \cup \text C}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{22}{100}+\frac{17}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{39}{100}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text C}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac {17}{100}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\bar {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} = 1 - {tex}\text P\left(\frac { {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac {24}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {76}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {19}{25}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text L}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{24}{100}}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{24}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{17}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{9}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{24}{72}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
    Probability that a randomly selected child is left-handed given that exactly one of the parents is left-handed.
    = {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text B \cup \text C}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{22}{100}+\frac{17}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{39}{100}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Three persons A, B and C apply for a job of Manager in a private company. Chances of their selection (A, B and C are in the ratio 1:2:4. The probabilities that A, B and C can introduce changes to improve profits of the company are 0.8, 0.5 and 0.3, respectively. If the change does not take place, find the probability that it is due to the appointment of C.
question_text: Three persons A, B and C apply for a job of Manager in a private company. Chances of their selection (A, B and C are in the ratio 1:2:4. The probabilities that A, B and C can introduce changes to improve profits of the company are 0.8, 0.5 and 0.3, respectively. If the change does not take place, find the probability that it is due to the appointment of C.
explanation: Let us define the following events A = selecting person A B = selecting person B C = selecting person C P(A) = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 1 + 2 + 4 } {/tex} , P(B) = {tex} \frac { 2 } { 1 + 2 + 4 }{/tex} and P(C) = {tex} \frac { 4 } { 1 + 2 + 4 }{/tex} P(A) = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 7 }{/tex} , P(B) = {tex} \frac { 2 } { 7 }{/tex} and P(C) = {tex} \frac { 4 } { 7 }{/tex} Let E = Event to introduce the changes in their profit. Also given {tex} P \left( \frac { E } { A } \right) {/tex} = 0.8, {tex}P \left( \frac { E } { B } \right){/tex} = 0.5 and {tex} P \left( \frac { E } { C } \right) {/tex} = 0.3 {tex} \Rightarrow P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { A } \right){/tex} = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2, {tex} P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { B } \right) {/tex} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 and {tex} \ P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { C } \right) {/tex} = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7 The probability that change does not take place by the appointment of C, {tex} P \left( \frac { C } { E } \right) = \frac { P ( { C } ) \cdot P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { C } \right) } { P ( A ) \times P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { A } \right) + P ( B ) \times P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { B } \right) + P ( { C } ) \times P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { C } \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \frac { 4 } { 7 } \times 0.7 } { \frac { 1 } { 7 } \times 0.2 + \frac { 2 } { 7 } \times 0.5 + \frac { 4 } { 7 } \times 0.7 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 2.8 } { 0.2 + 1.0 + 2.8 } = \frac { 2.8 } { 4 } {/tex} = 0.7
solution: Let us define the following events A = selecting person A B = selecting person B C = selecting person C P(A) = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 1 + 2 + 4 } {/tex} , P(B) = {tex} \frac { 2 } { 1 + 2 + 4 }{/tex} and P(C) = {tex} \frac { 4 } { 1 + 2 + 4 }{/tex} P(A) = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 7 }{/tex} , P(B) = {tex} \frac { 2 } { 7 }{/tex} and P(C) = {tex} \frac { 4 } { 7 }{/tex} Let E = Event to introduce the changes in their profit. Also given {tex} P \left( \frac { E } { A } \right) {/tex} = 0.8, {tex}P \left( \frac { E } { B } \right){/tex} = 0.5 and {tex} P \left( \frac { E } { C } \right) {/tex} = 0.3 {tex} \Rightarrow P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { A } \right){/tex} = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2, {tex} P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { B } \right) {/tex} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 and {tex} \ P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { C } \right) {/tex} = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7 The probability that change does not take place by the appointment of C, {tex} P \left( \frac { C } { E } \right) = \frac { P ( { C } ) \cdot P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { C } \right) } { P ( A ) \times P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { A } \right) + P ( B ) \times P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { B } \right) + P ( { C } ) \times P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { C } \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \frac { 4 } { 7 } \times 0.7 } { \frac { 1 } { 7 } \times 0.2 + \frac { 2 } { 7 } \times 0.5 + \frac { 4 } { 7 } \times 0.7 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 2.8 } { 0.2 + 1.0 + 2.8 } = \frac { 2.8 } { 4 } {/tex} = 0.7
solution_html:
Let us define the following events
A = selecting person A
B = selecting person B
C = selecting person C
P(A) = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 1 + 2 + 4 } {/tex}, P(B) = {tex} \frac { 2 } { 1 + 2 + 4 }{/tex}
and  P(C) = {tex} \frac { 4 } { 1 + 2 + 4 }{/tex}
P(A) = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 7 }{/tex}, P(B) = {tex} \frac { 2 } { 7 }{/tex}
and P(C) = {tex} \frac { 4 } { 7 }{/tex}
Let E = Event to introduce the changes in their profit.
Also given {tex} P \left( \frac { E } { A } \right) {/tex} = 0.8, {tex}P \left( \frac { E } { B } \right){/tex}  = 0.5 and {tex} P \left( \frac { E } { C } \right) {/tex} = 0.3
{tex} \Rightarrow P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { A } \right){/tex} = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2, {tex} P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { B } \right) {/tex} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5
and {tex} \ P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { C } \right) {/tex} = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7
The probability that change does not take place by the appointment of C,
{tex} P \left( \frac { C } { E } \right) = \frac { P ( { C } ) \cdot P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { C } \right) } { P ( A ) \times P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { A } \right) + P ( B ) \times P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { B } \right) + P ( { C } ) \times P \left( \frac { \overline { E } } { C } \right) }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \frac { 4 } { 7 } \times 0.7 } { \frac { 1 } { 7 } \times 0.2 + \frac { 2 } { 7 } \times 0.5 + \frac { 4 } { 7 } \times 0.7 }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 2.8 } { 0.2 + 1.0 + 2.8 } = \frac { 2.8 } { 4 } {/tex} = 0.7

question_html: The probability that A hits a target is {tex}\frac 13{/tex} and the probability that B hits it, is {tex}\frac 25{/tex}. What is the probability that the target will be hit, if each one of A and B shoots at the target?
question_text: The probability that A hits a target is {tex}\frac 13{/tex} and the probability that B hits it, is {tex}\frac 25{/tex} . What is the probability that the target will be hit, if each one of A and B shoots at the target?
explanation: P(A) = P(A hits target) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} P(B) = P(B hits target) = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} Now, Target will be hit if atleast one of them hits the target. Then, required probability is given by, P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) = P (target will be hit by either A or B) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B){/tex} = P(A) + P(B) - P(A)P(B) [A and B are independent] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{2}{5}-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{5+6-2}{15}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{9}{15}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
solution: P(A) = P(A hits target) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} P(B) = P(B hits target) = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} Now, Target will be hit if atleast one of them hits the target. Then, required probability is given by, P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) = P (target will be hit by either A or B) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B){/tex} = P(A) + P(B) - P(A)P(B) [A and B are independent] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{2}{5}-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{5+6-2}{15}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{9}{15}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
solution_html:
P(A) = P(A hits target) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
P(B) = P(B hits target) = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
Now, Target will be hit if atleast one of them hits the target. Then, required probability is given by,
P(A {tex}\cup{/tex}B) = P (target will be hit by either A or B)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B){/tex} = P(A) + P(B) - P(A)P(B) [A and B are independent]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{2}{5}-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{2}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{5+6-2}{15}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{9}{15}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}

question_html: In a factory, machine A produces 30% of the total output, machine B produces 25% and the machine C produces the remaining output. If defective items produced by machines A, B and C are 1%, 1.2%, 2% respectively. Three machines working together produce 10000 items in a day. An item is drawn at random from a day's output and found to be defective. Find the probability that it was produced by machine B?
question_text: In a factory, machine A produces 30% of the total output, machine B produces 25% and the machine C produces the remaining output. If defective items produced by machines A, B and C are 1%, 1.2%, 2% respectively. Three machines working together produce 10000 items in a day. An item is drawn at random from a day's output and found to be defective. Find the probability that it was produced by machine B?
explanation: Let A, E 1 , E 2 and E 3 denote the events that the item is defective, machine A is chosen, machine B is chosen and machine C is chosen, respectively. Therefore, we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{30}{100}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex} P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{45}{100}{/tex} Now, we have, {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{1}})=\frac{1}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{1.2}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {A}{E_{3}})=\frac{2}{100}{/tex} Using Bayes' theorem, we have, Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{1}} { A}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)+P\left(E_{3}\right) P\left(A / E_{3}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{1}{100}}{\frac{30}{100} \times+\times \frac{1}{100} \frac{25}{100} \frac{1.2}{100}+\frac{45}{100} \times \frac{2}{100}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{30}{30+30+90}=\frac{30}{150}{/tex} = 0.2
solution: Let A, E 1 , E 2 and E 3 denote the events that the item is defective, machine A is chosen, machine B is chosen and machine C is chosen, respectively. Therefore, we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{30}{100}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex} P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{45}{100}{/tex} Now, we have, {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{1}})=\frac{1}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{1.2}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {A}{E_{3}})=\frac{2}{100}{/tex} Using Bayes' theorem, we have, Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{1}} { A}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)+P\left(E_{3}\right) P\left(A / E_{3}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{1}{100}}{\frac{30}{100} \times+\times \frac{1}{100} \frac{25}{100} \frac{1.2}{100}+\frac{45}{100} \times \frac{2}{100}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{30}{30+30+90}=\frac{30}{150}{/tex} = 0.2
solution_html:
Let A, E1, E2 and E3 denote the events that the item is defective, machine A is chosen, machine B is chosen and machine C is chosen, respectively. Therefore, we have,
P(E1) = {tex}\frac{30}{100}{/tex}
P(E2) = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex}
P(E3) = {tex}\frac{45}{100}{/tex}
Now, we have,
{tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{1}})=\frac{1}{100}{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{1.2}{100}{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac {A}{E_{3}})=\frac{2}{100}{/tex}
Using Bayes' theorem, we have,
Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{1}} { A}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)+P\left(E_{3}\right) P\left(A / E_{3}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{1}{100}}{\frac{30}{100} \times+\times \frac{1}{100} \frac{25}{100} \frac{1.2}{100}+\frac{45}{100} \times \frac{2}{100}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{30}{30+30+90}=\frac{30}{150}{/tex} = 0.2

question_html: A box B1 contains 1 white ball and 3 red balls. Another box B2 contains 2 white balls and 3 red balls. If one ball is drawn at random from each of the boxes B1 and B2, then find the probability that the two balls drawn are of the same colour.
question_text: A box B 1 contains 1 white ball and 3 red balls. Another box B 2 contains 2 white balls and 3 red balls. If one ball is drawn at random from each of the boxes B 1 and B 2 , then find the probability that the two balls drawn are of the same colour.
explanation: {tex}P\left(\frac W{B_1}\right)=\frac14,\;P\left(\frac R{B_1}\right)=\frac34{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac W{B_2}\right)=\frac25,\;P\left(\frac R{B_2}\right)=\frac35{/tex} Probability that both the balls drawn from box B 1 and B 2 each of same colour = {tex}P\left(\frac W{B_1}\right)\times P\left(\frac W{B_2}\right)+P\left(\frac R{B_1}\right)\times P\left(\frac R{B_2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac14\times\frac25+\frac34\times\frac35=\frac2{20}+\frac9{20}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{11}{20}{/tex}
solution: {tex}P\left(\frac W{B_1}\right)=\frac14,\;P\left(\frac R{B_1}\right)=\frac34{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac W{B_2}\right)=\frac25,\;P\left(\frac R{B_2}\right)=\frac35{/tex} Probability that both the balls drawn from box B 1 and B 2 each of same colour = {tex}P\left(\frac W{B_1}\right)\times P\left(\frac W{B_2}\right)+P\left(\frac R{B_1}\right)\times P\left(\frac R{B_2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac14\times\frac25+\frac34\times\frac35=\frac2{20}+\frac9{20}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{11}{20}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}P\left(\frac W{B_1}\right)=\frac14,\;P\left(\frac R{B_1}\right)=\frac34{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac W{B_2}\right)=\frac25,\;P\left(\frac R{B_2}\right)=\frac35{/tex}
Probability that both the balls drawn from box B1 and B2 each of same colour
= {tex}P\left(\frac W{B_1}\right)\times P\left(\frac W{B_2}\right)+P\left(\frac R{B_1}\right)\times P\left(\frac R{B_2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac14\times\frac25+\frac34\times\frac35=\frac2{20}+\frac9{20}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{11}{20}{/tex}

question_html: Events A and B are such that P(A) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, P(B) = {tex}\frac{7}{12}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{P}(\bar{\mathrm{A}} \cup \bar{\mathrm{B}})=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Find whether the events A and B are independent or not.
question_text: Events A and B are such that P(A) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac{7}{12}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{P}(\bar{\mathrm{A}} \cup \bar{\mathrm{B}})=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Find whether the events A and B are independent or not.
explanation: P(A) = {tex}\frac12{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac7{12}{/tex} , {tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right)=\frac14{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right)=P\left(A\cap B\right)'{/tex} {tex}\frac14=1-P\left(A\cap B\right){/tex} {tex}P\left(A\cap B\right)=1-\frac14=\frac34{/tex} Also, P(A).P(B) = {tex}\frac12×\frac7{12}=\frac7{24}{/tex} Clearly ​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​, {tex}P\left(A\cap B\right)\neq P\left(A\right).P\left(B\right){/tex} ​​​​​​​ {tex}\because{/tex} ​​​​​​​ the Events A and B are independent
solution: P(A) = {tex}\frac12{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac7{12}{/tex} , {tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right)=\frac14{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right)=P\left(A\cap B\right)'{/tex} {tex}\frac14=1-P\left(A\cap B\right){/tex} {tex}P\left(A\cap B\right)=1-\frac14=\frac34{/tex} Also, P(A).P(B) = {tex}\frac12×\frac7{12}=\frac7{24}{/tex} Clearly ​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​, {tex}P\left(A\cap B\right)\neq P\left(A\right).P\left(B\right){/tex} ​​​​​​​ {tex}\because{/tex} ​​​​​​​ the Events A and B are independent
solution_html:
P(A) = {tex}\frac12{/tex}, P(B) = {tex}\frac7{12}{/tex}, {tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right)=\frac14{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right)=P\left(A\cap B\right)'{/tex}
{tex}\frac14=1-P\left(A\cap B\right){/tex}
{tex}P\left(A\cap B\right)=1-\frac14=\frac34{/tex}
Also, P(A).P(B) = {tex}\frac12×\frac7{12}=\frac7{24}{/tex}
Clearly ​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​, {tex}P\left(A\cap B\right)\neq P\left(A\right).P\left(B\right){/tex}​​​​​​​
{tex}\because{/tex}​​​​​​​ the Events A and B are independent

question_html: Three persons A, B and C apply for a job of manager in a private company. Chances of their selection are in the ratio 1 : 2 : 4. The probability that A, B and C can introduce changes to increase the profits of a company are 0.8, 0.5 and 0.3 respectively. If increase in the profit does not take place, find the probability that it is due to the appointment of A.
question_text: Three persons A, B and C apply for a job of manager in a private company. Chances of their selection are in the ratio 1 : 2 : 4. The probability that A, B and C can introduce changes to increase the profits of a company are 0.8, 0.5 and 0.3 respectively. If increase in the profit does not take place, find the probability that it is due to the appointment of A.
explanation: Let the events be described as below: A: No change takes place E 1 : Person A gets appointed E 2 : Person B gets appointed E 3 : Person C gets appointed. The chances of selection of A,B and C are in the ratio 1 : 2 : 4. So, {tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac17,\;P\left(E_2\right)=\frac27,\;P\left(E_3\right)=\frac37{/tex} Probabilities of A,B and C introducing changes to improve profits of company are 0.8,0.5 and 0.3 respectively. Hence the probability of no changes on the appointment of A,B and C are 0.2,0.5 and 0.7 respectively. {tex}\therefore P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=0.2=\frac15{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=0.5=\frac12{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_3}\right)=0.7=\frac7{10}{/tex} Now, required probability = {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)P\left(E_1\right)}{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)P\left(E_1\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)P\left(E_2\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_3}}\right)P\left(E_3\right)}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{{\frac15}\times{\frac17}}{{\frac15}\times{\frac17}+{\frac12}\times{\frac27}+{\frac7{10}}\times{\frac37}}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{\frac1{35}}{\frac{33}{70}}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac2{33}{/tex} ∴ If there is no change due to profit probability of A due to appointment is {tex}\frac{2}{33}{/tex} .
solution: Let the events be described as below: A: No change takes place E 1 : Person A gets appointed E 2 : Person B gets appointed E 3 : Person C gets appointed. The chances of selection of A,B and C are in the ratio 1 : 2 : 4. So, {tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac17,\;P\left(E_2\right)=\frac27,\;P\left(E_3\right)=\frac37{/tex} Probabilities of A,B and C introducing changes to improve profits of company are 0.8,0.5 and 0.3 respectively. Hence the probability of no changes on the appointment of A,B and C are 0.2,0.5 and 0.7 respectively. {tex}\therefore P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=0.2=\frac15{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=0.5=\frac12{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_3}\right)=0.7=\frac7{10}{/tex} Now, required probability = {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)P\left(E_1\right)}{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)P\left(E_1\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)P\left(E_2\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_3}}\right)P\left(E_3\right)}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{{\frac15}\times{\frac17}}{{\frac15}\times{\frac17}+{\frac12}\times{\frac27}+{\frac7{10}}\times{\frac37}}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{\frac1{35}}{\frac{33}{70}}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac2{33}{/tex} ∴ If there is no change due to profit probability of A due to appointment is {tex}\frac{2}{33}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let the events be described as below:
A: No change takes place
E1 : Person A gets appointed
E2 : Person B gets appointed
E3 : Person C gets appointed.
The chances of selection of A,B and C are in the ratio 1 : 2 : 4.
So, {tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac17,\;P\left(E_2\right)=\frac27,\;P\left(E_3\right)=\frac37{/tex}
Probabilities of A,B and C introducing changes to improve profits of company are 0.8,0.5 and 0.3 respectively. Hence the probability of no changes on the appointment of A,B and C are 0.2,0.5 and 0.7 respectively.
{tex}\therefore P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=0.2=\frac15{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=0.5=\frac12{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac A{E_3}\right)=0.7=\frac7{10}{/tex}
Now, required probability =
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)P\left(E_1\right)}{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)P\left(E_1\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)P\left(E_2\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_3}}\right)P\left(E_3\right)}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{{\frac15}\times{\frac17}}{{\frac15}\times{\frac17}+{\frac12}\times{\frac27}+{\frac7{10}}\times{\frac37}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{\frac1{35}}{\frac{33}{70}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac2{33}{/tex}
∴ If there is no change due to profit probability of A due to appointment is {tex}\frac{2}{33}{/tex}.
