question_html: There are two boxes, namely box-I and box-II. Box-I contains 3 red and 6 black balls. Box-II contains 5 red and 5 black balls. One of the two boxes, is selected at random and a ball is drawn at random. The ball drawn is found to be red. Find the probability that this red ball comes out from box-II.
question_text: There are two boxes, namely box-I and box-II. Box-I contains 3 red and 6 black balls. Box-II contains 5 red and 5 black balls. One of the two boxes, is selected at random and a ball is drawn at random. The ball drawn is found to be red. Find the probability that this red ball comes out from box-II.
explanation: Let E 1 be the event when box I is selected E 2 be the event when box II is selected A be the event of getting red ball in any box {tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac12,\;P\left(E_2\right)=\frac12{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=\frac39=\frac12{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=\frac5{10}=\frac12{/tex} Now, required probability = {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}A\right)=\frac{P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)P\left(E_2\right)}{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)P\left(E_1\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)P\left(E_2\right)}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}A\right)=\frac{{\frac12}\times{\frac12}}{{\frac12}\times{\frac13}+{\frac12}\times{\frac12}}=\frac{\frac14}{\frac5{12}}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}A\right)=\frac35{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 be the event when box I is selected E 2 be the event when box II is selected A be the event of getting red ball in any box {tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac12,\;P\left(E_2\right)=\frac12{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=\frac39=\frac12{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=\frac5{10}=\frac12{/tex} Now, required probability = {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}A\right)=\frac{P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)P\left(E_2\right)}{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)P\left(E_1\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)P\left(E_2\right)}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}A\right)=\frac{{\frac12}\times{\frac12}}{{\frac12}\times{\frac13}+{\frac12}\times{\frac12}}=\frac{\frac14}{\frac5{12}}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}A\right)=\frac35{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 be the event when box I is selected
E2 be the event when box II is selected
A be the event of getting red ball in any box
{tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac12,\;P\left(E_2\right)=\frac12{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=\frac39=\frac12{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=\frac5{10}=\frac12{/tex}
Now, required probability =
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}A\right)=\frac{P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)P\left(E_2\right)}{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)P\left(E_1\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)P\left(E_2\right)}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}A\right)=\frac{{\frac12}\times{\frac12}}{{\frac12}\times{\frac13}+{\frac12}\times{\frac12}}=\frac{\frac14}{\frac5{12}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}A\right)=\frac35{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Let A and B be two events such that P(A) = {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex}, P(B) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}. Find the value of P(B/A).
question_text: Let A and B be two events such that P(A) = {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} . Find the value of P(B/A).
explanation: ∵ P(A) = {tex}\frac58{/tex} ​​​​​​, P(B) = {tex}\frac12{/tex} , P(A/B) = {tex}\frac34{/tex} ∵ {tex}P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} ​​​​​​​ {tex}\frac34=\frac{P(A\cap B)}{\frac12}{/tex} ​ ​​​​​ {tex}P(A\cap B)=\frac38{/tex} Now, {tex}P\left(\frac{B}{A}\right)=\frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex} ​​​​​​​ {tex}P\left(\frac{B}{A}\right)=\frac{\frac{3}{8}}{\frac{5}{8}}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} ​​​​​​​
solution: ∵ P(A) = {tex}\frac58{/tex} ​​​​​​, P(B) = {tex}\frac12{/tex} , P(A/B) = {tex}\frac34{/tex} ∵ {tex}P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} ​​​​​​​ {tex}\frac34=\frac{P(A\cap B)}{\frac12}{/tex} ​ ​​​​​ {tex}P(A\cap B)=\frac38{/tex} Now, {tex}P\left(\frac{B}{A}\right)=\frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex} ​​​​​​​ {tex}P\left(\frac{B}{A}\right)=\frac{\frac{3}{8}}{\frac{5}{8}}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} ​​​​​​​
solution_html:
∵ P(A) = {tex}\frac58{/tex}​​​​​​, P(B) = {tex}\frac12{/tex} , P(A/B) = {tex}\frac34{/tex}
∵ {tex}P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex}​​​​​​​
{tex}\frac34=\frac{P(A\cap B)}{\frac12}{/tex}​
​​​​​{tex}P(A\cap B)=\frac38{/tex}
Now, {tex}P\left(\frac{B}{A}\right)=\frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex}​​​​​​​
{tex}P\left(\frac{B}{A}\right)=\frac{\frac{3}{8}}{\frac{5}{8}}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}​​​​​​​

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A card from a pack of 52 playing cards is lost. From the remaining cards, 2 cards are drawn at random without replacement and are found to be both aces. Find the probability that lost card being an ace.
question_text: A card from a pack of 52 playing cards is lost. From the remaining cards, 2 cards are drawn at random without replacement and are found to be both aces. Find the probability that lost card being an ace.
explanation: Let ,E 1 = Lost card is ace E 2 = lost card is not ace A: Drawn card (both) are Ace {tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac4{52}=\frac1{13}{/tex} {tex}P\left(E_2\right)=1-\frac1{13}=\frac{12}{13}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=\frac3{51}\times\frac2{50}{/tex} , {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=\frac4{51}\times\frac3{50}{/tex} Required probability= {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right).P\left(E_1\right)}{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right).P\left(E_1\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right).P\left(E_2\right)}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{{\frac3{51}}\times{\frac2{50}}\times{\frac1{13}}}{{\frac3{51}}\times{\frac2{50}}\times{\frac1{13}}+{\frac4{51}}\times{\frac3{50}}\times{\frac{12}{13}}}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac6{150}=\frac1{25}{/tex}
solution: Let ,E 1 = Lost card is ace E 2 = lost card is not ace A: Drawn card (both) are Ace {tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac4{52}=\frac1{13}{/tex} {tex}P\left(E_2\right)=1-\frac1{13}=\frac{12}{13}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=\frac3{51}\times\frac2{50}{/tex} , {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=\frac4{51}\times\frac3{50}{/tex} Required probability= {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right).P\left(E_1\right)}{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right).P\left(E_1\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right).P\left(E_2\right)}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{{\frac3{51}}\times{\frac2{50}}\times{\frac1{13}}}{{\frac3{51}}\times{\frac2{50}}\times{\frac1{13}}+{\frac4{51}}\times{\frac3{50}}\times{\frac{12}{13}}}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac6{150}=\frac1{25}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let ,E1 = Lost card is ace
E2 = lost card is not ace
A: Drawn card (both) are Ace
{tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac4{52}=\frac1{13}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(E_2\right)=1-\frac1{13}=\frac{12}{13}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=\frac3{51}\times\frac2{50}{/tex} , {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=\frac4{51}\times\frac3{50}{/tex}
Required probability= {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right).P\left(E_1\right)}{P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right).P\left(E_1\right)+P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right).P\left(E_2\right)}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{{\frac3{51}}\times{\frac2{50}}\times{\frac1{13}}}{{\frac3{51}}\times{\frac2{50}}\times{\frac1{13}}+{\frac4{51}}\times{\frac3{50}}\times{\frac{12}{13}}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac6{150}=\frac1{25}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A man is known to speak truth 7 out of 10 times. He threw a pair of dice and reports that a doublet appeared. Find the probability that it was actually a doublet.
question_text: A man is known to speak truth 7 out of 10 times. He threw a pair of dice and reports that a doublet appeared. Find the probability that it was actually a doublet.
explanation: Let A be the event that man says a doublet appears. E 1 = Doublet appears, E 2 = Doublet not appears {tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac16,\;P\left(E_2\right)=1-\frac16=\frac56{/tex} P(A/E 1 ) is the probability when the man says it is a doublet and it is actually a doublet, P(A/E 2 ) is probability when man says it is doublet but it was not {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=\frac7{10},\;P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=\frac3{10}{/tex} According to bayes' theorem, {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_1\right)P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)}{P\left(E_1\right)P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)+P\left(E_2\right)P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{{\frac16}\times{\frac3{10}}}{{\frac16}\times{\frac3{10}}+{\frac56}\times{\frac3{10}}}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{\frac1{20}}{\frac{11}{20}}=\frac1{11}{/tex}
solution: Let A be the event that man says a doublet appears. E 1 = Doublet appears, E 2 = Doublet not appears {tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac16,\;P\left(E_2\right)=1-\frac16=\frac56{/tex} P(A/E 1 ) is the probability when the man says it is a doublet and it is actually a doublet, P(A/E 2 ) is probability when man says it is doublet but it was not {tex}P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=\frac7{10},\;P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=\frac3{10}{/tex} According to bayes' theorem, {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_1\right)P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)}{P\left(E_1\right)P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)+P\left(E_2\right)P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{{\frac16}\times{\frac3{10}}}{{\frac16}\times{\frac3{10}}+{\frac56}\times{\frac3{10}}}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{\frac1{20}}{\frac{11}{20}}=\frac1{11}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A be the event that man says a doublet appears.
E1 = Doublet appears, E2 = Doublet not appears
{tex}P\left(E_1\right)=\frac16,\;P\left(E_2\right)=1-\frac16=\frac56{/tex}
P(A/E1) is the probability when the man says it is a doublet and it is actually a doublet, P(A/E2) is probability when man says it is doublet but it was not
{tex}P\left(\frac A{E_1}\right)=\frac7{10},\;P\left(\frac A{E_2}\right)=\frac3{10}{/tex}
According to bayes' theorem,
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_1\right)P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)}{P\left(E_1\right)P\left({\frac A{E_1}}\right)+P\left(E_2\right)P\left({\frac A{E_2}}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{{\frac16}\times{\frac3{10}}}{{\frac16}\times{\frac3{10}}+{\frac56}\times{\frac3{10}}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}A\right)=\frac{\frac1{20}}{\frac{11}{20}}=\frac1{11}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A pair of dice is thrown. It is given that the sum of numbers appearing on both dice is an even number. Find the probability that the number appearing on at least one die is 3.
question_text: A pair of dice is thrown. It is given that the sum of numbers appearing on both dice is an even number. Find the probability that the number appearing on at least one die is 3.
explanation: A pair of dice is thrown. Total outcomes, n = 36 Let A denote the event that the sum of numbers appearing on both dice is an even number and let B denote the event that the number appearing on at least one die is 3. Now, Number of favourable outcomes for A, n(A) = {(1, 1), (1, 3), (1, 5), (2, 2), (2, 4), (2, 6), (3, 1), (3, 3), (3, 5), (4, 2), (4, 4), (4, 6), (5, 1), (5, 3), (5, 5), (6, 2), (6, 4), (6, 6)} = 18 Number of favourable outcomes for B, n(B) = {(1, 3), (2, 3), (3, 3), (4, 3), (5, 3), (6, 3), (3, 1), (3, 2), (3, 4), (3, 5), (3, 6)} = 11 Number of favorable outcomes for A∩B, n(A∩B) = {(1, 3), (3, 3), (5, 3), (3, 1), (3, 5)} = 5 P (A) = {tex}\frac{n(A)}n=\frac{18}{36}=\frac12{/tex} P (B) = {tex}\frac{n(B)}n=\frac{11}{36}{/tex} {tex}P(A\cap B)=\frac{n(A\cap B)}n=\frac5{36}{/tex} Now, P ( {tex}\frac AB{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} P ( {tex}\frac AB{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{\frac5{36}}{\frac{11}{36}}=\frac5{11}{/tex}
solution: A pair of dice is thrown. Total outcomes, n = 36 Let A denote the event that the sum of numbers appearing on both dice is an even number and let B denote the event that the number appearing on at least one die is 3. Now, Number of favourable outcomes for A, n(A) = {(1, 1), (1, 3), (1, 5), (2, 2), (2, 4), (2, 6), (3, 1), (3, 3), (3, 5), (4, 2), (4, 4), (4, 6), (5, 1), (5, 3), (5, 5), (6, 2), (6, 4), (6, 6)} = 18 Number of favourable outcomes for B, n(B) = {(1, 3), (2, 3), (3, 3), (4, 3), (5, 3), (6, 3), (3, 1), (3, 2), (3, 4), (3, 5), (3, 6)} = 11 Number of favorable outcomes for A∩B, n(A∩B) = {(1, 3), (3, 3), (5, 3), (3, 1), (3, 5)} = 5 P (A) = {tex}\frac{n(A)}n=\frac{18}{36}=\frac12{/tex} P (B) = {tex}\frac{n(B)}n=\frac{11}{36}{/tex} {tex}P(A\cap B)=\frac{n(A\cap B)}n=\frac5{36}{/tex} Now, P ( {tex}\frac AB{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} P ( {tex}\frac AB{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{\frac5{36}}{\frac{11}{36}}=\frac5{11}{/tex}
solution_html:
A pair of dice is thrown.
Total outcomes, n = 36
Let A denote the event that the sum of numbers appearing on both dice is an even number and let B denote the event that the number appearing on at least one die is 3.
Now, Number of favourable outcomes for A, n(A) = {(1, 1), (1, 3), (1, 5), (2, 2), (2, 4), (2, 6), (3, 1), (3, 3), (3, 5), (4, 2), (4, 4), (4, 6), (5, 1), (5, 3), (5, 5), (6, 2), (6, 4), (6, 6)} = 18
Number of favourable outcomes for B, n(B) = {(1, 3), (2, 3), (3, 3), (4, 3), (5, 3), (6, 3), (3, 1), (3, 2), (3, 4), (3, 5), (3, 6)} = 11
Number of favorable outcomes for A∩B, n(A∩B) = {(1, 3), (3, 3), (5, 3), (3, 1), (3, 5)} = 5
P (A) = {tex}\frac{n(A)}n=\frac{18}{36}=\frac12{/tex}
P (B) = {tex}\frac{n(B)}n=\frac{11}{36}{/tex}
{tex}P(A\cap B)=\frac{n(A\cap B)}n=\frac5{36}{/tex}
Now,
P ({tex}\frac AB{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex}
P ({tex}\frac AB{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{\frac5{36}}{\frac{11}{36}}=\frac5{11}{/tex}

question_html: Probabilities of A and B solving a specific problem are {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex}, respectively. If both of them try independently to solve the problem, then find the probability that the problem is solved.
question_text: Probabilities of A and B solving a specific problem are {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex} , respectively. If both of them try independently to solve the problem, then find the probability that the problem is solved.
explanation: {tex}\because{/tex} P(A) = {tex}\frac23{/tex} ​​​​ ​​​and P(B) = {tex}\frac35{/tex} {tex}P(A\cap B)=P(A).P(B){/tex} {tex}P(A\cap B)=\frac23\times\frac35=\frac25{/tex} P (problem is solved) = Probability that A solves the problem or B solves the problem P (Problem solved) = {tex}P(A\cup B){/tex} = {tex}P(A)+P(B)-P(A\cap B){/tex} ​ ​​​​​​= {tex}\frac23+\frac35-\frac25{/tex} ​ ​​​​​​= {tex}\frac{10+9+6}{15}=\frac{25}{15}{/tex} P (Problem solved) = {tex}\frac53{/tex} ​​​​​​​
solution: {tex}\because{/tex} P(A) = {tex}\frac23{/tex} ​​​​ ​​​and P(B) = {tex}\frac35{/tex} {tex}P(A\cap B)=P(A).P(B){/tex} {tex}P(A\cap B)=\frac23\times\frac35=\frac25{/tex} P (problem is solved) = Probability that A solves the problem or B solves the problem P (Problem solved) = {tex}P(A\cup B){/tex} = {tex}P(A)+P(B)-P(A\cap B){/tex} ​ ​​​​​​= {tex}\frac23+\frac35-\frac25{/tex} ​ ​​​​​​= {tex}\frac{10+9+6}{15}=\frac{25}{15}{/tex} P (Problem solved) = {tex}\frac53{/tex} ​​​​​​​
solution_html:
{tex}\because{/tex} P(A) = {tex}\frac23{/tex}​​​​ ​​​and P(B) = {tex}\frac35{/tex}
{tex}P(A\cap B)=P(A).P(B){/tex}
{tex}P(A\cap B)=\frac23\times\frac35=\frac25{/tex}
P (problem is solved) = Probability that A solves the problem or B solves the problem
P (Problem solved) = {tex}P(A\cup B){/tex}
= {tex}P(A)+P(B)-P(A\cap B){/tex}​
​​​​​​= {tex}\frac23+\frac35-\frac25{/tex}​
​​​​​​= {tex}\frac{10+9+6}{15}=\frac{25}{15}{/tex}
P (Problem solved) = {tex}\frac53{/tex}​​​​​​​

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A pair of dice is thrown and the sum of the numbers appearing on the dice is observed to be 7. Find the probability that the number 5 has appeared on atleast one die.
question_text: A pair of dice is thrown and the sum of the numbers appearing on the dice is observed to be 7. Find the probability that the number 5 has appeared on atleast one die.
explanation: Let A be the event having sum 7 on the dice A = (1, 6), (6, 1), (5, 2), (2, 5), (4, 3), (3, 4) n(A) = 6 Let B be the event in which 5 appears atleast on one die B = (1, 5), (2, 5), (3, 5), (4, 5), (5, 5), (6, 5), (5, 1), (5, 2), (5, 3), (5, 4), (5, 6) n(B) = 11 A {tex}\cap{/tex} B = (2, 5), (5, 2) {tex}P\left(\frac BA\right)=\frac{n\left(A\cap B\right)}{n\left(A\right)}=\frac26=\frac13{/tex} So, the probability that 5 appears atleast on one dice is {tex}\frac13{/tex}
solution: Let A be the event having sum 7 on the dice A = (1, 6), (6, 1), (5, 2), (2, 5), (4, 3), (3, 4) n(A) = 6 Let B be the event in which 5 appears atleast on one die B = (1, 5), (2, 5), (3, 5), (4, 5), (5, 5), (6, 5), (5, 1), (5, 2), (5, 3), (5, 4), (5, 6) n(B) = 11 A {tex}\cap{/tex} B = (2, 5), (5, 2) {tex}P\left(\frac BA\right)=\frac{n\left(A\cap B\right)}{n\left(A\right)}=\frac26=\frac13{/tex} So, the probability that 5 appears atleast on one dice is {tex}\frac13{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A be the event having sum 7 on the dice
A = (1, 6), (6, 1), (5, 2), (2, 5), (4, 3), (3, 4)
n(A) = 6
Let B be the event in which 5 appears atleast on one die
B = (1, 5), (2, 5), (3, 5), (4, 5), (5, 5), (6, 5), (5, 1), (5, 2), (5, 3), (5, 4), (5, 6)
n(B) = 11
A{tex}\cap{/tex}B = (2, 5), (5, 2)
{tex}P\left(\frac BA\right)=\frac{n\left(A\cap B\right)}{n\left(A\right)}=\frac26=\frac13{/tex}
So, the probability that 5 appears atleast on one dice is {tex}\frac13{/tex}

question_html: A bag contains 3 red and 4 white balls. Three balls are drawn at random, one-by-one without replacement from the bag. If the first ball drawn is red in colour, then find the probability that the remaining two balls drawn are also red in colour.
question_text: A bag contains 3 red and 4 white balls. Three balls are drawn at random, one-by-one without replacement from the bag. If the first ball drawn is red in colour, then find the probability that the remaining two balls drawn are also red in colour.
explanation: Total balls = 3 + 4 = 7 Now if 1st ball was red then red balls left = 2 Total balls = 6 P(B 2 ) = {tex}\frac{{}^2C_1}{{}^6C_1}{/tex} ​​ ​​​​​And now for 3rd ball, red ball left = 1 Total balls= 5 P(B 3 ) = {tex}\frac{{}^1C_1}{{}^5C_1}{/tex} Now, required probability= {tex}\frac{{}^2C_1}{{}^6C_1}\times\frac{{}^1C_1}{{}^5C_1}=\frac26\times\frac15=\frac1{15}{/tex}
solution: Total balls = 3 + 4 = 7 Now if 1st ball was red then red balls left = 2 Total balls = 6 P(B 2 ) = {tex}\frac{{}^2C_1}{{}^6C_1}{/tex} ​​ ​​​​​And now for 3rd ball, red ball left = 1 Total balls= 5 P(B 3 ) = {tex}\frac{{}^1C_1}{{}^5C_1}{/tex} Now, required probability= {tex}\frac{{}^2C_1}{{}^6C_1}\times\frac{{}^1C_1}{{}^5C_1}=\frac26\times\frac15=\frac1{15}{/tex}
solution_html:
Total balls = 3 + 4 = 7
Now if 1st ball was red then red balls left = 2
Total balls = 6
P(B2) = {tex}\frac{{}^2C_1}{{}^6C_1}{/tex}​​
​​​​​And now for 3rd ball, red ball left = 1
Total balls= 5
P(B3) = {tex}\frac{{}^1C_1}{{}^5C_1}{/tex}
Now, required probability= {tex}\frac{{}^2C_1}{{}^6C_1}\times\frac{{}^1C_1}{{}^5C_1}=\frac26\times\frac15=\frac1{15}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: There are two bags. Bag I contains 1 red and 3 white balls, and Bag II contains 3 red and 5 white balls. A bag is selected at random and a ball is drawn from it. Find the probability that the ball so drawn is red in colour.
question_text: There are two bags. Bag I contains 1 red and 3 white balls, and Bag II contains 3 red and 5 white balls. A bag is selected at random and a ball is drawn from it. Find the probability that the ball so drawn is red in colour.
explanation: From the given data, Let assume that A is the event of choosing the Bag I, B is the event of choosing the Bag II and R is the event of drawing a red ball. ∴ P(A) = P(B) = {tex}\frac12{/tex} Now total balls in Bag I = 1 + 3 = 4 The probability of drawing a red ball from Bag I is, {tex}P\left(\frac RA\right)=\frac{{}^1C_1}{{}^4C_1}=\frac14{/tex} ​ Now total balls in Bag II = 3 + 5 = 8 The probability of drawing a red ball from Bag II is, {tex}P\left(\frac RB\right)=\frac{{}^3C_1}{{}^8C_1}=\frac38{/tex} ∴ P(R) = {tex}P\left(\frac RA\right)+P\left(\frac RB\right){/tex} ​​​​​​​ P(R) = {tex}\frac14+\frac38=\frac58{/tex} ​​​​​​​
solution: From the given data, Let assume that A is the event of choosing the Bag I, B is the event of choosing the Bag II and R is the event of drawing a red ball. ∴ P(A) = P(B) = {tex}\frac12{/tex} Now total balls in Bag I = 1 + 3 = 4 The probability of drawing a red ball from Bag I is, {tex}P\left(\frac RA\right)=\frac{{}^1C_1}{{}^4C_1}=\frac14{/tex} ​ Now total balls in Bag II = 3 + 5 = 8 The probability of drawing a red ball from Bag II is, {tex}P\left(\frac RB\right)=\frac{{}^3C_1}{{}^8C_1}=\frac38{/tex} ∴ P(R) = {tex}P\left(\frac RA\right)+P\left(\frac RB\right){/tex} ​​​​​​​ P(R) = {tex}\frac14+\frac38=\frac58{/tex} ​​​​​​​
solution_html:
From the given data,
Let assume that A is the event of choosing the Bag I, B is the event of choosing the Bag II and R is the event of drawing a red ball.
∴ P(A) = P(B) = {tex}\frac12{/tex}
Now total balls in Bag I = 1 + 3 = 4
The probability of drawing a red ball from Bag I is,
{tex}P\left(\frac RA\right)=\frac{{}^1C_1}{{}^4C_1}=\frac14{/tex}​
Now total balls in Bag II = 3 + 5 = 8
The probability of drawing a red ball from Bag II is,
{tex}P\left(\frac RB\right)=\frac{{}^3C_1}{{}^8C_1}=\frac38{/tex}
∴ P(R) = {tex}P\left(\frac RA\right)+P\left(\frac RB\right){/tex}​​​​​​​
P(R) = {tex}\frac14+\frac38=\frac58{/tex}​​​​​​​

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Three friends A, B and C got their photograph clicked. Find the probability that B is standing at the central position, given that A is standing at the left corner.
question_text: Three friends A, B and C got their photograph clicked. Find the probability that B is standing at the central position, given that A is standing at the left corner.
explanation: Let B be the event in which B is at the center and A be the event in which A is at the left. So, we need to find P(B/A) Total outcomes = ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA B = ABC, CBA n(B) = 2 A = ABC, ACB n(A) = 2 {tex}n(A\cap B)=1{/tex} Now, {tex}P\left(\frac BA\right)=\frac{n\left(A\cap B\right)}{n\left(A\right)}=\frac12{/tex} Hence, the probability that B is standing at Central position and A is standing at left position is {tex}\frac12{/tex}
solution: Let B be the event in which B is at the center and A be the event in which A is at the left. So, we need to find P(B/A) Total outcomes = ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA B = ABC, CBA n(B) = 2 A = ABC, ACB n(A) = 2 {tex}n(A\cap B)=1{/tex} Now, {tex}P\left(\frac BA\right)=\frac{n\left(A\cap B\right)}{n\left(A\right)}=\frac12{/tex} Hence, the probability that B is standing at Central position and A is standing at left position is {tex}\frac12{/tex}
solution_html:
Let B be the event in which B is at the center and A be the event in which A is at the left.
So, we need to find P(B/A)
Total outcomes = ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA
B = ABC, CBA n(B) = 2
A = ABC, ACB n(A) = 2
{tex}n(A\cap B)=1{/tex}
Now, {tex}P\left(\frac BA\right)=\frac{n\left(A\cap B\right)}{n\left(A\right)}=\frac12{/tex}
Hence, the probability that B is standing at Central position and A is standing at left position is {tex}\frac12{/tex}

question_html:
In a game of Archery, each ring of the Archery target is valued. The centremost ring is worth 10 points and rest of the rings are allotted points 9 to 1 in sequential order moving outwards.
Archer A is likely to earn 10 points with a probability of 0.8 and Archer B is likely the earn 10 points with a probability of 0.9.

If both of them hit the Archery target, then find the probability that


exactly one of them earns 10 points.
question_text: In a game of Archery, each ring of the Archery target is valued. The centremost ring is worth 10 points and rest of the rings are allotted points 9 to 1 in sequential order moving outwards. Archer A is likely to earn 10 points with a probability of 0.8 and Archer B is likely the earn 10 points with a probability of 0.9. If both of them hit the Archery target, then find the probability that exactly one of them earns 10 points.
explanation: Assume, that P(A) = probability of Archer A is likely to earn 10 points = 0.8 P(B) = probability of Archer B is likely to earn 10 points = 0.9 Now, for P ( {tex}\bar A{/tex} ) = 1 - P(A) = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 and P ( {tex}\bar B{/tex} ) = 1 - P(B) = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1 Now, the required probability for exactly one of them earns 10 points = P(A) {tex}\cdot{/tex} P( {tex}\bar B{/tex} ) + P( {tex}\bar A{/tex} ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} P(B) = 0.8 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 0.1 + 0.2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 0.9 = 0.08 + 0.18 = 0.26
solution: Assume, that P(A) = probability of Archer A is likely to earn 10 points = 0.8 P(B) = probability of Archer B is likely to earn 10 points = 0.9 Now, for P ( {tex}\bar A{/tex} ) = 1 - P(A) = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 and P ( {tex}\bar B{/tex} ) = 1 - P(B) = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1 Now, the required probability for exactly one of them earns 10 points = P(A) {tex}\cdot{/tex} P( {tex}\bar B{/tex} ) + P( {tex}\bar A{/tex} ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} P(B) = 0.8 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 0.1 + 0.2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 0.9 = 0.08 + 0.18 = 0.26
solution_html:
Assume, that
P(A) = probability of Archer A is likely to earn 10 points = 0.8
P(B) = probability of Archer B is likely to earn 10 points = 0.9
Now, for P ({tex}\bar A{/tex}) = 1 - P(A) = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 and P ({tex}\bar B{/tex}) = 1 - P(B) = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1
Now, the required probability for exactly one of them earns 10 points
= P(A){tex}\cdot{/tex}P({tex}\bar B{/tex}) + P({tex}\bar A{/tex}){tex}\cdot{/tex}P(B)
= 0.8{tex}\cdot{/tex}0.1 + 0.2{tex}\cdot{/tex}0.9
= 0.08 + 0.18
= 0.26

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
In a game of Archery, each ring of the Archery target is valued. The centremost ring is worth 10 points and rest of the rings are allotted points 9 to 1 in sequential order moving outwards.
Archer A is likely to earn 10 points with a probability of 0.8 and Archer B is likely the earn 10 points with a probability of 0.9.

If both of them hit the Archery target, then find the probability that


both of them earn 10 points.
question_text: In a game of Archery, each ring of the Archery target is valued. The centremost ring is worth 10 points and rest of the rings are allotted points 9 to 1 in sequential order moving outwards. Archer A is likely to earn 10 points with a probability of 0.8 and Archer B is likely the earn 10 points with a probability of 0.9. If both of them hit the Archery target, then find the probability that both of them earn 10 points.
explanation: Assume, that P(A) = probability of Archer A is likely to earn 10 points = 0.8 P(B) = probability of Archer B is likely to earn 10 points = 0.9 Now, for P( {tex}\bar A{/tex} ) = 1 - P(A) = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 and P( {tex}\bar B{/tex} ) = 1 - P(B) = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1 Now, the required probability for both of them earn 10 points, {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(A) {tex}\times{/tex} P(B) = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.9 = 0.72 Therefore, Required probability is 0.26
solution: Assume, that P(A) = probability of Archer A is likely to earn 10 points = 0.8 P(B) = probability of Archer B is likely to earn 10 points = 0.9 Now, for P( {tex}\bar A{/tex} ) = 1 - P(A) = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 and P( {tex}\bar B{/tex} ) = 1 - P(B) = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1 Now, the required probability for both of them earn 10 points, {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(A) {tex}\times{/tex} P(B) = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.9 = 0.72 Therefore, Required probability is 0.26
solution_html:
Assume, that
P(A) = probability of Archer A is likely to earn 10 points = 0.8
P(B) = probability of Archer B is likely to earn 10 points = 0.9
Now, for P({tex}\bar A{/tex}) = 1 - P(A) = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 and P({tex}\bar B{/tex}) = 1 - P(B) = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1
Now, the required probability for both of them earn 10 points,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(A) {tex}\times{/tex} P(B) = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.9 = 0.72
Therefore, Required probability is 0.26

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A biased die is tossed and respective probabilities for various faces to turn up are the following:

Face 1 2 3 4 5 6
Probability 0·1 0·24 0·19 0·18 0·15 K

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. What is the value of K? (2)
  2. If a face showing an even number has turned up, then what is the probability that it is the face with 2 or 4? (2)
question_text: A biased die is tossed and respective probabilities for various faces to turn up are the following: Face 1 2 3 4 5 6 Probability 0·1 0·24 0·19 0·18 0·15 K Based on the above information, answer the following questions: What is the value of K? (2) If a face showing an even number has turned up, then what is the probability that it is the face with 2 or 4? (2)
explanation: P(1) + P(2) + P(3) + P(4) + P(5) + P(6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.1 + 0.24 + 0.19 + 0.18 + 0.15 + K = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K = 0.14 A: face shows 2 or 4 B: even face have turned up Required probability = P (A/B) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})}{/tex} here P (A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(2) + P(4) = 0.24 + 0.18 = 0.42 P(B) = P(2) + P(4) + P(6) = 0.56 {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{0.42}{0.56}{/tex} = {tex}\frac34{/tex}
solution: P(1) + P(2) + P(3) + P(4) + P(5) + P(6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.1 + 0.24 + 0.19 + 0.18 + 0.15 + K = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K = 0.14 A: face shows 2 or 4 B: even face have turned up Required probability = P (A/B) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})}{/tex} here P (A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(2) + P(4) = 0.24 + 0.18 = 0.42 P(B) = P(2) + P(4) + P(6) = 0.56 {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{0.42}{0.56}{/tex} = {tex}\frac34{/tex}
solution_html:
1. P(1) + P(2) + P(3) + P(4) + P(5) + P(6)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.1 + 0.24 + 0.19 + 0.18 + 0.15 + K = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K = 0.14

  2. A: face shows 2 or 4
    B: even face have turned up
    Required probability = P (A/B) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})}{/tex}
    here P (A{tex}\cap{/tex}B) = P(2) + P(4)
    = 0.24 + 0.18 = 0.42
    P(B) = P(2) + P(4) + P(6) = 0.56
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{0.42}{0.56}{/tex} = {tex}\frac34{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A shopkeeper sells three types of flower seeds A1, A2, A3. They are sold in the form of a mixture, where the proportions of these seeds are 4 : 4 : 2, respectively. The germination rates of the three types of seeds are 45%, 60% and 35% respectively.

  1. Calculate the probability that a randomly chosen seed will germinate. (2)
  2. Calculate the probability that the seed is of type A2, given that a randomly chosen seed germinates. (2)
question_text: A shopkeeper sells three types of flower seeds A1, A2, A3. They are sold in the form of a mixture, where the proportions of these seeds are 4 : 4 : 2, respectively. The germination rates of the three types of seeds are 45%, 60% and 35% respectively. Calculate the probability that a randomly chosen seed will germinate. (2) Calculate the probability that the seed is of type A2, given that a randomly chosen seed germinates. (2)
explanation: We have, A 1 : A 2 : A 4 = 4 : 4 : 2 P(A1) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex} , P(A 2 ) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex} and P(A 3 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{10}{/tex} where A 1 , A 2 and A 3 denote the three types of flower seed. Let E be the event that a seed germinates and {tex}\bar {E}{/tex} be the event that a seed does not germinate. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {45} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {60} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {35} {100}{/tex} And {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {55} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {40} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {65} {100}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E) = P(A 1 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} + P(A 2 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} + P(A 3 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{1000}=\frac{490}{1000}{/tex} = 0.49 {tex}P (\frac {A_2} {E}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})}{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_1\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_1}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_3\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_3}})}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{10} \times \frac{60}{100}}{\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{490}{1000}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {24} {49}{/tex} = 0.48
solution: We have, A 1 : A 2 : A 4 = 4 : 4 : 2 P(A1) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex} , P(A 2 ) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex} and P(A 3 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{10}{/tex} where A 1 , A 2 and A 3 denote the three types of flower seed. Let E be the event that a seed germinates and {tex}\bar {E}{/tex} be the event that a seed does not germinate. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {45} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {60} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {35} {100}{/tex} And {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {55} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {40} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {65} {100}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E) = P(A 1 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} + P(A 2 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} + P(A 3 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{1000}=\frac{490}{1000}{/tex} = 0.49 {tex}P (\frac {A_2} {E}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})}{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_1\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_1}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_3\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_3}})}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{10} \times \frac{60}{100}}{\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{490}{1000}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {24} {49}{/tex} = 0.48
solution_html:
1. We have, A1 : A2 : A4 = 4 : 4 : 2
    P(A1) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex}, P(A2) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex} and P(A3) = {tex}\frac{2}{10}{/tex}
    where A1, A2 and A3 denote the three types of flower seed.
    Let E be the event that a seed germinates and {tex}\bar {E}{/tex} be the event that a seed does not germinate.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {45} {100}{/tex}, {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {60} {100}{/tex}, {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {35} {100}{/tex}
    And {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {55} {100}{/tex}, {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {40} {100}{/tex}, {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {65} {100}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E) = P(A1) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} + P(A2) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} + P(A3) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{1000}=\frac{490}{1000}{/tex} = 0.49

  2. {tex}P (\frac {A_2} {E}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})}{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_1\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_1}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_3\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_3}})}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{10} \times \frac{60}{100}}{\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{100}}{/tex} 
    = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{490}{1000}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {24} {49}{/tex} = 0.48

question_html: A coin is tossed once. If a head comes up, a die is thrown but if a tail comes up, the coin is tossed again. Find the probability of obtaining a head and the number 6.
question_text: A coin is tossed once. If a head comes up, a die is thrown but if a tail comes up, the coin is tossed again. Find the probability of obtaining a head and the number 6.
explanation: Given : let H be head, and T be tails where as 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 be the numbers on the dice which are thrown when a head comes up or else coin is tossed again if its tail. Clearly ,sample space S = {(TH),(TT) ,(H1), (H2), (H3), (H4), (H5), (H6)} We need to find the probability of obtaining a head and the number 6 From sample space, it is clear that the probability of obtaining a head and the number 6 is {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} i.e., {H6} with total no of elements in sample space as 8.
solution: Given : let H be head, and T be tails where as 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 be the numbers on the dice which are thrown when a head comes up or else coin is tossed again if its tail. Clearly ,sample space S = {(TH),(TT) ,(H1), (H2), (H3), (H4), (H5), (H6)} We need to find the probability of obtaining a head and the number 6 From sample space, it is clear that the probability of obtaining a head and the number 6 is {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} i.e., {H6} with total no of elements in sample space as 8.
solution_html:
Given : let H be head, and T be tails where as 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 be the numbers on the dice which are thrown when a head comes up or else coin is tossed again if its tail.
Clearly ,sample space S = {(TH),(TT) ,(H1), (H2), (H3), (H4), (H5), (H6)}
We need to find the probability of obtaining a head and the number 6
From sample space, it is clear that the probability of obtaining a head and the number 6 is {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} i.e., {H6} with total no of elements in sample space as 8.

question_html: If A and B are two independent events and P(A) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and P(B) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, find P{tex}(\overline{\mathrm{A}} \mid \overline{\mathrm{B}}){/tex}.
question_text: If A and B are two independent events and P(A) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and P(B) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , find P {tex}(\overline{\mathrm{A}} \mid \overline{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} .
explanation: A and B are independent {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\overline{\mathrm{A}}{/tex} and {tex}\overline{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} are independent {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}(\frac{\overline{\mathrm{A}}} { \overline{\mathrm{B}}}){/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{P}(\overline{\mathrm{A}}){/tex} = 1 - P(A) = 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution: A and B are independent {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\overline{\mathrm{A}}{/tex} and {tex}\overline{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} are independent {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}(\frac{\overline{\mathrm{A}}} { \overline{\mathrm{B}}}){/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{P}(\overline{\mathrm{A}}){/tex} = 1 - P(A) = 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
A and B are independent {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\overline{\mathrm{A}}{/tex} and {tex}\overline{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} are independent
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}(\frac{\overline{\mathrm{A}}} { \overline{\mathrm{B}}}){/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{P}(\overline{\mathrm{A}}){/tex} = 1 - P(A) = 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}

question_html:
Based on the given information, answer any four of the following five questions: 4{tex}\times{/tex}1=4 
In answering a multiple choice test for class XII, a student either knows or guesses or copies the answer to a multiple choice question with four choices. The probability that he makes a guess is 13 and the probability that he copies the answer is 16. The probability that his answer is correct given that he copied is 18.
Let E1, E2, E3 be the events that the student guesses, copies or knows the answer respectively and A is the event that the student answers correctly.

  1. What is the probability that the student knows the answer? (1)
    1. 1
    2. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

    1. 1
    2. 0
    3. {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}
  3. What is the probability that he answers correctly given that he had made a guess? (1)
    1. {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
    2. 0
    3. 1
    4. {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}

    2. {tex}\frac 13{/tex}

question_html: Given two independent events A and B such that P (A) = 0.3 and P (B) = 0.6 Find {tex}P(\bar{A} \cap \bar{B}){/tex}
question_text: Given two independent events A and B such that P (A) = 0.3 and P (B) = 0.6 Find {tex}P(\bar{A} \cap \bar{B}){/tex}
explanation: We are given that, P(A) = 0.3 P(B) = 0.6 Since A and B are independent events, therefore, {tex}P(\bar{A} \cap \bar B)=P(\bar{A}) P(\bar B){/tex} = [1 - P(A)][1 - P(B)] = {tex}0.7 \times 0.4{/tex} = {tex}0.28{/tex}
solution: We are given that, P(A) = 0.3 P(B) = 0.6 Since A and B are independent events, therefore, {tex}P(\bar{A} \cap \bar B)=P(\bar{A}) P(\bar B){/tex} = [1 - P(A)][1 - P(B)] = {tex}0.7 \times 0.4{/tex} = {tex}0.28{/tex}
solution_html:
We are given that,
P(A) = 0.3
P(B) = 0.6
Since A and B are independent events, therefore,
{tex}P(\bar{A} \cap \bar B)=P(\bar{A}) P(\bar B){/tex}
= [1 - P(A)][1 - P(B)]
= {tex}0.7 \times 0.4{/tex}
= {tex}0.28{/tex}

question_html: In a shop X, 30 tins of ghee of type A and 40 tins of ghee of type B which look alike, are kept for sale. While in shop Y, similar 50 tins of ghee of type A and 60 tins of ghee of type B are there. One tin of ghee is purchased from one of the randomly selected shop and is found to be of type B. Find the probability that it is purchased from shop Y.
question_text: In a shop X, 30 tins of ghee of type A and 40 tins of ghee of type B which look alike, are kept for sale. While in shop Y, similar 50 tins of ghee of type A and 60 tins of ghee of type B are there. One tin of ghee is purchased from one of the randomly selected shop and is found to be of type B. Find the probability that it is purchased from shop Y.
explanation: E 1 = Ghee purchased from shop X E 2 = Ghee purchased from shop Y A = Getting type B Ghee P(E 1 ) = P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}P\left(A / E_{1}\right)=\frac{40}{70}=\frac47{/tex} , {tex}P\left(A / E_{2}\right)=\frac{60}{110}=\frac 6{11}{/tex} P(E 2 /A) = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6}{11}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6}{11}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{7}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\frac{6}{22}}{\frac{42+44}{2 \times 11 \times 7}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{21}{43}{/tex}
solution: E 1 = Ghee purchased from shop X E 2 = Ghee purchased from shop Y A = Getting type B Ghee P(E 1 ) = P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}P\left(A / E_{1}\right)=\frac{40}{70}=\frac47{/tex} , {tex}P\left(A / E_{2}\right)=\frac{60}{110}=\frac 6{11}{/tex} P(E 2 /A) = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6}{11}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6}{11}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{7}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\frac{6}{22}}{\frac{42+44}{2 \times 11 \times 7}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{21}{43}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1 = Ghee purchased from shop X
E2 = Ghee purchased from shop Y
A = Getting type B Ghee
P(E1) = P(E2) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(A / E_{1}\right)=\frac{40}{70}=\frac47{/tex}, {tex}P\left(A / E_{2}\right)=\frac{60}{110}=\frac 6{11}{/tex}
P(E2/A) = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6}{11}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6}{11}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{7}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\frac{6}{22}}{\frac{42+44}{2 \times 11 \times 7}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{21}{43}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Two cards are drawn at random and one-by-one without replacement from a well-shuffled pack of 52 playing cards. Find the probability that one card is red and the other is black.
question_text: Two cards are drawn at random and one-by-one without replacement from a well-shuffled pack of 52 playing cards. Find the probability that one card is red and the other is black.
explanation: P(one red and one black) = P (first red and second black ) + P(first black and second red) = {tex}\frac{26}{52} \times \frac{26}{51}+\frac{26}{52} \times \frac{26}{51}{/tex} [because cards are drawn without replacement] = {tex}\frac{13}{51}+\frac{13}{51}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{26}{51}{/tex}
solution: P(one red and one black) = P (first red and second black ) + P(first black and second red) = {tex}\frac{26}{52} \times \frac{26}{51}+\frac{26}{52} \times \frac{26}{51}{/tex} [because cards are drawn without replacement] = {tex}\frac{13}{51}+\frac{13}{51}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{26}{51}{/tex}
solution_html:
P(one red and one black) = P (first red and second black ) + P(first black and second red)
= {tex}\frac{26}{52} \times \frac{26}{51}+\frac{26}{52} \times \frac{26}{51}{/tex} [because cards are drawn without replacement]
= {tex}\frac{13}{51}+\frac{13}{51}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{26}{51}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: From the set {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, two numbers a and b (a {tex}\neq{/tex} b) are chosen at random. The probability that {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex} is an integer is:
question_text: From the set {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, two numbers a and b (a {tex}\neq{/tex} b) are chosen at random. The probability that {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex} is an integer is:

options.explanation: The event happens if the two numbers a, b, (different from each other) should be chosen from the given set {1, 2, 3, 4, 5 } such that {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex} be an integer. The favorable cases may be enumerated as; {tex}\frac{2}{1}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{3}{1}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{4}{1}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{4}{2}{/tex} & {tex}\frac{5}{1}{/tex} which are 5 in numbers. And total number of ways of choosing two numbers, with order, out of 5 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} C(5, 2) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}\frac{5!}{2!}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3!) = 20. Hence the required probability = {tex}\frac{5}{20}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4} {/tex}

And total number of ways of choosing two numbers, with order, out of 5 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} C(5, 2) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}\frac{5!}{2!}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3!) = 20. Hence the required probability = {tex}\frac{5}{20}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If A and B are two independent events with P(A) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and P(B) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}, then P(B'|A) is equal to:
question_text: If A and B are two independent events with P(A) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and P(B) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} , then P(B'|A) is equal to:
explanation: {tex}P(B^{\prime}){/tex} = 1 - P(B) = 1 - {tex}\frac 14{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {B^{\prime}}A) = \frac {P(B^{\prime} \cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex} = {tex} \frac {P(B^{\prime})P(A)}{P(A)}{/tex} = {tex}P(B^{\prime}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A bag contains 3 white, 4 black and 2 red balls. If 2 balls are drawn at random (without replacement), then the probability that both the balls are white is:
question_text: A bag contains 3 white, 4 black and 2 red balls. If 2 balls are drawn at random (without replacement), then the probability that both the balls are white is:

options.explanation: Total balls = 3 + 4 + 2 = 9 n(S) = 9 C 2 n(E) = 3C 2 P(E) = {tex}\frac {n(E)}{n(S)} = \frac {3C_2}{9C_2} = \frac {\frac {3 \times 2}{2 \times 1}}{\frac {9 \times 8}{2 \times 1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {3 \times 2}{9 \times 8} = \frac 1{3 \times 4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{12}{/tex}

explanation: Total balls = 3 + 4 + 2 = 9 n(S) = 9 C 2 n(E) = 3C 2 P(E) = {tex}\frac {n(E)}{n(S)} = \frac {3C_2}{9C_2} = \frac {\frac {3 \times 2}{2 \times 1}}{\frac {9 \times 8}{2 \times 1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {3 \times 2}{9 \times 8} = \frac 1{3 \times 4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{12}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The probability of finding a green signal on a busy crossing X is 30%. What is the probability of finding a green signal on X on two consecutive days out of three?
question_text: The probability of finding a green signal on a busy crossing X is 30%. What is the probability of finding a green signal on X on two consecutive days out of three?
explanation: Probability of getting a green signal = 30% = {tex}30 \over 100{/tex} = {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} Probability of NOT getting a green signal = 1 - {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} = {tex}7 \over 10 {/tex} Now the probability of finding a green signal on X on two consecutive days out of three is possible if of day 1 and 2 the signal will green and on day three it will not green or on day 1 signal is not green but on day 2 and 3 it will green, Hence the required probability is, = ( {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}7 \over 10 {/tex} ) + ( {tex}7 \over 10 {/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} ) = {tex}63\over 1000{/tex} + {tex}63\over 1000{/tex} = {tex}63\over 500{/tex}
solution: Probability of getting a green signal = 30% = {tex}30 \over 100{/tex} = {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} Probability of NOT getting a green signal = 1 - {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} = {tex}7 \over 10 {/tex} Now the probability of finding a green signal on X on two consecutive days out of three is possible if of day 1 and 2 the signal will green and on day three it will not green or on day 1 signal is not green but on day 2 and 3 it will green, Hence the required probability is, = ( {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}7 \over 10 {/tex} ) + ( {tex}7 \over 10 {/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} ) = {tex}63\over 1000{/tex} + {tex}63\over 1000{/tex} = {tex}63\over 500{/tex}
solution_html:
Probability of getting a green signal = 30% = {tex}30 \over 100{/tex} = {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex}
Probability of NOT getting a green signal = 1 - {tex}3 \over 10 {/tex} = {tex}7 \over 10 {/tex}
Now the probability of finding a green signal on X on two consecutive days out of three is possible if of day 1 and 2 the signal will green and on day three it will not green or on day 1 signal is not green but on day 2 and 3 it will green,

Hence the required probability is,
= ({tex}3 \over 10 {/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} ({tex}3 \over 10 {/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} ({tex}7 \over 10 {/tex}) + ({tex}7 \over 10 {/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} ({tex}3 \over 10 {/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} ({tex}3 \over 10 {/tex}) = {tex}63\over 1000{/tex} + {tex}63\over 1000{/tex} = {tex}63\over 500{/tex}

question_text: A bag contains 3 black, 4 red and 2 green balls. If three balls are drawn simultaneously at random, then the probability that the balls are of different colours is ________.
explanation: Probability = {tex}\frac{{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1 \cdot{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1 \cdot{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1}{{ }^{9} \mathrm{C}_3}=\frac{2}{7}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Suppose that 5 men out of 100 and 25 women out of 1000 are good orators. Assuming that there are equal number of men and women, find the probability of choosing a good orator.
question_text: Suppose that 5 men out of 100 and 25 women out of 1000 are good orators. Assuming that there are equal number of men and women, find the probability of choosing a good orator.
explanation: Let A, E 1 and E 2 denote the events that the person is good orator, is man and is woman respectively.Therefote, we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now, we have, {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{1}})=\frac{5}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{25}{1000}{/tex} Using Bayes' theorem, we get Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{1}}{A}t)=\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{100}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{25}{1000}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{1+\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution: Let A, E 1 and E 2 denote the events that the person is good orator, is man and is woman respectively.Therefote, we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now, we have, {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{1}})=\frac{5}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{25}{1000}{/tex} Using Bayes' theorem, we get Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{1}}{A}t)=\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{100}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{25}{1000}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{1+\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A, E1 and E2 denote the events that the person is good orator, is man and is woman respectively.Therefote, we have,
P(E1) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
P(E2) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Now, we have,
{tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{1}})=\frac{5}{100}{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{25}{1000}{/tex}
Using Bayes' theorem, we get
Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{1}}{A}t)=\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{100}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{25}{1000}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{1+\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find [P({tex}\frac BA{/tex}) + P({tex}\frac AB{/tex})] if P(A) {tex}=\frac{3}{10},{/tex} {tex}P(B)=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} and {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}.
question_text: Find [P( {tex}\frac BA{/tex} ) + P( {tex}\frac AB{/tex} )] if P(A) {tex}=\frac{3}{10},{/tex} {tex}P(B)=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} and {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{3}{10}+\frac{2}{5}-\frac{3}{5}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex} Now, P(B|A) + P(A|B) {tex}=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}+\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{7}{12}{/tex}
solution: {tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{3}{10}+\frac{2}{5}-\frac{3}{5}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex} Now, P(B|A) + P(A|B) {tex}=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}+\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{7}{12}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{3}{10}+\frac{2}{5}-\frac{3}{5}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
Now, P(B|A) + P(A|B)
{tex}=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}+\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{7}{12}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A black and a red dice are rolled. Find the conditional probability of obtaining a sum greater than 9, given that the black die resulted in a 5.
question_text: A black and a red dice are rolled. Find the conditional probability of obtaining a sum greater than 9, given that the black die resulted in a 5.
explanation: Let the first observation be from the black die and second from the red die. When two dice (one black die and another red) are rolled, the sample space S has 6×6 = 36 number of elements. Let A : obtaining a sum greater than 9 = {(4,6),(5,5),(5,6),(6,4),(6,5),(6,6)} and B: black die resulted in a 5 = {(5,1),(5,2),(5,3),(5,4),(5,5),(5,6)} {tex}{/tex} {tex}\therefore A\cap B{/tex} = {(5,5),(5,6)} {tex}{/tex} The conditional probability of obtaining a sum greater than 9, given that the black die resulted in a 5, is given by P(A/B) {tex}\therefore P(A/B)=\frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}=\frac{\frac{2}{36}}{\frac{6}{36}}=\frac{2}{6}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution: Let the first observation be from the black die and second from the red die. When two dice (one black die and another red) are rolled, the sample space S has 6×6 = 36 number of elements. Let A : obtaining a sum greater than 9 = {(4,6),(5,5),(5,6),(6,4),(6,5),(6,6)} and B: black die resulted in a 5 = {(5,1),(5,2),(5,3),(5,4),(5,5),(5,6)} {tex}{/tex} {tex}\therefore A\cap B{/tex} = {(5,5),(5,6)} {tex}{/tex} The conditional probability of obtaining a sum greater than 9, given that the black die resulted in a 5, is given by P(A/B) {tex}\therefore P(A/B)=\frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}=\frac{\frac{2}{36}}{\frac{6}{36}}=\frac{2}{6}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the first observation be from the black die and second from the red die.
When two dice (one black die and another red) are rolled, the sample space S has 6×6 = 36 number of elements.
Let A : obtaining a sum greater than 9 = {(4,6),(5,5),(5,6),(6,4),(6,5),(6,6)}
and B: black die resulted in a 5 = {(5,1),(5,2),(5,3),(5,4),(5,5),(5,6)}
{tex}{/tex}{tex}\therefore A\cap B{/tex} = {(5,5),(5,6)}
{tex}{/tex}The conditional probability of obtaining a sum greater than 9, given that the black die resulted in a 5, is given by P(A/B)
{tex}\therefore P(A/B)=\frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}=\frac{\frac{2}{36}}{\frac{6}{36}}=\frac{2}{6}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A bag contains two coins, one biased and the other unbiased. When tossed, the biased coin has a 60% chance of showing heads. One of the coins is selected at random and on tossing it shows tails. What is the probability it was an unbiased coin?
question_text: A bag contains two coins, one biased and the other unbiased. When tossed, the biased coin has a 60% chance of showing heads. One of the coins is selected at random and on tossing it shows tails. What is the probability it was an unbiased coin?
explanation: Let E 1 be the event that unbiased coin is tossed. E 2 be the event that biased coin is tossed. A be the event that coin tossed shows tail P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} , {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{E_1}}{\mathrm A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{5}}=\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 be the event that unbiased coin is tossed. E 2 be the event that biased coin is tossed. A be the event that coin tossed shows tail P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} , {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{E_1}}{\mathrm A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{5}}=\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 be the event that unbiased coin is tossed.
E2 be the event that biased coin is tossed.
A be the event that coin tossed shows tail
P(E1) = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)=\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{E_1}}{\mathrm A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_2\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{5}}=\frac{5}{9}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: There are two bags, I and II, Bag I contains 3 red and 5 black balls and Bag II contains 4 red and 3 black balls. One ball is transferred randomly from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn randomly from Bag II. If the ball so drawn is found to be black in colour, then find the probability that the transferred ball is also black.
question_text: There are two bags, I and II, Bag I contains 3 red and 5 black balls and Bag II contains 4 red and 3 black balls. One ball is transferred randomly from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn randomly from Bag II. If the ball so drawn is found to be black in colour, then find the probability that the transferred ball is also black.
explanation: E1 = Event that the ball transfered from Bag I is Black E2 = Event that the ball transfered from Bag I is Red A = Event that the ball drawn from Bag II is Black P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac 58{/tex} ; P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac 38{/tex} ; {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right)=\frac{4}{8}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ; {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)=\frac{3}{8}{/tex} {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{1}{2}+\frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{3}{8}}=\frac{20}{29}{/tex}
solution: E1 = Event that the ball transfered from Bag I is Black E2 = Event that the ball transfered from Bag I is Red A = Event that the ball drawn from Bag II is Black P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac 58{/tex} ; P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac 38{/tex} ; {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right)=\frac{4}{8}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ; {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)=\frac{3}{8}{/tex} {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{1}{2}+\frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{3}{8}}=\frac{20}{29}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1 = Event that the ball transfered from Bag I is Black
E2 = Event that the ball transfered from Bag I is Red
A = Event that the ball drawn from Bag II is Black
P(E1) = {tex}\frac 58{/tex}; P(E2) = {tex}\frac 38{/tex}; {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text A}{\text E_1}\right)=\frac{4}{8}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}; {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)=\frac{3}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{1}{2}+\frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{3}{8}}=\frac{20}{29}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A and B throw a pair of dice alternately till one of them gets the sum of the numbers as multiples of 6 and wins the game. If A starts first, find the probability of B winning the game.
question_text: A and B throw a pair of dice alternately till one of them gets the sum of the numbers as multiples of 6 and wins the game. If A starts first, find the probability of B winning the game.
explanation: Let S denote Sum of numbers as multiples of six P(S) = {tex}\frac {6}{36}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {1}{6}{/tex} , {tex}\text P(\bar {\text S}) {/tex} = {tex}\frac {5}{6}{/tex} P(B winning) = {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}) {/tex} P(B) + {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}) {/tex} {tex}\text P(\bar {\text B}) {/tex} {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}) {/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} P(B) + ... = {tex}\frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{6}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{6}{/tex} + ... = {tex}\frac {5}{11}{/tex}
solution: Let S denote Sum of numbers as multiples of six P(S) = {tex}\frac {6}{36}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {1}{6}{/tex} , {tex}\text P(\bar {\text S}) {/tex} = {tex}\frac {5}{6}{/tex} P(B winning) = {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}) {/tex} P(B) + {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}) {/tex} {tex}\text P(\bar {\text B}) {/tex} {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}) {/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} P(B) + ... = {tex}\frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{6}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{6}{/tex} + ... = {tex}\frac {5}{11}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let S denote Sum of numbers as multiples of six
P(S) = {tex}\frac {6}{36}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {1}{6}{/tex}, {tex}\text P(\bar {\text S}) {/tex} = {tex}\frac {5}{6}{/tex}
P(B winning) = {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}) {/tex}P(B) + {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}) {/tex}{tex}\text P(\bar {\text B}) {/tex}{tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}) {/tex}{tex}\cdot{/tex}P(B) + ...
= {tex}\frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{6}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{6}{/tex} + ...
= {tex}\frac {5}{11}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A card from a pack of 52 cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, two cards are drawn randomly one-by-one without replacement and are found to be both kings. Find the probability of the lost card being a king.
question_text: A card from a pack of 52 cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, two cards are drawn randomly one-by-one without replacement and are found to be both kings. Find the probability of the lost card being a king.
explanation: Let E 1 : Lost card is king Let E 2 : Lost card is not a king A: Two cards drawn are kings P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac {1}{13}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac {12}{13}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)=\frac{{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}{/tex} , {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)=\frac{{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}{/tex} {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text E_1}{\text A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text E_1}\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text E_1}\right)+\text P\left(\text E_2\right) \text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text E_2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}}{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}+\frac{12}{13} \times \frac{{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{75}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{25}{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 : Lost card is king Let E 2 : Lost card is not a king A: Two cards drawn are kings P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac {1}{13}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac {12}{13}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)=\frac{{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}{/tex} , {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)=\frac{{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}{/tex} {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text E_1}{\text A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text E_1}\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text E_1}\right)+\text P\left(\text E_2\right) \text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text E_2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}}{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}+\frac{12}{13} \times \frac{{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{75}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{25}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1: Lost card is king
Let E2: Lost card is not a king
A: Two cards drawn are kings
P(E1) = {tex}\frac {1}{13}{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex}\frac {12}{13}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_1}\right)=\frac{{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}{/tex}, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac {\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{E}_2}\right)=\frac{{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}{/tex}
{tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text E_1}{\text A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text E_1}\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text E_1}\right)+\text P\left(\text E_2\right) \text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text E_2}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}}{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}+\frac{12}{13} \times \frac{{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^{51} \mathrm{C}_2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{75}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{25}{/tex}
