subject: Mathematics
question_html: A purse contains 3 silver and 6 copper coins and a second purse contains 4 silver and 3 copper coins. If a coin is drawn at random from one of the two purses, find the probability that it is a silver coin.
question_text: A purse contains 3 silver and 6 copper coins and a second purse contains 4 silver and 3 copper coins. If a coin is drawn at random from one of the two purses, find the probability that it is a silver coin.
explanation: {tex}E_1{/tex} : Event of coin drawn from purse 1 {tex}{E}_2{/tex} : Event of coin drawn from purse 2 A : Silver coin is drawn {tex} {P}\left({E}_1\right)=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left({E}_2\right)=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(\frac{{~A}}{{E}_1}\right)=\frac{3}{9} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(\frac{{~A}}{{E}_2}\right)=\frac{4}{7} {/tex} {tex} {P}({~A})=P\left(E_1\right) \cdot p\left(\frac{A}{E_1}\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot p\left(\frac{A}{E_2}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{9}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{7} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{21+36}{126}=\frac{57}{126} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{19}{42} {/tex}
solution: {tex}E_1{/tex} : Event of coin drawn from purse 1 {tex}{E}_2{/tex} : Event of coin drawn from purse 2 A : Silver coin is drawn {tex} {P}\left({E}_1\right)=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left({E}_2\right)=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(\frac{{~A}}{{E}_1}\right)=\frac{3}{9} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(\frac{{~A}}{{E}_2}\right)=\frac{4}{7} {/tex} {tex} {P}({~A})=P\left(E_1\right) \cdot p\left(\frac{A}{E_1}\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot p\left(\frac{A}{E_2}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{9}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{7} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{21+36}{126}=\frac{57}{126} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{19}{42} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}E_1{/tex} : Event of coin drawn from purse 1
{tex}{E}_2{/tex} : Event of coin drawn from purse 2
A : Silver coin is drawn
{tex} {P}\left({E}_1\right)=\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
{tex} {P}\left({E}_2\right)=\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
{tex} {P}\left(\frac{{~A}}{{E}_1}\right)=\frac{3}{9} {/tex}
{tex} {P}\left(\frac{{~A}}{{E}_2}\right)=\frac{4}{7} {/tex}
{tex} {P}({~A})=P\left(E_1\right) \cdot p\left(\frac{A}{E_1}\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot p\left(\frac{A}{E_2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{9}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{7} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{21+36}{126}=\frac{57}{126} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{19}{42} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: There are two bags, I and II. The bag I contain 3 red and 5 black balls and Bag II contains 4 red and 3 black balls. One ball is transferred randomly from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn randomly from Bag II. If the ball so drawn is found to be black in colour, then find the probability that the transferred ball is also black.
question_text: There are two bags, I and II. The bag I contain 3 red and 5 black balls and Bag II contains 4 red and 3 black balls. One ball is transferred randomly from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn randomly from Bag II. If the ball so drawn is found to be black in colour, then find the probability that the transferred ball is also black.
explanation: {tex}E_1={/tex} Ball transferred from Bug 1 to Bay 2 is Red {tex}E_2{/tex} : Ball transferred from B1 to B2 is Black A: Ball drawn from Bag to Bag 2 is Black {tex} P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{3}{8} P\left(E_2\right)=\frac{5}{8}{/tex} Now {tex}B_2{/tex} Contains Total 8 balls. {tex} P\left(A \mid E_1\right)=\frac{3}{8} P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{4}{8}{/tex} Required prob. {tex} \text { wired prob. } P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right) {/tex} {tex} P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{4}{8}}{\frac{3}{8} \times \frac{3}{8}+\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{4}{8}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{20}{9+20}=\frac{20}{29} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{12}{35} {/tex}
solution: {tex}E_1={/tex} Ball transferred from Bug 1 to Bay 2 is Red {tex}E_2{/tex} : Ball transferred from B1 to B2 is Black A: Ball drawn from Bag to Bag 2 is Black {tex} P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{3}{8} P\left(E_2\right)=\frac{5}{8}{/tex} Now {tex}B_2{/tex} Contains Total 8 balls. {tex} P\left(A \mid E_1\right)=\frac{3}{8} P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{4}{8}{/tex} Required prob. {tex} \text { wired prob. } P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right) {/tex} {tex} P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{4}{8}}{\frac{3}{8} \times \frac{3}{8}+\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{4}{8}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{20}{9+20}=\frac{20}{29} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{12}{35} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}E_1={/tex} Ball transferred from Bug 1 to Bay 2 is Red
{tex}E_2{/tex} : Ball transferred from B1 to B2 is Black
A: Ball drawn from Bag to Bag 2 is Black
{tex} P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{3}{8} P\left(E_2\right)=\frac{5}{8}{/tex}
Now {tex}B_2{/tex} Contains Total 8 balls.
{tex} P\left(A \mid E_1\right)=\frac{3}{8} P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{4}{8}{/tex}
Required prob.
{tex} \text { wired prob. } P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right) {/tex}
{tex} P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right) {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{4}{8}}{\frac{3}{8} \times \frac{3}{8}+\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{4}{8}} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{20}{9+20}=\frac{20}{29} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{12}{35} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: There are three coins. One is a two headed coin (having head on both faces), another is a biased coin that comes up heads 75% of the time and third is an unbiased coin. One of the three coins is chosen at random and tossed, it shows heads, what is the probability that it was the two headed coin?
question_text: There are three coins. One is a two headed coin (having head on both faces), another is a biased coin that comes up heads 75% of the time and third is an unbiased coin. One of the three coins is chosen at random and tossed, it shows heads, what is the probability that it was the two headed coin?
explanation: Let E 1 : Two headed coin, E 2 : Biased coin and E 3 : Unbiased coin. Let A : Coin shows head. Then P (E 1 ) = P (E 2 ) = P (E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} As the two-headed coin has head on both sides so it will show head only. Also P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_1}{/tex} ) = P (correct answer given that he knows) = 1 And P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_2}{/tex} ) = P (coin shows head given that the coin is biased) = 75% = {tex}\frac{75}{100}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} And P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_3}{/tex} ) = P (coin shows head given that the coin is unbiased) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now the probability that the coin is two headed, being given that it shows head, is P( {tex}\frac{E_1}{A}{/tex} ). By using Bayes’ theorem, we have: {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1} | \mathrm{A}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{3}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1}{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{3}\left(1+\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{9}}{\frac{9}{4}}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} P( {tex}\frac{E_1}{A}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 : Two headed coin, E 2 : Biased coin and E 3 : Unbiased coin. Let A : Coin shows head. Then P (E 1 ) = P (E 2 ) = P (E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} As the two-headed coin has head on both sides so it will show head only. Also P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_1}{/tex} ) = P (correct answer given that he knows) = 1 And P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_2}{/tex} ) = P (coin shows head given that the coin is biased) = 75% = {tex}\frac{75}{100}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} And P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_3}{/tex} ) = P (coin shows head given that the coin is unbiased) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now the probability that the coin is two headed, being given that it shows head, is P( {tex}\frac{E_1}{A}{/tex} ). By using Bayes’ theorem, we have: {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1} | \mathrm{A}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{3}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1}{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{3}\left(1+\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{9}}{\frac{9}{4}}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} P( {tex}\frac{E_1}{A}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 : Two headed coin,
E2 : Biased coin
and E3 : Unbiased coin.
Let A : Coin shows head.
Then P (E1) = P (E2) = P (E3) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
As the two-headed coin has head on both sides so it will show head only.
Also P({tex}\frac{A}{E_1}{/tex}) = P (correct answer given that he knows) = 1
And P({tex}\frac{A}{E_2}{/tex}) = P (coin shows head given that the coin is biased) = 75% = {tex}\frac{75}{100}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} 
And P({tex}\frac{A}{E_3}{/tex}) = P (coin shows head given that the coin is unbiased) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
Now the probability that the coin is two headed, being given that it shows head, is P({tex}\frac{E_1}{A}{/tex}).
By using Bayes’ theorem, we have:
{tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1} | \mathrm{A}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{3}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1}{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{3}\left(1+\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{9}}{\frac{9}{4}}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore {/tex} P({tex}\frac{E_1}{A}{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A die marked 1, 2, 3 in red and 4, 5, 6 in green is tossed. Let A be the event number is even and B be the event number is marked red. Find whether the events A and B are independent or not.
question_text: A die marked 1, 2, 3 in red and 4, 5, 6 in green is tossed. Let A be the event number is even and B be the event number is marked red . Find whether the events A and B are independent or not.
explanation: When a die is thrown, the sample space is S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n(S) = 6 Also, A : number is even and B : number Is red {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {2, 4, 6} and B = {1, 2, 3} and A {tex}\cap{/tex} B = {2} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n(A) = 3, n (B) = 3 and n(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = 1 Now, P(A) = {tex}\frac { n ( A ) } { n ( S ) } = \frac { 3 } { 6 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} P(B) = {tex}\frac { n ( B ) } { n ( S ) } = \frac { 3 } { 6 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} and P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = {tex}\frac { n ( A \cap B ) } { n ( S ) } = \frac { 1 } { 6 }{/tex} Now, P(A) {tex}\times{/tex} P(B) = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \neq \frac { 1 } { 6 } = P ( A \cap B ){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad P ( A \cap B ) \neq P ( A ) \times P ( B ){/tex} Thus, A and B are not independent events.
solution: When a die is thrown, the sample space is S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n(S) = 6 Also, A : number is even and B : number Is red {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {2, 4, 6} and B = {1, 2, 3} and A {tex}\cap{/tex} B = {2} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n(A) = 3, n (B) = 3 and n(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = 1 Now, P(A) = {tex}\frac { n ( A ) } { n ( S ) } = \frac { 3 } { 6 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} P(B) = {tex}\frac { n ( B ) } { n ( S ) } = \frac { 3 } { 6 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} and P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = {tex}\frac { n ( A \cap B ) } { n ( S ) } = \frac { 1 } { 6 }{/tex} Now, P(A) {tex}\times{/tex} P(B) = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \neq \frac { 1 } { 6 } = P ( A \cap B ){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad P ( A \cap B ) \neq P ( A ) \times P ( B ){/tex} Thus, A and B are not independent events.
solution_html:
When a die is thrown, the sample space is
S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n(S) = 6
Also, A : number is even and B : number Is red
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {2, 4, 6} and B = {1, 2, 3} and A{tex}\cap{/tex}B = {2}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n(A) = 3, n (B) = 3 and n(A{tex}\cap{/tex}B) = 1
Now, P(A) = {tex}\frac { n ( A ) } { n ( S ) } = \frac { 3 } { 6 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}     
P(B) ={tex}\frac { n ( B ) } { n ( S ) } = \frac { 3 } { 6 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}     
and P(A{tex}\cap{/tex}B) = {tex}\frac { n ( A \cap B ) } { n ( S ) } = \frac { 1 } { 6 }{/tex} 
Now, P(A) {tex}\times{/tex} P(B) = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
 {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \neq \frac { 1 } { 6 } = P ( A \cap B ){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad P ( A \cap B ) \neq P ( A ) \times P ( B ){/tex}
Thus, A and B are not independent events.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A manufacturer has three machine operators A, B and C. The first operator A produces 1% defective items, where as the other two operators B and C produce 5% and 7% defective items respectively. A is on the job for 50% of the time, B is on the job for 30% of the time and C on the job for 20% of the time. A defective item is produced, what is the probability that it was produced by A?
question_text: A manufacturer has three machine operators A, B and C. The first operator A produces 1% defective items, where as the other two operators B and C produce 5% and 7% defective items respectively. A is on the job for 50% of the time, B is on the job for 30% of the time and C on the job for 20% of the time. A defective item is produced, what is the probability that it was produced by A?
explanation: Let E 1 = the item is manufactured by the operator A, E 2 = the item is manufactured by the operator B, E 3 = the item is manufactured by the operator C and A = the item is defective Now P (E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{{50}}{{100}}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{{30}}{{100}}{/tex} , P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{{20}}{{100}}{/tex} Now {tex}P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right){/tex} = P(item drawn is manufactured by operator A) = {tex}\frac{1}{{100}}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{{100}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A|{E_3}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{{100}}{/tex} Now Required probability = Probability that the item is manufactured by operator. A given that the item drawn is defective {tex}P(\frac {{E_1}} {A}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right)}}{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right) + P\left( {{E_2}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right) + P\left( {{E_3}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_3}} \right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\frac{{50}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{50}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}} + \frac{{30}}{{100}} \times \frac{5}{{100}} \times \frac{7}{{100}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{50}}{{50 + 150 + 140}} = \frac{5}{{34}}{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 = the item is manufactured by the operator A, E 2 = the item is manufactured by the operator B, E 3 = the item is manufactured by the operator C and A = the item is defective Now P (E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{{50}}{{100}}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{{30}}{{100}}{/tex} , P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{{20}}{{100}}{/tex} Now {tex}P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right){/tex} = P(item drawn is manufactured by operator A) = {tex}\frac{1}{{100}}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{{100}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A|{E_3}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{{100}}{/tex} Now Required probability = Probability that the item is manufactured by operator. A given that the item drawn is defective {tex}P(\frac {{E_1}} {A}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right)}}{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right) + P\left( {{E_2}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right) + P\left( {{E_3}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_3}} \right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\frac{{50}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{50}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}} + \frac{{30}}{{100}} \times \frac{5}{{100}} \times \frac{7}{{100}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{50}}{{50 + 150 + 140}} = \frac{5}{{34}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 = the item is manufactured by the operator A, E2 = the item is manufactured by the operator B, E3 = the item is manufactured by the operator C and A = the item is defective
Now P (E1) = {tex}\frac{{50}}{{100}}{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex}\frac{{30}}{{100}}{/tex}, P(E3) = {tex}\frac{{20}}{{100}}{/tex}
Now {tex}P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right){/tex} = P(item drawn is manufactured by operator A) = {tex}\frac{1}{{100}}{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{{100}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A|{E_3}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{{100}}{/tex}
Now Required probability = Probability that the item is manufactured by operator. A given that the item drawn is defective
{tex}P(\frac {{E_1}} {A}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right)}}{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right) + P\left( {{E_2}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right) + P\left( {{E_3}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_3}} \right)}}{/tex}

= {tex}\frac{{\frac{{50}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{50}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}} + \frac{{30}}{{100}} \times \frac{5}{{100}} \times \frac{7}{{100}}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{{50}}{{50 + 150 + 140}} = \frac{5}{{34}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: There are three coins. One is a two headed coin (having head on both faces), another is a biased coin that comes up heads 75% of the time and third is an unbiased coin. One of the three coins is chosen at random and tossed, it shows heads, what is the probability that it was the two headed coin?
question_text: There are three coins. One is a two headed coin (having head on both faces), another is a biased coin that comes up heads 75% of the time and third is an unbiased coin. One of the three coins is chosen at random and tossed, it shows heads, what is the probability that it was the two headed coin?

options.explanation: Let {tex}{{E}_{1}} , {{E}_{2}}{/tex} and {tex}{{E}_{3}}{/tex} and are events of selection of a two headed coin, biased coin and unbiased coin respectively. {tex}\therefore P({{E}_{1}})=P({{E}_{2}})=P({{E}_{2}})=\frac{1}{3}.{/tex} Let A = event of getting head. {tex}P(A/{{E}_{1}})=1,P(A/{{E}_{2}})=\frac{3}{4},P(A/{{E}_{3}})=\frac{1}{2}.{/tex} {tex}P({{E}_{1}}/A){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P(A/{{E}_{1}}).P({{E}_{1}})}{P(A/{{E}_{1}}).P({{E}_{1}})+P(A/{{E}_{2}}).P({{E}_{2}})+P(A/{{E}_{3}}).P({{E}_{3}})}{/tex} = {tex} \frac{\frac{1}{3}.1}{\frac{1}{3}.1+\frac{1}{3}.\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{3}\frac{1}{2}}\\{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}

explanation: Let {tex}{{E}_{1}} , {{E}_{2}}{/tex} and {tex}{{E}_{3}}{/tex} and are events of selection of a two headed coin, biased coin and unbiased coin respectively. {tex}\therefore P({{E}_{1}})=P({{E}_{2}})=P({{E}_{2}})=\frac{1}{3}.{/tex} Let A = event of getting head. {tex}P(A/{{E}_{1}})=1,P(A/{{E}_{2}})=\frac{3}{4},P(A/{{E}_{3}})=\frac{1}{2}.{/tex} {tex}P({{E}_{1}}/A){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P(A/{{E}_{1}}).P({{E}_{1}})}{P(A/{{E}_{1}}).P({{E}_{1}})+P(A/{{E}_{2}}).P({{E}_{2}})+P(A/{{E}_{3}}).P({{E}_{3}})}{/tex} = {tex} \frac{\frac{1}{3}.1}{\frac{1}{3}.1+\frac{1}{3}.\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{3}\frac{1}{2}}\\{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Mother, father and son line up at random for a family photo. If A and B are two events given by A = Son on one end, B = Father in the middle, find P(B/A).
question_text: Mother, father and son line up at random for a family photo. If A and B are two events given by A = Son on one end, B = Father in the middle, find P(B/A).
explanation: If mother (M), father (F), and son (S) line up the family picture, then the sample space will be S = {(S, F, M), (S, M, F), (M, F, S), (F, M, S), (F, S, M), (M, S, F)} A = {(S, F, M), (S, M, F), (M, F, S), (F, M, S)} B = {(S, F, M), (M, F, S)} {tex}\therefore A \cap B{/tex} = {(M, F, S), (S, F, M)} Total number of possible arrangements = 6 {tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B} / \mathrm{A})=\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B} \cap \mathrm{A})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 / 6}{4 / 6}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution: If mother (M), father (F), and son (S) line up the family picture, then the sample space will be S = {(S, F, M), (S, M, F), (M, F, S), (F, M, S), (F, S, M), (M, S, F)} A = {(S, F, M), (S, M, F), (M, F, S), (F, M, S)} B = {(S, F, M), (M, F, S)} {tex}\therefore A \cap B{/tex} = {(M, F, S), (S, F, M)} Total number of possible arrangements = 6 {tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B} / \mathrm{A})=\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B} \cap \mathrm{A})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 / 6}{4 / 6}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
If mother (M), father (F), and son (S) line up the family picture, then the sample space will be
S = {(S, F, M), (S, M, F), (M, F, S), (F, M, S), (F, S, M), (M, S, F)}
A = {(S, F, M), (S, M, F), (M, F, S), (F, M, S)}
B = {(S, F, M), (M, F, S)}
{tex}\therefore A \cap B{/tex} = {(M, F, S), (S, F, M)}
Total number of possible arrangements = 6
{tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B} / \mathrm{A})=\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B} \cap \mathrm{A})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 / 6}{4 / 6}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: In answering a question on a multiple choice test ,a student either knows the answer or guesses. Let {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} be the probability that he knows the answer and ¼ be the probability he guesses. Assuming that a student who guesses at the answer will be correct with probability {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}. What is the probability that the student knows the answer given that he answered it correctly.
question_text: In answering a question on a multiple choice test ,a student either knows the answer or guesses. Let {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} be the probability that he knows the answer and ¼ be the probability he guesses. Assuming that a student who guesses at the answer will be correct with probability {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} . What is the probability that the student knows the answer given that he answered it correctly.
explanation: Let E1 : the student knows the answer.Then, {tex}P({E_1}) = \frac{3}{4}{/tex} E 2 : the student guesses the answer.Then, {tex}P({E_2}) = \frac{1}{4}{/tex} A: the answer is correct P (A/E 1 ) = 1, P (A/E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{A}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1 \times \frac{3}{4}}}{{1 \times \frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}}} = \frac{{12}}{{13}}{/tex}
solution: Let E1 : the student knows the answer.Then, {tex}P({E_1}) = \frac{3}{4}{/tex} E 2 : the student guesses the answer.Then, {tex}P({E_2}) = \frac{1}{4}{/tex} A: the answer is correct P (A/E 1 ) = 1, P (A/E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{A}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1 \times \frac{3}{4}}}{{1 \times \frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}}} = \frac{{12}}{{13}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 : the student knows the answer.Then, {tex}P({E_1}) = \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
E2 : the student guesses the answer.Then, {tex}P({E_2}) = \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
A: the answer is correct
P (A/E1) = 1, P (A/E2) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{A}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{1 \times \frac{3}{4}}}{{1 \times \frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}}} = \frac{{12}}{{13}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If P(not A) = 0·7, P(B) = 0·7 and P(B/A) = 0·5, then find P(A/B).
question_text: If P(not A) = 0·7, P(B) = 0·7 and P(B/A) = 0·5, then find P(A/B).
explanation: Given, P( {tex}\overline A{/tex} ) = 0.7 P(B) = 0.7 and P(B/A) = 0.5 We know that {tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B} | \mathrm{A})=\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})}{/tex} {tex}0.5=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{0.3}{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} P( {tex}\overline A{/tex} )=0.7 then P(A)=1 - 0.7 = 0.3] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P(A \cap B)=0.15{/tex} Now, {tex}P(A | B)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}=\frac{0.15}{0.7}=\frac{15}{70}=\frac{3}{14}{/tex}
solution: Given, P( {tex}\overline A{/tex} ) = 0.7 P(B) = 0.7 and P(B/A) = 0.5 We know that {tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B} | \mathrm{A})=\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})}{/tex} {tex}0.5=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{0.3}{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} P( {tex}\overline A{/tex} )=0.7 then P(A)=1 - 0.7 = 0.3] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P(A \cap B)=0.15{/tex} Now, {tex}P(A | B)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}=\frac{0.15}{0.7}=\frac{15}{70}=\frac{3}{14}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, P({tex}\overline A{/tex}) = 0.7
P(B) = 0.7 and P(B/A) = 0.5
We know that
{tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B} | \mathrm{A})=\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})}{/tex}
{tex}0.5=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{0.3}{/tex}
[{tex}\because {/tex} P({tex}\overline A{/tex})=0.7 then P(A)=1 - 0.7 = 0.3]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad P(A \cap B)=0.15{/tex}
Now,
{tex}P(A | B)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}=\frac{0.15}{0.7}=\frac{15}{70}=\frac{3}{14}{/tex}
