question_html: 12 cards numbered 1 to 12 (one number on one card), are placed in a box and mixed up thoroughly. Then a card is drawn at random from the box. If it is known that the number on the drawn card is greater than 5, find the probability that the card bears an odd number.
question_text: 12 cards numbered 1 to 12 (one number on one card), are placed in a box and mixed up thoroughly. Then a card is drawn at random from the box. If it is known that the number on the drawn card is greater than 5, find the probability that the card bears an odd number.
explanation: Consider the following events: A: card bears odd number B: Number on the cards is greater than 5 {tex}A \cap B{/tex} = {7, 9, 11} {tex}P(A | B){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 / 12}{7 /1 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex}
solution: Consider the following events: A: card bears odd number B: Number on the cards is greater than 5 {tex}A \cap B{/tex} = {7, 9, 11} {tex}P(A | B){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 / 12}{7 /1 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider the following events:

A: card bears odd number
B: Number on the cards is greater than 5
{tex}A \cap B{/tex} = {7, 9, 11}
{tex}P(A | B){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 / 12}{7 /1 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: An insurance company insured 3000 cyclists, 6000 scooter drivers and 9000 car drivers. The probability of an accident involving a cyclist, a scooter driver and a car driver are 0·3, 0·05 and 0·02 respectively. One of the insured persons meets with an accident. What is the probability that he is a cyclist?
question_text: An insurance company insured 3000 cyclists, 6000 scooter drivers and 9000 car drivers. The probability of an accident involving a cyclist, a scooter driver and a car driver are 0·3, 0·05 and 0·02 respectively. One of the insured persons meets with an accident. What is the probability that he is a cyclist?
explanation: E 1 : Selected person is cyclist E 2 : Selected person is scooter driver E 3 : Selected person is car driver A : insured person met with an accident P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{3}{18}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{6}{18}{/tex} , P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{9}{18}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right){/tex} = 0.3, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right){/tex} = 0.05, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right){/tex} = 0.02 {tex}P\left(E_{1} / A\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{3}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{3}{18} \times \frac{30}{100}}{\frac{3}{18} \times \frac{30}{100}+\frac{6}{18} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{9}{18} \times \frac{2}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{90}{138}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15}{23}{/tex} Required Probability = {tex}\frac{15}{23}{/tex}
solution: E 1 : Selected person is cyclist E 2 : Selected person is scooter driver E 3 : Selected person is car driver A : insured person met with an accident P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{3}{18}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{6}{18}{/tex} , P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{9}{18}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right){/tex} = 0.3, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right){/tex} = 0.05, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right){/tex} = 0.02 {tex}P\left(E_{1} / A\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{3}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{3}{18} \times \frac{30}{100}}{\frac{3}{18} \times \frac{30}{100}+\frac{6}{18} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{9}{18} \times \frac{2}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{90}{138}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15}{23}{/tex} Required Probability = {tex}\frac{15}{23}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1 : Selected person is cyclist
E2 : Selected person is scooter driver
E3 : Selected person is car driver
A : insured person met with an accident

P(E1) = {tex}\frac{3}{18}{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex}\frac{6}{18}{/tex}, P(E3) = {tex}\frac{9}{18}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right){/tex} = 0.3, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right){/tex} = 0.05, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right){/tex} = 0.02
{tex}P\left(E_{1} / A\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{3}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{3}{18} \times \frac{30}{100}}{\frac{3}{18} \times \frac{30}{100}+\frac{6}{18} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{9}{18} \times \frac{2}{100}}{/tex}

= {tex}\frac{90}{138}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15}{23}{/tex}

Required Probability = {tex}\frac{15}{23}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: There are two boxes I and II. Box I contains 3 red and 6 black balls. Box II contains 5 red and n black balls. One of the two boxes, box I and box II is selected at random and a ball is drawn at random. The ball drawn is found to be red. If the probability that this red ball comes out from box II is {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex}, find the value of n.
question_text: There are two boxes I and II. Box I contains 3 red and 6 black balls. Box II contains 5 red and n black balls. One of the two boxes, box I and box II is selected at random and a ball is drawn at random. The ball drawn is found to be red. If the probability that this red ball comes out from box II is {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex} , find the value of n .
explanation: Let the events be E 1 : bag I is elected E 2 : bag II is selected A: getting a red ball P(E 1 ) = P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} P(A/E 1 ) {tex}=\frac{3}{9}=\frac{1}{3};{/tex} P(A/E) {tex}=\frac{5}{5+n}{/tex} We have, {tex}P\left(E_{2} / A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A /E_{2}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)}{/tex} [Bayes Theorem] P(E 2 /A) {tex}=\frac{3}{5}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{5+n}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{5+n}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3}{5}=\frac{15}{5+n+15}{/tex} (n + 20)3 = 75 3n = 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 5 Therefore, the value of n is 5.
solution: Let the events be E 1 : bag I is elected E 2 : bag II is selected A: getting a red ball P(E 1 ) = P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} P(A/E 1 ) {tex}=\frac{3}{9}=\frac{1}{3};{/tex} P(A/E) {tex}=\frac{5}{5+n}{/tex} We have, {tex}P\left(E_{2} / A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A /E_{2}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)}{/tex} [Bayes Theorem] P(E 2 /A) {tex}=\frac{3}{5}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{5+n}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{5+n}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3}{5}=\frac{15}{5+n+15}{/tex} (n + 20)3 = 75 3n = 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 5 Therefore, the value of n is 5.
solution_html:
Let the events be
E1: bag I is elected
E2: bag II is selected
A: getting a red ball
P(E1) = P(E2) = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex}
P(A/E1) {tex}=\frac{3}{9}=\frac{1}{3};{/tex} P(A/E) {tex}=\frac{5}{5+n}{/tex}
We have, {tex}P\left(E_{2} / A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A /E_{2}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)}{/tex} [Bayes Theorem]
P(E2/A) {tex}=\frac{3}{5}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{5+n}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{5+n}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3}{5}=\frac{15}{5+n+15}{/tex}
(n + 20)3 = 75
3n = 15
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 5
Therefore, the value of n is 5.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Given that A and B are two independent events such that P(A) = 0.3 and P(B) = 0.5. Find P(A | B).
question_text: Given that A and B are two independent events such that P(A) = 0.3 and P(B) = 0.5. Find P(A | B).
explanation: P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}=\frac{P(A) P(B)}{P(B)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.5×0.3}{0.5}=0.3{/tex}
solution: P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}=\frac{P(A) P(B)}{P(B)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.5×0.3}{0.5}=0.3{/tex}
solution_html:
P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}=\frac{P(A) P(B)}{P(B)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.5×0.3}{0.5}=0.3{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The probabilities of A, B and C solving a problem independently are {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} respectively. If all the three try to solve the problem independently, find the probability that the problem is solved.
question_text: The probabilities of A, B and C solving a problem independently are {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} respectively. If all the three try to solve the problem independently, find the probability that the problem is solved.
explanation: Required probability = 1 - P(problem is not solved) = 1 - {tex}{P}\left({A}^{\prime} \cap {B}^{\prime} \cap {C}^{\prime}\right){/tex} = 1 - P(A') P(B') P(C') = 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
solution: Required probability = 1 - P(problem is not solved) = 1 - {tex}{P}\left({A}^{\prime} \cap {B}^{\prime} \cap {C}^{\prime}\right){/tex} = 1 - P(A') P(B') P(C') = 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Required probability = 1 - P(problem is not solved)
= 1 - {tex}{P}\left({A}^{\prime} \cap {B}^{\prime} \cap {C}^{\prime}\right){/tex}
= 1 - P(A') P(B') P(C')
= 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If P(A) = 0.6, P(B) = 0.5 and P(B|A) = 0.4, find P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) and P(A|B).
question_text: If P(A) = 0.6, P(B) = 0.5 and P(B|A) = 0.4, find P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) and P(A|B).
explanation: P(B|A) = 0.4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex} = 0.4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(B \cap A){/tex} = 0.24 {tex}P(A \cup B){/tex} = P(A) + P(B) - {tex}P(A \cap B){/tex} = 0.6 + 0.5 - 0.24 = 0.86 P(A I B) = {tex}\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.24}{0.5}{/tex} = 0.48
solution: P(B|A) = 0.4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex} = 0.4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(B \cap A){/tex} = 0.24 {tex}P(A \cup B){/tex} = P(A) + P(B) - {tex}P(A \cap B){/tex} = 0.6 + 0.5 - 0.24 = 0.86 P(A I B) = {tex}\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.24}{0.5}{/tex} = 0.48
solution_html:
P(B|A) = 0.4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex} = 0.4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P(B \cap A){/tex} = 0.24
{tex}P(A \cup B){/tex} = P(A) + P(B) - {tex}P(A \cap B){/tex} = 0.6 + 0.5 - 0.24 = 0.86
P(A I B) = {tex}\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.24}{0.5}{/tex} = 0.48

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The probability of two students A and B coming to school on time are {tex}\frac {2} {7}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {4} {7}{/tex}, respectively. Assuming that the events A coming on time and B coming on time are independent, find the probability of only one of them coming to school on time.
question_text: The probability of two students A and B coming to school on time are {tex}\frac {2} {7}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {4} {7}{/tex} , respectively. Assuming that the events A coming on time and B coming on time are independent, find the probability of only one of them coming to school on time.
explanation: P(A) = {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac{4}{7}{/tex} P( {tex}\overline A{/tex} ) = 1 - {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{7}{/tex} P( {tex}\overline B{/tex} ) = 1 - {tex}\frac{4}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex} Required probability = {tex}P(A \cap \overline{B}){/tex} + {tex}P(\overline{A} \cap B){/tex} = P(A) {tex}\cdot{/tex} P( {tex}\overline B{/tex} ) + P( {tex}\overline A{/tex} ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} P(B) = {tex}\frac{2}{7} \times \frac{3}{7}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{5}{7} \times \frac{4}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{49}+\frac{20}{49}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{26}{49}{/tex}
solution: P(A) = {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac{4}{7}{/tex} P( {tex}\overline A{/tex} ) = 1 - {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{7}{/tex} P( {tex}\overline B{/tex} ) = 1 - {tex}\frac{4}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex} Required probability = {tex}P(A \cap \overline{B}){/tex} + {tex}P(\overline{A} \cap B){/tex} = P(A) {tex}\cdot{/tex} P( {tex}\overline B{/tex} ) + P( {tex}\overline A{/tex} ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} P(B) = {tex}\frac{2}{7} \times \frac{3}{7}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{5}{7} \times \frac{4}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{49}+\frac{20}{49}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{26}{49}{/tex}
solution_html:
P(A) = {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex}, P(B) = {tex}\frac{4}{7}{/tex} 
P({tex}\overline A{/tex} ) = 1 - {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{7}{/tex} 
P({tex}\overline B{/tex}) = 1 - {tex}\frac{4}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex} 
Required probability = {tex}P(A \cap \overline{B}){/tex} + {tex}P(\overline{A} \cap B){/tex} 
= P(A) {tex}\cdot{/tex} P({tex}\overline B{/tex}) + P({tex}\overline A{/tex}) {tex}\cdot{/tex} P(B)
= {tex}\frac{2}{7} \times \frac{3}{7}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{5}{7} \times \frac{4}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{49}+\frac{20}{49}{/tex} 
 = {tex}\frac{26}{49}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Bag I contains 4 red and 2 green balls and Bag II contains 3 red and 5 green balls. One ball is transferred at random from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn at random from Bag II. The ball so drawn is found to be green in colour. Find the probability that the transferred ball is also green.
question_text: Bag I contains 4 red and 2 green balls and Bag II contains 3 red and 5 green balls. One ball is transferred at random from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn at random from Bag II. The ball so drawn is found to be green in colour. Find the probability that the transferred ball is also green.
explanation: Let E 1 : Transferred ball is green E 2 : Transferred ball is red A: Green ball is found Here, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{6}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{4}{6}{/tex} P(A/E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{6}{9}{/tex} , P(A/E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex} Using Baye’s theorem P(E 1 /A) = {tex}\frac{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}}{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}+\frac{4}{6} \times \frac{5}{9}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{12}{12+20}=\frac{3}{8}{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 : Transferred ball is green E 2 : Transferred ball is red A: Green ball is found Here, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{6}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{4}{6}{/tex} P(A/E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{6}{9}{/tex} , P(A/E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex} Using Baye’s theorem P(E 1 /A) = {tex}\frac{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}}{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}+\frac{4}{6} \times \frac{5}{9}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{12}{12+20}=\frac{3}{8}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1: Transferred ball is green
E2: Transferred ball is red
A: Green ball is found
Here, P(E1) = {tex}\frac{2}{6}{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex}\frac{4}{6}{/tex}
P(A/E1) = {tex}\frac{6}{9}{/tex}, P(A/E2) = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
Using Baye’s theorem
P(E1/A) = {tex}\frac{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{2}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}}{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}+\frac{4}{6} \times \frac{5}{9}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{12}{12+20}=\frac{3}{8}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A card from a pack of 52 playing cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, two cards are drawn at random (without replacement) and both are found to be spades. Find the probability of the lost card being a spade.
question_text: A card from a pack of 52 playing cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, two cards are drawn at random (without replacement) and both are found to be spades. Find the probability of the lost card being a spade.
explanation: Let E 1 , E 2 , E 3 and E 4 be the events of losing a card of spades, clubs, hearts and diamonds respectively. Therefore, we have, P(E 1 ) = P(E 2 ) = P(E 3 ) = P(E 4 ) = {tex}\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Let E be the event of drawing 2 spades from the remaining 51 cards. Therefore,we have, {tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{1}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of spades is missing = {tex}\frac{^{12} C_{2}}{^{51} C_{2}}=\frac{(12 \times 11)}{2 !} \times \frac{2 !}{(51 \times 50)}=\frac{22}{425}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{2}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of clubs is missing = {tex}\frac{^{13} C_{2}}{51}=\frac{(13 \times 12)}{2 !} \times \frac{2 !}{(51 \times 50)}=\frac{26}{425}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{3}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of hearts is missing = {tex}\frac{^{13} C_{2}}{^{51} C_{2}}=\frac{26}{425}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{4}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of diamonds is missing = {tex}\frac{^{13}C_{2}}{^{51} C_{2}}=\frac{26}{425}{/tex} Therefore, by Baye's theorm, {tex} \ P\left(\frac E{E_{1}}\right){/tex} = probability of the lost card being a spade, given that 2 spades are drawn from the remaining 51 cards = {tex}\frac{P\left(\frac E {E_{1}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{1}\right)}{P\left(\frac E {E_{1}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{1}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{2}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{2}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{3}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{3}\right)+p(\frac E{E_4})\cdot P(E_4)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{425}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{425}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{26}{425}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{26}{425}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{26}{425}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{22}{100}{/tex} = 0.22 Hence, the required probability is 0.22.
solution: Let E 1 , E 2 , E 3 and E 4 be the events of losing a card of spades, clubs, hearts and diamonds respectively. Therefore, we have, P(E 1 ) = P(E 2 ) = P(E 3 ) = P(E 4 ) = {tex}\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Let E be the event of drawing 2 spades from the remaining 51 cards. Therefore,we have, {tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{1}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of spades is missing = {tex}\frac{^{12} C_{2}}{^{51} C_{2}}=\frac{(12 \times 11)}{2 !} \times \frac{2 !}{(51 \times 50)}=\frac{22}{425}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{2}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of clubs is missing = {tex}\frac{^{13} C_{2}}{51}=\frac{(13 \times 12)}{2 !} \times \frac{2 !}{(51 \times 50)}=\frac{26}{425}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{3}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of hearts is missing = {tex}\frac{^{13} C_{2}}{^{51} C_{2}}=\frac{26}{425}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{4}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of diamonds is missing = {tex}\frac{^{13}C_{2}}{^{51} C_{2}}=\frac{26}{425}{/tex} Therefore, by Baye's theorm, {tex} \ P\left(\frac E{E_{1}}\right){/tex} = probability of the lost card being a spade, given that 2 spades are drawn from the remaining 51 cards = {tex}\frac{P\left(\frac E {E_{1}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{1}\right)}{P\left(\frac E {E_{1}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{1}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{2}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{2}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{3}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{3}\right)+p(\frac E{E_4})\cdot P(E_4)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{425}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{425}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{26}{425}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{26}{425}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{26}{425}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{22}{100}{/tex} = 0.22 Hence, the required probability is 0.22.
solution_html:
Let E1, E2, E3 and E4 be the events of losing a card of spades, clubs, hearts and diamonds respectively.
Therefore, we have,
 P(E1) = P(E2) = P(E3) = P(E4) = {tex}\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Let E be the event of drawing 2 spades from the remaining 51 cards. Therefore,we have,
{tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{1}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of spades is missing
= {tex}\frac{^{12} C_{2}}{^{51} C_{2}}=\frac{(12 \times 11)}{2 !} \times \frac{2 !}{(51 \times 50)}=\frac{22}{425}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{2}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of clubs is missing
= {tex}\frac{^{13} C_{2}}{51}=\frac{(13 \times 12)}{2 !} \times \frac{2 !}{(51 \times 50)}=\frac{26}{425}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{3}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of hearts is missing
= {tex}\frac{^{13} C_{2}}{^{51} C_{2}}=\frac{26}{425}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac E{E_{4}}\right){/tex} = probability of drawing 2 spades, given that a card of diamonds is missing
= {tex}\frac{^{13}C_{2}}{^{51} C_{2}}=\frac{26}{425}{/tex}

Therefore, by Baye's theorm,
{tex} \ P\left(\frac E{E_{1}}\right){/tex} = probability of the lost card being a spade, given that 2 spades are drawn from the remaining 51 cards
= {tex}\frac{P\left(\frac E {E_{1}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{1}\right)}{P\left(\frac E {E_{1}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{1}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{2}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{2}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{3}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{3}\right)+p(\frac E{E_4})\cdot P(E_4)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{425}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{425}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{26}{425}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{26}{425}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{26}{425}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{22}{100}{/tex} = 0.22
Hence, the required probability is 0.22.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A bag contains 5 red and 4 black balls, a second bag contains 3 red and 6 black balls. One of the two bags is selected at random and two balls are drawn at random (without replacement) both of which are found to be red. Find the probability that the balls are drawn from the second bag.
question_text: A bag contains 5 red and 4 black balls, a second bag contains 3 red and 6 black balls. One of the two bags is selected at random and two balls are drawn at random (without replacement) both of which are found to be red. Find the probability that the balls are drawn from the second bag.
explanation: E 1 = Event of selecting first bag E 2 = Event of selecting second bag A = Event both balls drawn are red. P(E 1 ) = P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ;P(A|E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{20}{72}{/tex} ; P(A|E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{{ }^3 C_2}{{ }^9 C_2}=\frac{6}{72}{/tex} P(E 2 |A) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A|E}_2\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A|E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{72}}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{20}{72}+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{72}}=\frac{6}{26}=\frac{3}{13}{/tex}
solution: E 1 = Event of selecting first bag E 2 = Event of selecting second bag A = Event both balls drawn are red. P(E 1 ) = P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ;P(A|E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{20}{72}{/tex} ; P(A|E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{{ }^3 C_2}{{ }^9 C_2}=\frac{6}{72}{/tex} P(E 2 |A) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A|E}_2\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A|E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{72}}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{20}{72}+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{72}}=\frac{6}{26}=\frac{3}{13}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1 = Event of selecting first bag
E2 = Event of selecting second bag
A = Event both balls drawn are red.
P(E1) = P(E2) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex};P(A|E1) = {tex}\frac{{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2}{{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{20}{72}{/tex}; P(A|E2) = {tex}\frac{{ }^3 C_2}{{ }^9 C_2}=\frac{6}{72}{/tex}
P(E2|A) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A|E}_2\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A|E}_2\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{72}}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{20}{72}+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{72}}=\frac{6}{26}=\frac{3}{13}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The probabilities of solving a specific problem independently by A and B are {tex}\frac{1} {3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1} {5}{/tex} respectively. If both try to solve the problem independently, find the probability that the problem is solved.
question_text: The probabilities of solving a specific problem independently by A and B are {tex}\frac{1} {3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1} {5}{/tex} respectively. If both try to solve the problem independently, find the probability that the problem is solved.
explanation: Probability of solving the problem by A, P(A)= {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Probability of solving the problem by B, P(B)= {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} Since the problem is solved independently by A and B, ∴P(AB)=P(A)⋅P(B)= {tex}\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{5} =\frac{1}{15}{/tex} Probability that the problem is solved =P(A∪B) =P(A)+P(B)−P(AB) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{5}-\frac{1}{15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5+3-1}{15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{15}{/tex}
solution: Probability of solving the problem by A, P(A)= {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Probability of solving the problem by B, P(B)= {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} Since the problem is solved independently by A and B, ∴P(AB)=P(A)⋅P(B)= {tex}\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{5} =\frac{1}{15}{/tex} Probability that the problem is solved =P(A∪B) =P(A)+P(B)−P(AB) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{5}-\frac{1}{15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5+3-1}{15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{15}{/tex}
solution_html:
Probability of solving the problem by A, P(A)={tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Probability of solving the problem by B, P(B)={tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}
Since the problem is solved independently by A and B,
∴P(AB)=P(A)⋅P(B)={tex}\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{5} =\frac{1}{15}{/tex}
Probability that the problem is solved =P(A∪B)
=P(A)+P(B)−P(AB)
={tex}\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{5}-\frac{1}{15}{/tex}
={tex}\frac{5+3-1}{15}{/tex}

= {tex}\frac{7}{15}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If A and B are independent events with P(A) = {tex}\frac {3} {7}{/tex} and P(B) = {tex}\frac {2} {5}{/tex}, then find P(A' {tex}\cap{/tex} B').
question_text: If A and B are independent events with P(A) = {tex}\frac {3} {7}{/tex} and P(B) = {tex}\frac {2} {5}{/tex} , then find P(A' {tex}\cap{/tex} B').
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \cap \mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right)=\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right){/tex} = {tex}(1-\frac{3}{7})(1-\frac{2}{5}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{12}{35}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \cap \mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right)=\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right){/tex} = {tex}(1-\frac{3}{7})(1-\frac{2}{5}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{12}{35}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \cap \mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right)=\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right){/tex}

= {tex}(1-\frac{3}{7})(1-\frac{2}{5}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{3}{5}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{12}{35}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: There are three coins. One is a coin having tails on both faces, another is a biased coin that comes up tails 70% of the time and the third is an unbiased coin. One of the coins is chosen at random and tossed, it shows tail. Find the probability that it was a coin with tail on both the faces.
question_text: There are three coins. One is a coin having tails on both faces, another is a biased coin that comes up tails 70% of the time and the third is an unbiased coin. One of the coins is chosen at random and tossed, it shows tail. Find the probability that it was a coin with tail on both the faces.
explanation: E 1 : Selected coin has tail on both faces E 2 : Selected coin is biased E 3 : Selected coin is unbiased A: Tail comes up P(E 1 ) = P(E 2 ) = P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac {1} {3}{/tex} P(A|E 1 ) = 1, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{AlE}_2\right)=\frac{7}{10}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{AlE}_3\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1 \mid \mathrm{A}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid E_1\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid E_1\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid E_2\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_3\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{AlE}_3\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{3} \times 1}{\frac{1}{3} \times 1+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{7}{10}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{22}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{5}{11}{/tex}
solution: E 1 : Selected coin has tail on both faces E 2 : Selected coin is biased E 3 : Selected coin is unbiased A: Tail comes up P(E 1 ) = P(E 2 ) = P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac {1} {3}{/tex} P(A|E 1 ) = 1, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{AlE}_2\right)=\frac{7}{10}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{AlE}_3\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1 \mid \mathrm{A}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid E_1\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid E_1\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid E_2\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_3\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{AlE}_3\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{3} \times 1}{\frac{1}{3} \times 1+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{7}{10}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{22}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{5}{11}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1: Selected coin has tail on both faces
E2: Selected coin is biased
E3: Selected coin is unbiased
A: Tail comes up
P(E1) = P(E2) = P(E3) = {tex}\frac {1} {3}{/tex}
P(A|E1) = 1, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{AlE}_2\right)=\frac{7}{10}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{AlE}_3\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1 \mid \mathrm{A}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid E_1\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid E_1\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid E_2\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_3\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{AlE}_3\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{3} \times 1}{\frac{1}{3} \times 1+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{7}{10}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{10}{22}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{5}{11}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: In answering a question on a multiple choice questions test with four choices in each question, out of which only one is correct, a student either guesses or copies or knows the answer. The probability that he makes a guess is {tex}\frac {1} {4}{/tex} and the probability the he copies is also {tex}\frac {1} {4}{/tex}. The probability that the answer is correct, given that he copied it is {tex}\frac {3} {4}{/tex}. Find the probability that he knows the answer to the question, given that he correctly answered it.
question_text: In answering a question on a multiple choice questions test with four choices in each question, out of which only one is correct, a student either guesses or copies or knows the answer. The probability that he makes a guess is {tex}\frac {1} {4}{/tex} and the probability the he copies is also {tex}\frac {1} {4}{/tex} . The probability that the answer is correct, given that he copied it is {tex}\frac {3} {4}{/tex} . Find the probability that he knows the answer to the question, given that he correctly answered it.
explanation: Let E 1 = Student guesses the answer E 2 = Student copies the answer E 3 = Student knows the answer A = Student answers the question correctly. {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_3\right)=1-\left(\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{3}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_3\right)=1{/tex} The required probability {tex}=P\left(E_3 \mid A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_3\right) \times P\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^3 P\left(E_i\right) \times P\left(A \mid E_i\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times 1}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \times 1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{8}+\frac{3}{8}+1}=\frac{8}{12}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 = Student guesses the answer E 2 = Student copies the answer E 3 = Student knows the answer A = Student answers the question correctly. {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_3\right)=1-\left(\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{3}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_3\right)=1{/tex} The required probability {tex}=P\left(E_3 \mid A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_3\right) \times P\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^3 P\left(E_i\right) \times P\left(A \mid E_i\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times 1}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \times 1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{8}+\frac{3}{8}+1}=\frac{8}{12}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 = Student guesses the answer
E2 = Student copies the answer
E3 = Student knows the answer
A = Student answers the question correctly.
{tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_3\right)=1-\left(\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{3}{4}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} \mid \mathrm{E}_3\right)=1{/tex}
The required probability
{tex}=P\left(E_3 \mid A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_3\right) \times P\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^3 P\left(E_i\right) \times P\left(A \mid E_i\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times 1}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \times 1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{8}+\frac{3}{8}+1}=\frac{8}{12}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Four cards are drawn one by one with replacement from a well-shuffled deck of playing cards. Find the probability that at least three cards are of diamonds.
question_text: Four cards are drawn one by one with replacement from a well-shuffled deck of playing cards. Find the probability that at least three cards are of diamonds.
explanation: n = 4, {tex}\mathrm{p}=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{q}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} P (atleast 3 are diamonds) = P (X = 3) + P(X = 4) {tex}={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_3\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^3\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)+{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_4\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^4{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^4[12+1]=\frac{13}{256}{/tex}
solution: n = 4, {tex}\mathrm{p}=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{q}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} P (atleast 3 are diamonds) = P (X = 3) + P(X = 4) {tex}={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_3\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^3\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)+{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_4\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^4{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^4[12+1]=\frac{13}{256}{/tex}
solution_html:
n = 4, {tex}\mathrm{p}=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{q}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
P (atleast 3 are diamonds) = P (X = 3) + P(X = 4)
{tex}={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_3\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^3\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)+{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_4\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^4{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^4[12+1]=\frac{13}{256}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A bag contains 5 red and 3 black balls and another bag contains 2 red and 6 black balls. Two balls are drawn at random (without replacement) from one of the bags and both are found to be red. Find the probability that balls are drawn from the first bag.
question_text: A bag contains 5 red and 3 black balls and another bag contains 2 red and 6 black balls. Two balls are drawn at random (without replacement) from one of the bags and both are found to be red. Find the probability that balls are drawn from the first bag.
explanation: E 1 : ball drawn from first bag E 2 : ball drawn from second bag A : both drawn balls are red P(E 1 ) P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} P(A|E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{4}{7}=\frac{20}{56}{/tex} P(A|E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{8} \times \frac{1}{7}=\frac{2}{56}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1 \mid \mathrm{A}\right)=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{20}{56}}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{20}{56}+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{56}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{20}{112}}{\frac{22}{112}}=\frac{10}{11}{/tex}
solution: E 1 : ball drawn from first bag E 2 : ball drawn from second bag A : both drawn balls are red P(E 1 ) P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} P(A|E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{4}{7}=\frac{20}{56}{/tex} P(A|E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{8} \times \frac{1}{7}=\frac{2}{56}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1 \mid \mathrm{A}\right)=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{20}{56}}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{20}{56}+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{56}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{20}{112}}{\frac{22}{112}}=\frac{10}{11}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1 : ball drawn from first bag
E2 : ball drawn from second bag
A : both drawn balls are red
P(E1) P(E2) = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex}
P(A|E1) = {tex}\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{4}{7}=\frac{20}{56}{/tex}
P(A|E2) = {tex}\frac{2}{8} \times \frac{1}{7}=\frac{2}{56}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1 \mid \mathrm{A}\right)=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{20}{56}}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{20}{56}+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{56}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{20}{112}}{\frac{22}{112}}=\frac{10}{11}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Bag I contains 3 white and 4 black balls, while Bag II contains 5 white and 3 black balls. One ball is transferred at random from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn at random from Bag II. The ball so drawn is found to be white. Find the probability that the transferred ball is also white.
question_text: Bag I contains 3 white and 4 black balls, while Bag II contains 5 white and 3 black balls. One ball is transferred at random from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn at random from Bag II. The ball so drawn is found to be white. Find the probability that the transferred ball is also white.
explanation: Let E 1 : Transferred ball is white E 2 : Transferred ball is black A: white ball is found Here, {tex} \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{3}{7}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{4}{7} {/tex} {tex} \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{6}{9}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{5}{9} {/tex} Using Baye’s theorem {tex} P\left(E_1 / A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(A / E_1\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(A / E_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{3}{7} \times \frac{6}{9}}{\frac{3}{7} \times \frac{6}{9}+\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{5}{9}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{18}{18+20}=\frac{9}{19} {/tex}
solution: Let E 1 : Transferred ball is white E 2 : Transferred ball is black A: white ball is found Here, {tex} \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{3}{7}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{4}{7} {/tex} {tex} \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{6}{9}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{5}{9} {/tex} Using Baye’s theorem {tex} P\left(E_1 / A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(A / E_1\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(A / E_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{3}{7} \times \frac{6}{9}}{\frac{3}{7} \times \frac{6}{9}+\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{5}{9}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{18}{18+20}=\frac{9}{19} {/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1: Transferred ball is white
E2: Transferred ball is black
A: white ball is found
Here, {tex} \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{3}{7}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{4}{7} {/tex}
{tex} \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_1\right)=\frac{6}{9}, \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_2\right)=\frac{5}{9} {/tex}
Using Baye’s theorem
{tex} P\left(E_1 / A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(A / E_1\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(A / E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(A / E_2\right)} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\frac{3}{7} \times \frac{6}{9}}{\frac{3}{7} \times \frac{6}{9}+\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{5}{9}} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{18}{18+20}=\frac{9}{19} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Two groups are competing for the position on the Board of directors of a corporation. The probabilities that the first and the second groups will win are 0.6 and 0.4 respectively. Further, if the first group wins, the probability of introducing a new product is 0.7 and the corresponding probability if 0.3, if the second group wins. Find the probability that the new product introduced was by the second group.
question_text: Two groups are competing for the position on the Board of directors of a corporation. The probabilities that the first and the second groups will win are 0.6 and 0.4 respectively. Further, if the first group wins, the probability of introducing a new product is 0.7 and the corresponding probability if 0.3, if the second group wins. Find the probability that the new product introduced was by the second group.
explanation: Given: P (G 1 ) = 0.6, P (G 2 ) = 0.4 Let P denotes the launching of new product. {tex}\therefore {/tex} P(P | G 1 ) = 0.7, P(P | G 2 ) = 0.3 P(G 1 |P) = {tex}\frac{{P\left( {{G_2}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_2}} \right)}}{{P\left( {{G_1}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_1}} \right) + P\left( {{G_2}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_2}} \right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{0.4 \times 0.3}}{{0.6 \times 0.7 + 0.4 \times 0.3}} = \frac{{12}}{{54}} = \frac{2}{9}{/tex}
solution: Given: P (G 1 ) = 0.6, P (G 2 ) = 0.4 Let P denotes the launching of new product. {tex}\therefore {/tex} P(P | G 1 ) = 0.7, P(P | G 2 ) = 0.3 P(G 1 |P) = {tex}\frac{{P\left( {{G_2}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_2}} \right)}}{{P\left( {{G_1}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_1}} \right) + P\left( {{G_2}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_2}} \right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{0.4 \times 0.3}}{{0.6 \times 0.7 + 0.4 \times 0.3}} = \frac{{12}}{{54}} = \frac{2}{9}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: P (G1) = 0.6, P (G2) = 0.4
Let P denotes the launching of new product.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} P(P|G1) = 0.7, P(P|G2) = 0.3
P(G1|P) ={tex}\frac{{P\left( {{G_2}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_2}} \right)}}{{P\left( {{G_1}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_1}} \right) + P\left( {{G_2}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_2}} \right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{0.4 \times 0.3}}{{0.6 \times 0.7 + 0.4 \times 0.3}} = \frac{{12}}{{54}} = \frac{2}{9}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Suppose a girl throws a die. If she gets a 5 or 6, she tosses a coin three times and notes the number of heads. If she gets 1, 2, 3 or 4, she tosses a coin once and notes whether a head or tail is obtained. If she obtained exactly one head, what is the probability that she threw 1, 2, 3 or 4 with the die?
question_text: Suppose a girl throws a die. If she gets a 5 or 6, she tosses a coin three times and notes the number of heads. If she gets 1, 2, 3 or 4, she tosses a coin once and notes whether a head or tail is obtained. If she obtained exactly one head, what is the probability that she threw 1, 2, 3 or 4 with the die?
explanation: E 1 : 1, 2, 3, 4 is shown on dice E 2 : 5 or 6 is shown on dice P(E 1 ) = {tex} \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex} \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} Let, A exactly one head shown up {tex}P(\frac {A}{{E_1}}) = \frac{1}{2},P(\frac {A}{{E_2}}) = \frac{3}{8}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {{E_1}}{A}) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1}) + P({E_2})P(A/{E_2})}} = \frac{8}{{11}}{/tex}
solution: E 1 : 1, 2, 3, 4 is shown on dice E 2 : 5 or 6 is shown on dice P(E 1 ) = {tex} \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex} \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} Let, A exactly one head shown up {tex}P(\frac {A}{{E_1}}) = \frac{1}{2},P(\frac {A}{{E_2}}) = \frac{3}{8}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {{E_1}}{A}) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1}) + P({E_2})P(A/{E_2})}} = \frac{8}{{11}}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1 : 1, 2, 3, 4 is shown on dice
E2 : 5 or 6 is shown on dice
P(E1) = {tex} \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex} \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Let, A exactly one head shown up
{tex}P(\frac {A}{{E_1}}) = \frac{1}{2},P(\frac {A}{{E_2}}) = \frac{3}{8}{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac {{E_1}}{A}) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1}) + P({E_2})P(A/{E_2})}} = \frac{8}{{11}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Suppose a girl throws a die. If she gets 1 or 2, she tosses a coin three times and notes the number of tails. If she gets 3, 4, 5 or 6, she tosses a coin once and notes whether a head or tail is obtained. If she obtained exactly one tail, what is the probability that she threw 3, 4, 5 or 6 with the die?
question_text: Suppose a girl throws a die. If she gets 1 or 2, she tosses a coin three times and notes the number of tails. If she gets 3, 4, 5 or 6, she tosses a coin once and notes whether a head or tail is obtained. If she obtained exactly one tail, what is the probability that she threw 3, 4, 5 or 6 with the die?
explanation: Let E 1 be the event that girl will get 1 or 2 P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac { 2 } { 6 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} Let E 2 be the event that girl will get 3, 4, 5 or 6 P(E 2 ) = {tex} \frac { 4 } { 6 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex} Let A be the event that she obtained exactly a tail. {tex}P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 1 } } \right) {/tex} = p(getting exactly one tail when a coin is tossed three times) = {tex}\frac { 3 } { 2^3 }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 3 } { 8 }{/tex} {tex}P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 2 } } \right) {/tex} = P(getting exactly a tail when a coin is tossed once) = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} Now, required probability, {tex}\Rightarrow P \left( \frac { E _ { 2 } } { A } \right) = \frac { P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 2 } } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) \cdot P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 1 } } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 2 } } \right) }{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{8}+\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8}{11}{/tex} Hence the probability that she threw 3, 4, 5 or 6 with the die is {tex}\frac { 8 } { 11 }{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 be the event that girl will get 1 or 2 P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac { 2 } { 6 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} Let E 2 be the event that girl will get 3, 4, 5 or 6 P(E 2 ) = {tex} \frac { 4 } { 6 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex} Let A be the event that she obtained exactly a tail. {tex}P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 1 } } \right) {/tex} = p(getting exactly one tail when a coin is tossed three times) = {tex}\frac { 3 } { 2^3 }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 3 } { 8 }{/tex} {tex}P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 2 } } \right) {/tex} = P(getting exactly a tail when a coin is tossed once) = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} Now, required probability, {tex}\Rightarrow P \left( \frac { E _ { 2 } } { A } \right) = \frac { P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 2 } } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) \cdot P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 1 } } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 2 } } \right) }{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{8}+\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8}{11}{/tex} Hence the probability that she threw 3, 4, 5 or 6 with the die is {tex}\frac { 8 } { 11 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 be the event that girl will get 1 or 2
P(E1) = {tex}\frac { 2 } { 6 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
Let E2 be the event that girl will get 3, 4, 5 or 6
P(E2) = {tex} \frac { 4 } { 6 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex}
Let A be the event that she obtained exactly a tail.
{tex}P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 1 } } \right) {/tex} = p(getting exactly one tail when a coin is tossed three times) = {tex}\frac { 3 } { 2^3 }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 3 } { 8 }{/tex}
{tex}P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 2 } } \right) {/tex} = P(getting exactly a tail when a coin is tossed once) = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
Now, required probability,
{tex}\Rightarrow P \left( \frac { E _ { 2 } } { A } \right) = \frac { P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 2 } } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) \cdot P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 1 } } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( \frac { A } { E _ { 2 } } \right) }{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{8}+\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{8}{11}{/tex}
Hence the probability that she threw 3, 4, 5 or 6 with the die is {tex}\frac { 8 } { 11 }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Bag A contains 3 red and 5 black balls, while bag B contains 4 red and 4 black balls. Two balls are transferred at random from bag A to bag B and then a ball is drawn from bag B at random. If the ball drawn from bag B is found to be red, find the probability that two red balls were transferred from A to B.
question_text: Bag A contains 3 red and 5 black balls, while bag B contains 4 red and 4 black balls. Two balls are transferred at random from bag A to bag B and then a ball is drawn from bag B at random. If the ball drawn from bag B is found to be red, find the probability that two red balls were transferred from A to B.
explanation: Let us define the following events: E 1 = one red and one black ball is transferred E 2 = two red balls are transferred E 3 = two black balls are transferred E = drawn ball is red. Then, P(E) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 3 } C _ { 1 } \times ^ { 5 } C _ { 1 } } { 8 } = \frac { 3 \times 5 } { 28 } = \frac { 15 } { 28 }{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 3 } C _ { 2 } } {^{ 8} { C _ { 2 } } } = \frac { 3 } { 28 }{/tex} P(E 3 ) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 5 } C _ { 2 } } { ^ { 8 } C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 10 } { 28 }{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {E}{E_1}) = \frac {5}{10}{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E}{ E _ { 2 }}) = \frac { 6 } { 10 }{/tex} {tex} P(\frac {E}{{E_3}}) = \frac{4}{{10}}{/tex} Now, required probability= P( {tex}\frac {E_2}{E}{/tex} ) {tex} = \frac { P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 2 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( E / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 2 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 3 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 3 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 3 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 6 } { 10 } } { \frac { 15 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 5 } { 10 } + \frac { 3 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 6 } { 10 } + \frac { 10 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 4 } { 10 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 18 } { 75 + 18 + 40 } = \frac { 18 } { 133 }{/tex}
solution: Let us define the following events: E 1 = one red and one black ball is transferred E 2 = two red balls are transferred E 3 = two black balls are transferred E = drawn ball is red. Then, P(E) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 3 } C _ { 1 } \times ^ { 5 } C _ { 1 } } { 8 } = \frac { 3 \times 5 } { 28 } = \frac { 15 } { 28 }{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 3 } C _ { 2 } } {^{ 8} { C _ { 2 } } } = \frac { 3 } { 28 }{/tex} P(E 3 ) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 5 } C _ { 2 } } { ^ { 8 } C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 10 } { 28 }{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {E}{E_1}) = \frac {5}{10}{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E}{ E _ { 2 }}) = \frac { 6 } { 10 }{/tex} {tex} P(\frac {E}{{E_3}}) = \frac{4}{{10}}{/tex} Now, required probability= P( {tex}\frac {E_2}{E}{/tex} ) {tex} = \frac { P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 2 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( E / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 2 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 3 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 3 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 3 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 6 } { 10 } } { \frac { 15 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 5 } { 10 } + \frac { 3 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 6 } { 10 } + \frac { 10 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 4 } { 10 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 18 } { 75 + 18 + 40 } = \frac { 18 } { 133 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let us define the following events:
E1 = one red and one black ball is transferred 
E2 = two red balls are transferred
E3 = two black balls are transferred
E = drawn ball is red.
Then,
P(E) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 3 } C _ { 1 } \times ^ { 5 } C _ { 1 } } { 8 } = \frac { 3 \times 5 } { 28 } = \frac { 15 } { 28 }{/tex}
P(E2) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 3 } C _ { 2 } } {^{ 8} { C _ { 2 } } } = \frac { 3 } { 28 }{/tex}
P(E3) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 5 } C _ { 2 } } { ^ { 8 } C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 10 } { 28 }{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac {E}{E_1}) = \frac {5}{10}{/tex}
{tex}P (\frac {E}{ E _ { 2 }}) = \frac { 6 } { 10 }{/tex}
{tex} P(\frac {E}{{E_3}}) = \frac{4}{{10}}{/tex}
Now, required probability= P({tex}\frac {E_2}{E}{/tex})
{tex} = \frac { P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 2 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( E / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 2 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 3 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 3 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \frac { 3 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 6 } { 10 } } { \frac { 15 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 5 } { 10 } + \frac { 3 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 6 } { 10 } + \frac { 10 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 4 } { 10 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 18 } { 75 + 18 + 40 } = \frac { 18 } { 133 }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A black and a red dice are rolled. Find the conditional probability of obtaining the sum 8, given that the red die resulted in a number less than 4.
question_text: A black and a red dice are rolled. Find the conditional probability of obtaining the sum 8, given that the red die resulted in a number less than 4.
explanation: When 2 die are rolled, total number of outcomes = 36. Let first die is red and second die is black. Let A be the event of getting sum equal to 8. {tex}\therefore {/tex} A = {(2, 6), (3, 5), (4, 4), (5, 3), (6, 2)} Let A be the event B that red die results in a number less than 4. {tex}\therefore {/tex} B = {(1, 1), (1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5), (1, 6), (2, 1), (2, 2), (2, 3), (2, 4), (2, 5), (2, 6), (3, 1), (3, 2), (3, 3), (3, 4), (3, 5), (3, 6)} P (B) = {tex}\frac{{n\left( B \right)}}{{n\left( S \right)}} = \frac{{18}}{{36}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}A \cap B{/tex} = {(2, 6), (3, 5)} {tex} \Rightarrow n\left( {A \cap B} \right) = 2{/tex} {tex}P\left( {A \cap B} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{{36}} = \frac{1}{{18}}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {A\over B} \right) = \frac{{P\left( {A \cap B} \right)}}{{P\left( B \right)}} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\frac{1}{{18}}}}{{\frac{1}{2}}} \\= \frac{2}{{18}} \\= \frac{1}{9}.{/tex}
solution: When 2 die are rolled, total number of outcomes = 36. Let first die is red and second die is black. Let A be the event of getting sum equal to 8. {tex}\therefore {/tex} A = {(2, 6), (3, 5), (4, 4), (5, 3), (6, 2)} Let A be the event B that red die results in a number less than 4. {tex}\therefore {/tex} B = {(1, 1), (1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5), (1, 6), (2, 1), (2, 2), (2, 3), (2, 4), (2, 5), (2, 6), (3, 1), (3, 2), (3, 3), (3, 4), (3, 5), (3, 6)} P (B) = {tex}\frac{{n\left( B \right)}}{{n\left( S \right)}} = \frac{{18}}{{36}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}A \cap B{/tex} = {(2, 6), (3, 5)} {tex} \Rightarrow n\left( {A \cap B} \right) = 2{/tex} {tex}P\left( {A \cap B} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{{36}} = \frac{1}{{18}}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {A\over B} \right) = \frac{{P\left( {A \cap B} \right)}}{{P\left( B \right)}} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\frac{1}{{18}}}}{{\frac{1}{2}}} \\= \frac{2}{{18}} \\= \frac{1}{9}.{/tex}
solution_html:
When 2 die are rolled, total number of outcomes = 36.
Let first die is red and second die is black.
Let A be the event of getting sum equal to 8.
{tex}\therefore {/tex}  A = {(2, 6), (3, 5), (4, 4), (5, 3), (6, 2)}
Let A be the event B that red die results in a number less than 4.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} B = {(1, 1), (1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5), (1, 6), (2, 1), (2, 2), (2, 3), (2, 4), (2, 5), (2, 6), (3, 1), (3, 2), (3, 3), (3, 4), (3, 5), (3, 6)}
P (B) = {tex}\frac{{n\left( B \right)}}{{n\left( S \right)}} = \frac{{18}}{{36}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}A \cap B{/tex} = {(2, 6), (3, 5)}
{tex} \Rightarrow n\left( {A \cap B} \right) = 2{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {A \cap B} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{{36}} = \frac{1}{{18}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {A\over B} \right) = \frac{{P\left( {A \cap B} \right)}}{{P\left( B \right)}} {/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\frac{1}{{18}}}}{{\frac{1}{2}}} \\= \frac{2}{{18}} \\= \frac{1}{9}.{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A die is tossed thrice. Find the probability of getting an odd number at least once.
question_text: A die is tossed thrice. Find the probability of getting an odd number at least once.
explanation: S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} n(s) = 6 Let A represents an odd number. {tex}\therefore {/tex} A = {1, 3, 5} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} n(A) = 3 {tex}P (A) = \frac{{n(A)}}{{n(S)}} = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}{\text{P}}\left( {\bar A} \right) = 1 - P(A) = 1 - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} n = 3 Now P (at least one success) = 1 - P (an odd number on none of the three dice) {tex}= 1 - {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^3} = 1 - \frac{1}{8} = \frac{7}{8}{/tex}
solution: S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} n(s) = 6 Let A represents an odd number. {tex}\therefore {/tex} A = {1, 3, 5} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} n(A) = 3 {tex}P (A) = \frac{{n(A)}}{{n(S)}} = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}{\text{P}}\left( {\bar A} \right) = 1 - P(A) = 1 - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} n = 3 Now P (at least one success) = 1 - P (an odd number on none of the three dice) {tex}= 1 - {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^3} = 1 - \frac{1}{8} = \frac{7}{8}{/tex}
solution_html:
S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} n(s) = 6
Let A represents an odd number.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} A = {1, 3, 5} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} n(A) = 3
{tex}P (A) = \frac{{n(A)}}{{n(S)}} = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{\text{P}}\left( {\bar A} \right) = 1 - P(A) = 1 - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
n = 3
Now P (at least one success) = 1 - P (an odd number on none of the three dice)
{tex}= 1 - {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^3} = 1 - \frac{1}{8} = \frac{7}{8}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate {tex}{P\left( {A \cup B} \right)}{/tex} if 2P(A) = P (B) = {tex}\frac{5}{{13}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A|B} \right) = \frac{2}{5}{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex}{P\left( {A \cup B} \right)}{/tex} if 2P(A) = P (B) = {tex}\frac{5}{{13}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A|B} \right) = \frac{2}{5}{/tex}
explanation: Given: 2 P(A) = P (B) = {tex}\frac{5}{{13}}{/tex} , {tex}P\left( {A|B} \right) = \frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} P (A) = {tex}\frac{5}{{26}}{/tex} Now, {tex}P\left( {A \cap B} \right) = P\left( {B|A} \right){/tex} . P (B) = {tex}\frac{2}{5} \times \frac{5}{{13}} = \frac{2}{{13}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A \cup B} \right){/tex} = P(A) + P (B) – {tex}P\left( {A \cap B} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{{26}} + \frac{5}{{13}} - \frac{2}{{13}} = \frac{{11}}{{26}}{/tex}
solution: Given: 2 P(A) = P (B) = {tex}\frac{5}{{13}}{/tex} , {tex}P\left( {A|B} \right) = \frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} P (A) = {tex}\frac{5}{{26}}{/tex} Now, {tex}P\left( {A \cap B} \right) = P\left( {B|A} \right){/tex} . P (B) = {tex}\frac{2}{5} \times \frac{5}{{13}} = \frac{2}{{13}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A \cup B} \right){/tex} = P(A) + P (B) – {tex}P\left( {A \cap B} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{{26}} + \frac{5}{{13}} - \frac{2}{{13}} = \frac{{11}}{{26}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: 2 P(A) = P (B) = {tex}\frac{5}{{13}}{/tex}, {tex}P\left( {A|B} \right) = \frac{2}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} P (A) = {tex}\frac{5}{{26}}{/tex}
Now, {tex}P\left( {A \cap B} \right) = P\left( {B|A} \right){/tex} . P (B) = {tex}\frac{2}{5} \times \frac{5}{{13}} = \frac{2}{{13}}{/tex}
and {tex}P\left( {A \cup B} \right){/tex} = P(A) + P (B) – {tex}P\left( {A \cap B} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{{26}} + \frac{5}{{13}} - \frac{2}{{13}} = \frac{{11}}{{26}}{/tex}

question_html: Evaluate P(A  {tex}\cup{/tex} B), if 2P(A) = P(B) = {tex}\frac{5}{13}{/tex} and P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex}.
question_text: Evaluate P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B), if 2P(A) = P(B) = {tex}\frac{5}{13}{/tex} and P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} .
explanation: P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{{P}({B})}{/tex} gives {tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{2}{13}{/tex} {tex}{P}({A} \cup {B}){/tex} = P(A) + P(B) - {tex}P(A \cap B){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{26}+\frac{5}{13}-\frac{2}{13}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{11}{26}{/tex}
solution: P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{{P}({B})}{/tex} gives {tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{2}{13}{/tex} {tex}{P}({A} \cup {B}){/tex} = P(A) + P(B) - {tex}P(A \cap B){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{26}+\frac{5}{13}-\frac{2}{13}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{11}{26}{/tex}
solution_html:
P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{{P}({A} \cap {B})}{{P}({B})}{/tex} gives {tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{2}{13}{/tex}
{tex}{P}({A} \cup {B}){/tex}= P(A) + P(B) - {tex}P(A \cap B){/tex}
={tex}\frac{5}{26}+\frac{5}{13}-\frac{2}{13}{/tex}={tex}\frac{11}{26}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A man is known to speak truth 3 out of 4 times. He throws a die and reports that it is a six. Find the probability that it is actually a six.
question_text: A man is known to speak truth 3 out of 4 times. He throws a die and reports that it is a six. Find the probability that it is actually a six.
explanation: Let E be the event that the man reports that six occurs in the throwing of the dice and let S 1 be the event that six occurs and S 2 be the event six does not occur. Then P(S 1 ) =Probability that six occurs = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} P(S 2 ) =Probability that six does not occur = {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} P(E|S 1 ) = Probability that the man reports that six occurs when six has actually occurred on the die = Probability that the man speaks the truth = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} P(E|S 2 ) = Probability that the man reports that six occurs when six hasn't actually occurred on the die = Probability that the man does not speak the truth {tex}1 - \frac{3}{4} = \frac{1}{4}{/tex} Thus, by Bayes' theorem, we get P(S 1 |E) = Probability that the report of the man that six has occurred is actually a six {tex}= \frac{{P({S_1})P(E/{S_2})}}{{P({S_1})P(E/{S_1}) + P({S_2})P(E/{S_2})}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1/6×3/4}{1/6×3/4+5/6×1/4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {1}{8} \times \frac {24}{8} = \frac {3}{8}{/tex}
solution: Let E be the event that the man reports that six occurs in the throwing of the dice and let S 1 be the event that six occurs and S 2 be the event six does not occur. Then P(S 1 ) =Probability that six occurs = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} P(S 2 ) =Probability that six does not occur = {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} P(E|S 1 ) = Probability that the man reports that six occurs when six has actually occurred on the die = Probability that the man speaks the truth = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} P(E|S 2 ) = Probability that the man reports that six occurs when six hasn't actually occurred on the die = Probability that the man does not speak the truth {tex}1 - \frac{3}{4} = \frac{1}{4}{/tex} Thus, by Bayes' theorem, we get P(S 1 |E) = Probability that the report of the man that six has occurred is actually a six {tex}= \frac{{P({S_1})P(E/{S_2})}}{{P({S_1})P(E/{S_1}) + P({S_2})P(E/{S_2})}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1/6×3/4}{1/6×3/4+5/6×1/4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {1}{8} \times \frac {24}{8} = \frac {3}{8}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E be the event that the man reports that six occurs in the throwing of the dice and let S1 be the event that six occurs and S2 be the event six does not occur.
Then P(S1) =Probability that six occurs = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
P(S2) =Probability that six does not occur = {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}
P(E|S1) = Probability that the man reports that six occurs when six has actually occurred on the die
= Probability that the man speaks the truth = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
P(E|S2) = Probability that the man reports that six occurs  when six hasn't actually occurred on the die
= Probability that the man does not speak the truth {tex}1 - \frac{3}{4} = \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Thus, by Bayes' theorem, we get
P(S1|E) = Probability that the report of the man that six has occurred is actually a six
{tex}= \frac{{P({S_1})P(E/{S_2})}}{{P({S_1})P(E/{S_1}) + P({S_2})P(E/{S_2})}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1/6×3/4}{1/6×3/4+5/6×1/4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {1}{8} \times \frac {24}{8} = \frac {3}{8}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
There are three categories of students in a class of 60 students:
A : Very hardworking; B : Regular but not so hardworking; C : Careless and irregular. 10 students are in category A, 30 in category B and rest in category C. It is found that the probability of students of category A, unable to get good marks in the final year examination is 0.002, of category B it is 0.02 and of category C, this probability is 0.20. A student selected at random was found to be one who could not get good marks in the examination. Find the probability that this student is of category C.
question_text: There are three categories of students in a class of 60 students: A : Very hardworking; B : Regular but not so hardworking; C : Careless and irregular. 10 students are in category A, 30 in category B and rest in category C. It is found that the probability of students of category A, unable to get good marks in the final year examination is 0.002, of category B it is 0.02 and of category C, this probability is 0.20. A student selected at random was found to be one who could not get good marks in the examination. Find the probability that this student is of category C.
explanation: Let E denote the event that the student could not get good marks in the examination. Also, A : the event that student is very hardworking B : the event that student is regular but not so hardworking C : the event that student is careless and irregular Therefore, we have, P(A) = {tex}\frac{10}{60}{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac{30}{60}{/tex} and P(C) = {tex}\frac{20}{60}{/tex} Also,we have, {tex}P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right){/tex} = Pobability that the student of category A could not get good marks in the examination = 0.002 {tex}P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right){/tex} = Pobability that the student of category B could not get good marks in the examination = 0.02 {tex}P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex} = Pobability that the student of category C could not get good marks in the examination = 0.2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required probability is given by, {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{E}\right)=\frac{P(C) P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{P(A) P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{20}{60} \times 0.2}{\frac{10}{60} \times 0.002+\frac{30}{60} \times 0.02+\frac{20}{60} \times 0.2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{4.62}=\frac{400}{462}=\frac{200}{231}{/tex}
solution: Let E denote the event that the student could not get good marks in the examination. Also, A : the event that student is very hardworking B : the event that student is regular but not so hardworking C : the event that student is careless and irregular Therefore, we have, P(A) = {tex}\frac{10}{60}{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac{30}{60}{/tex} and P(C) = {tex}\frac{20}{60}{/tex} Also,we have, {tex}P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right){/tex} = Pobability that the student of category A could not get good marks in the examination = 0.002 {tex}P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right){/tex} = Pobability that the student of category B could not get good marks in the examination = 0.02 {tex}P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex} = Pobability that the student of category C could not get good marks in the examination = 0.2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required probability is given by, {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{E}\right)=\frac{P(C) P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{P(A) P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{20}{60} \times 0.2}{\frac{10}{60} \times 0.002+\frac{30}{60} \times 0.02+\frac{20}{60} \times 0.2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{4.62}=\frac{400}{462}=\frac{200}{231}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E denote the event that the student could not get good marks in the examination.
Also, A : the event that student is very hardworking
B : the event that student is regular but not so hardworking
C : the event that student is careless and irregular
Therefore, we have,
P(A) = {tex}\frac{10}{60}{/tex}, P(B) = {tex}\frac{30}{60}{/tex} and P(C) = {tex}\frac{20}{60}{/tex}
Also,we have,
{tex}P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right){/tex} = Pobability that the student of category A could not get good marks in the examination = 0.002
{tex}P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right){/tex} = Pobability that the student of category B could not get good marks in the examination = 0.02
{tex}P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex} = Pobability that the student of category C could not get good marks in the examination = 0.2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required probability is given by, {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{E}\right)=\frac{P(C) P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{P(A) P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{20}{60} \times 0.2}{\frac{10}{60} \times 0.002+\frac{30}{60} \times 0.02+\frac{20}{60} \times 0.2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{4.62}=\frac{400}{462}=\frac{200}{231}{/tex}

question_html: Prove that if E and F are independent events, then the events E and F' are also independent.
question_text: Prove that if E and F are independent events, then the events E and F' are also independent.
explanation: We have to prove that if E and F are independent events, then the events E and F' are also independent. Given, E and F are independent events, therefore {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F) = P(E ) P(F) ..... ( i) Now, we have, P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F') + P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F ) = P (E) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F') = P(E ) - P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F') = P(E) - P(E )P(F) [using Eq.1] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P (E {tex}\cap{/tex} F ') = P(E ) [l - P (F )] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F ') = P(E )P(F') {tex}\therefore{/tex} E and F 'are also independent events.
solution: We have to prove that if E and F are independent events, then the events E and F' are also independent. Given, E and F are independent events, therefore {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F) = P(E ) P(F) ..... ( i) Now, we have, P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F') + P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F ) = P (E) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F') = P(E ) - P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F') = P(E) - P(E )P(F) [using Eq.1] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P (E {tex}\cap{/tex} F ') = P(E ) [l - P (F )] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F ') = P(E )P(F') {tex}\therefore{/tex} E and F 'are also independent events.
solution_html:
We have to prove that if E and F are independent events, then the events E and F' are also independent.

Given, E and F are independent events, therefore
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E{tex}\cap{/tex}F) = P(E ) P(F) ..... ( i)
Now, we have,
P(E{tex}\cap{/tex}F') + P(E {tex}\cap{/tex}F ) = P (E)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E {tex}\cap{/tex}F') = P(E ) - P(E{tex}\cap{/tex}F)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E {tex}\cap{/tex}F') = P(E) - P(E )P(F) [using Eq.1] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}P (E {tex}\cap{/tex}F ') = P(E ) [l - P (F )]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(E {tex}\cap{/tex} F ') = P(E )P(F')
{tex}\therefore{/tex} E and F 'are also independent events.

question_html:
Often it is taken that a truthful person commands, more respect in the society. A man is known to speak the truth 4 out of 5 times. He throws a die and reports that it is a six. Find the probability that it is actually a six.
Do you also agree that the value of truthfulness leads to more respect in the society?
question_text: Often it is taken that a truthful person commands, more respect in the society. A man is known to speak the truth 4 out of 5 times. He throws a die and reports that it is a six. Find the probability that it is actually a six. Do you also agree that the value of truthfulness leads to more respect in the society?
explanation: Let E 1 : the event that getting a six E 2 : the event that not getting a six E = event that the man reports that it is a six Then, {tex} P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 6 } , P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 6 } = \frac { 5 } { 6 }{/tex} P(E/E 1 ) = probability that the man reports that six occurs, when si has actually occurred = probability that the man speaks the truth {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 5 }{/tex} P(E/E 2 )= probabiity that the man reports that six occurs, when six has not actually occurred = probability that the man does not speak the truth {tex}= 1 - \frac { 4 } { 5 } = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex} Probability of getting a six, given that the man reports it is to be six =P( {tex}E_1/E{/tex} ) {tex}= \frac { P \left( E / E _ { 1 } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E / E _ { 1 } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E / E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 4 } { 5 } \times \frac { 1 } { 6 } } { \left( \frac { 4 } { 5 } \times \frac { 1 } { 6 } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 5 } \times \frac { 5 } { 6 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 4 } { 5 } \times \frac { 1 } { 6 } } { \frac { 4 } { 30 } + \frac { 5 } { 30 } } = \frac { 4 / 30 } { 9 / 30 } = \frac { 4 } { 9 }{/tex} Hence, the required probability is {tex}\frac { 4 } { 9 }{/tex} . Yes, I agree with that the value of truthfulness leads to more respect in the society.
solution: Let E 1 : the event that getting a six E 2 : the event that not getting a six E = event that the man reports that it is a six Then, {tex} P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 6 } , P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 6 } = \frac { 5 } { 6 }{/tex} P(E/E 1 ) = probability that the man reports that six occurs, when si has actually occurred = probability that the man speaks the truth {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 5 }{/tex} P(E/E 2 )= probabiity that the man reports that six occurs, when six has not actually occurred = probability that the man does not speak the truth {tex}= 1 - \frac { 4 } { 5 } = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex} Probability of getting a six, given that the man reports it is to be six =P( {tex}E_1/E{/tex} ) {tex}= \frac { P \left( E / E _ { 1 } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E / E _ { 1 } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E / E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 4 } { 5 } \times \frac { 1 } { 6 } } { \left( \frac { 4 } { 5 } \times \frac { 1 } { 6 } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 5 } \times \frac { 5 } { 6 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 4 } { 5 } \times \frac { 1 } { 6 } } { \frac { 4 } { 30 } + \frac { 5 } { 30 } } = \frac { 4 / 30 } { 9 / 30 } = \frac { 4 } { 9 }{/tex} Hence, the required probability is {tex}\frac { 4 } { 9 }{/tex} . Yes, I agree with that the value of truthfulness leads to more respect in the society.
solution_html:
Let E1: the event that getting a six
E2: the event that not getting a six
E = event that the man reports that it is a six
Then, {tex} P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 6 } , P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 6 } = \frac { 5 } { 6 }{/tex}
P(E/E1 ) = probability that the man reports that six occurs, when si has actually occurred
= probability that the man speaks the truth{tex}= \frac { 4 } { 5 }{/tex}
P(E/E2)= probabiity that the man reports that six occurs, when six has not actually occurred
= probability that the man does not speak the truth
{tex}= 1 - \frac { 4 } { 5 } = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex}
Probability of getting a six, given that the man reports it is to be six =P({tex}E_1/E{/tex})
{tex}= \frac { P \left( E / E _ { 1 } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E / E _ { 1 } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E / E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \frac { 4 } { 5 } \times \frac { 1 } { 6 } } { \left( \frac { 4 } { 5 } \times \frac { 1 } { 6 } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 5 } \times \frac { 5 } { 6 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \frac { 4 } { 5 } \times \frac { 1 } { 6 } } { \frac { 4 } { 30 } + \frac { 5 } { 30 } } = \frac { 4 / 30 } { 9 / 30 } = \frac { 4 } { 9 }{/tex}
Hence, the required probability is {tex}\frac { 4 } { 9 }{/tex} .
Yes, I agree with that the value of truthfulness leads to more respect in the society.
