question_html: Bag I contains 3 red and 4 black balls and Bag II contains 4 red and 5 black balls. One ball is transferred from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn from Bag II. The ball so drawn is found to be red in colour. Find the probability that the transferred ball is black.
question_text: Bag I contains 3 red and 4 black balls and Bag II contains 4 red and 5 black balls. One ball is transferred from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn from Bag II. The ball so drawn is found to be red in colour. Find the probability that the transferred ball is black.
explanation: Let us define events, A 1 : Red ball is transferred from bag I to II. A 2 : Black ball is transferred from bag I to II. {tex}\therefore P\left(A_{1}\right)=\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(A_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{7}{/tex} Let X be the event that the drawn ball is red {tex}\therefore{/tex} when red ball is transferred from bag I to II, {tex}P\left(X | A_{1}\right)=\frac{5}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} And, when black ball is transferred from bag I to II, {tex}P\left(X | A_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} Hence, {tex}P\left(A_{2} | X\right)=\frac{P\left(A_{2}\right) P\left(X | A_{2}\right)}{P\left(A_{1}\right) P\left(X | A_{1}\right)+P\left(A_{2}\right) P\left(X | A_{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{2}{5}}{\frac{3}{7} \times \frac{1}{2}+\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{2}{5}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{16}{31}{/tex}
solution: Let us define events, A 1 : Red ball is transferred from bag I to II. A 2 : Black ball is transferred from bag I to II. {tex}\therefore P\left(A_{1}\right)=\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(A_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{7}{/tex} Let X be the event that the drawn ball is red {tex}\therefore{/tex} when red ball is transferred from bag I to II, {tex}P\left(X | A_{1}\right)=\frac{5}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} And, when black ball is transferred from bag I to II, {tex}P\left(X | A_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} Hence, {tex}P\left(A_{2} | X\right)=\frac{P\left(A_{2}\right) P\left(X | A_{2}\right)}{P\left(A_{1}\right) P\left(X | A_{1}\right)+P\left(A_{2}\right) P\left(X | A_{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{2}{5}}{\frac{3}{7} \times \frac{1}{2}+\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{2}{5}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{16}{31}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let us define events,
A1 : Red ball is transferred from bag I to II.
A2 : Black ball is transferred from bag I to II.
{tex}\therefore P\left(A_{1}\right)=\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(A_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{7}{/tex} 
Let X be the event that the drawn ball is red
{tex}\therefore{/tex} when red ball is transferred from bag I to II, {tex}P\left(X | A_{1}\right)=\frac{5}{10}{/tex}  = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
And, when black ball is transferred from bag I to II, {tex}P\left(X | A_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}P\left(A_{2} | X\right)=\frac{P\left(A_{2}\right) P\left(X | A_{2}\right)}{P\left(A_{1}\right) P\left(X | A_{1}\right)+P\left(A_{2}\right) P\left(X | A_{2}\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{2}{5}}{\frac{3}{7} \times \frac{1}{2}+\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{2}{5}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{16}{31}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: In a certain college, 4% of boys and 1% of girls are taller than 1.75 metres. Furthermore, 60% of the students in colleges are girls. A student selected at random from the college is found to be taller than 1.75 metres. Find the probability that the selected students are  girl.
question_text: In a certain college, 4% of boys and 1% of girls are taller than 1.75 metres. Furthermore, 60% of the students in colleges are girls. A student selected at random from the college is found to be taller than 1.75 metres. Find the probability that the selected students are girl.
explanation: Let A, E 1 and E 2 denote the events that the height of the student is more than 1.75 m, selected student is a girl and selected student is a boy, respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{60}{100}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{40}{100}{/tex} Now, {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{1}})=\frac{1}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{4}{100}{/tex} Using Bayes' theorem, we get Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{1}}{ A})=\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{1}{100}}{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{1}{100}+\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{4}{100}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6}{6+16}=\frac{6}{22}=\frac{3}{11}{/tex}
solution: Let A, E 1 and E 2 denote the events that the height of the student is more than 1.75 m, selected student is a girl and selected student is a boy, respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{60}{100}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{40}{100}{/tex} Now, {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{1}})=\frac{1}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{4}{100}{/tex} Using Bayes' theorem, we get Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{1}}{ A})=\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{1}{100}}{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{1}{100}+\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{4}{100}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6}{6+16}=\frac{6}{22}=\frac{3}{11}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A, E1 and E2 denote the events that the height of the student is more than 1.75 m, selected student is a girl and selected student is a boy, respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E1) = {tex}\frac{60}{100}{/tex}
P(E2) = {tex}\frac{40}{100}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{1}})=\frac{1}{100}{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{4}{100}{/tex}
Using Bayes' theorem, we get
Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{1}}{ A})=\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{1}{100}}{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{1}{100}+\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{4}{100}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{6}{6+16}=\frac{6}{22}=\frac{3}{11}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Suppose that 5% of men and 0.25% of women have grey hair. A grey haired person is selected at random. What is the probability of this person being male? Assume that there are equal number of males and females.
question_text: Suppose that 5% of men and 0.25% of women have grey hair. A grey haired person is selected at random. What is the probability of this person being male? Assume that there are equal number of males and females.
explanation: Men are represented by E 1 and women are represented by E 2 . {tex}\therefore {/tex} P (E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and P (E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} A represents grey hair persons. {tex}\therefore P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right) = \frac{5}{{100}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A/{E_2}} \right) = \frac{{25}}{{10000}}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {{E_1}/A} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A|{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1}) + P({E_2})P(A|{E_2})}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{{100}}}}{{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{{100}} + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{25}}{{10000}}}} = \frac{{\frac{5}{{100}}}}{{\frac{5}{{100}} + \frac{{25}}{{10000}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{5}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{525}}{{10000}}}} = \frac{5}{{100}} \times \frac{{10000}}{{525}} = \frac{{20}}{{21}}{/tex}
solution: Men are represented by E 1 and women are represented by E 2 . {tex}\therefore {/tex} P (E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and P (E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} A represents grey hair persons. {tex}\therefore P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right) = \frac{5}{{100}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A/{E_2}} \right) = \frac{{25}}{{10000}}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {{E_1}/A} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A|{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1}) + P({E_2})P(A|{E_2})}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{{100}}}}{{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{{100}} + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{25}}{{10000}}}} = \frac{{\frac{5}{{100}}}}{{\frac{5}{{100}} + \frac{{25}}{{10000}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{5}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{525}}{{10000}}}} = \frac{5}{{100}} \times \frac{{10000}}{{525}} = \frac{{20}}{{21}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Men are represented by E1 and women are represented by E2.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} P (E1) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and P (E2) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
A represents grey hair persons.

{tex}\therefore P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right) = \frac{5}{{100}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A/{E_2}} \right) = \frac{{25}}{{10000}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {{E_1}/A} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A|{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1}) + P({E_2})P(A|{E_2})}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{{100}}}}{{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{{100}} + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{25}}{{10000}}}} = \frac{{\frac{5}{{100}}}}{{\frac{5}{{100}} + \frac{{25}}{{10000}}}}{/tex}{tex} = \frac{{\frac{5}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{525}}{{10000}}}} = \frac{5}{{100}} \times \frac{{10000}}{{525}} = \frac{{20}}{{21}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A factory has two machines A and B. Past record shows that machine A produced 60% of the items of output and machine B produced 40% of the items. Further, 2% of the items produced by machine A and 1% produced by machine B were defective. All the items are put into one stockpile and then one item is chosen at random from this and is found to be defective. What is the probability that it was produced by machine B?
question_text: A factory has two machines A and B. Past record shows that machine A produced 60% of the items of output and machine B produced 40% of the items. Further, 2% of the items produced by machine A and 1% produced by machine B were defective. All the items are put into one stockpile and then one item is chosen at random from this and is found to be defective. What is the probability that it was produced by machine B?
explanation: Given: P(A) = {tex}\frac{{60}}{{100}}{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac{{40}}{{100}}{/tex} Let D denotes a defective item : {tex}\therefore {/tex} P(D|A) = {tex}\frac{2}{{100}}{/tex} and P(D|B) {tex} = \frac{1}{{100}}{/tex} P(B|D) = {tex}\frac{{P\left( B \right)P\left( {D|B} \right)}}{{P\left( A \right)P\left( {D|A} \right) + P\left( B \right)P\left( {D|B} \right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\frac{{40}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{60}}{{100}} \times \frac{2}{{100}} + \frac{{40}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{40}}{{120 + 40}} = \frac{{40}}{{160}} = \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
solution: Given: P(A) = {tex}\frac{{60}}{{100}}{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac{{40}}{{100}}{/tex} Let D denotes a defective item : {tex}\therefore {/tex} P(D|A) = {tex}\frac{2}{{100}}{/tex} and P(D|B) {tex} = \frac{1}{{100}}{/tex} P(B|D) = {tex}\frac{{P\left( B \right)P\left( {D|B} \right)}}{{P\left( A \right)P\left( {D|A} \right) + P\left( B \right)P\left( {D|B} \right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\frac{{40}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{60}}{{100}} \times \frac{2}{{100}} + \frac{{40}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{40}}{{120 + 40}} = \frac{{40}}{{160}} = \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: P(A) = {tex}\frac{{60}}{{100}}{/tex},  P(B) = {tex}\frac{{40}}{{100}}{/tex}
Let D denotes a defective item :
{tex}\therefore {/tex} P(D|A) = {tex}\frac{2}{{100}}{/tex}and P(D|B) {tex} = \frac{1}{{100}}{/tex}
P(B|D) ={tex}\frac{{P\left( B \right)P\left( {D|B} \right)}}{{P\left( A \right)P\left( {D|A} \right) + P\left( B \right)P\left( {D|B} \right)}}{/tex}
={tex}\frac{{\frac{{40}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{60}}{{100}} \times \frac{2}{{100}} + \frac{{40}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{/tex}
={tex}\frac{{40}}{{120 + 40}} = \frac{{40}}{{160}} = \frac{1}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Bag I contains 3 red and 4 black balls while another Bag II contains 5 red and 6 black balls. One ball is drawn at random from one of the bags and it is found to be red. Find the probability that it was drawn from Bag II.
question_text: Bag I contains 3 red and 4 black balls while another Bag II contains 5 red and 6 black balls. One ball is drawn at random from one of the bags and it is found to be red. Find the probability that it was drawn from Bag II.
explanation: Let E 1 : Bag selected is Bag I E 2 : Bag selected is Bag II A : Ball selected is Red B : Ball selected is Black Now, P(ball was drawn from bag II, if ball is red) = P( {tex}\frac{E_2}{A}{/tex} ) P( {tex}\frac{E_2}{A}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{P(A) P\left(A | E_{2}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A | E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A | E_{2}\right)}{/tex} P(E 1 ) = Probability bag selected is Bag I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = Probability bag selected is Bag II = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_2}{/tex} ) = Probability red ball was selected from Bag II = {tex}\frac{5}{5+6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{11}{/tex} P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_1}{/tex} ) = Probability red ball was selected from Bag I = {tex}\frac{3}{3+4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex} Putting values in formula, P( {tex}\frac{E_{2}} { A}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{11}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{7}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{11}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{11}}{\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{3}{7} + \frac{5}{11}\right]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{5}{11}}{\frac{33+35}{77}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{5}{11}}{\frac{68}{77}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{11} \times \frac{77}{68}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{35}{68}{/tex} Therefore, required probability is {tex}\frac{35}{68}{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 : Bag selected is Bag I E 2 : Bag selected is Bag II A : Ball selected is Red B : Ball selected is Black Now, P(ball was drawn from bag II, if ball is red) = P( {tex}\frac{E_2}{A}{/tex} ) P( {tex}\frac{E_2}{A}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{P(A) P\left(A | E_{2}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A | E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A | E_{2}\right)}{/tex} P(E 1 ) = Probability bag selected is Bag I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = Probability bag selected is Bag II = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_2}{/tex} ) = Probability red ball was selected from Bag II = {tex}\frac{5}{5+6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{11}{/tex} P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_1}{/tex} ) = Probability red ball was selected from Bag I = {tex}\frac{3}{3+4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex} Putting values in formula, P( {tex}\frac{E_{2}} { A}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{11}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{7}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{11}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{11}}{\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{3}{7} + \frac{5}{11}\right]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{5}{11}}{\frac{33+35}{77}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{5}{11}}{\frac{68}{77}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{11} \times \frac{77}{68}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{35}{68}{/tex} Therefore, required probability is {tex}\frac{35}{68}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 : Bag selected is Bag I
E2 : Bag selected is Bag II
A : Ball selected is Red
B : Ball selected is Black
Now,
P(ball was drawn from bag II, if ball is red) = P({tex}\frac{E_2}{A}{/tex})
P({tex}\frac{E_2}{A}{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{P(A) P\left(A | E_{2}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A | E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A | E_{2}\right)}{/tex}
P(E1) = Probability bag selected is Bag I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
P(E2) = Probability bag selected is Bag II = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
P({tex}\frac{A}{E_2}{/tex}) = Probability red ball was selected from Bag II = {tex}\frac{5}{5+6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{11}{/tex} 
P({tex}\frac{A}{E_1}{/tex}) = Probability red ball was selected from Bag I = {tex}\frac{3}{3+4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex}
Putting values in formula,
P({tex}\frac{E_{2}} { A}{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{11}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{7}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{11}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{11}}{\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{3}{7} + \frac{5}{11}\right]}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{5}{11}}{\frac{33+35}{77}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{5}{11}}{\frac{68}{77}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{11} \times \frac{77}{68}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{35}{68}{/tex}
Therefore, required probability is {tex}\frac{35}{68}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Given three identical boxes I, II and III each containing two coins. In box I both coins are gold coins, in box II, both are silver coins and in the box III, there is one gold and one silver coin. A person chooses a box at random and takes out a coin. If the coin is of gold, what is the probability that the other coin in the box is also of gold?
question_text: Given three identical boxes I, II and III each containing two coins. In box I both coins are gold coins, in box II, both are silver coins and in the box III, there is one gold and one silver coin. A person chooses a box at random and takes out a coin. If the coin is of gold, what is the probability that the other coin in the box is also of gold?
explanation: Let E 1 , E 2 and E 3 be the events that boxes I, II and III are chosen. P (E 1 ) = P (E 2 ) = P (E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} let A be the event the coin drawn is of gold. {tex}p(A|{E_1}) = \frac{2}{2} = 1{/tex} {tex}P(A|{E_2}) = 0{/tex} {tex}P(A|{E_3}) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}P({E_1}|A) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A|{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A|{E_1}) + P({E_2}) + P(A|{E_2}) + P({E_3})P(A|{E_3})}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{3}×1}{\frac{1}{3}×1+\frac{1}{3}×0+\frac{1}{3}×\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 , E 2 and E 3 be the events that boxes I, II and III are chosen. P (E 1 ) = P (E 2 ) = P (E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} let A be the event the coin drawn is of gold. {tex}p(A|{E_1}) = \frac{2}{2} = 1{/tex} {tex}P(A|{E_2}) = 0{/tex} {tex}P(A|{E_3}) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}P({E_1}|A) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A|{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A|{E_1}) + P({E_2}) + P(A|{E_2}) + P({E_3})P(A|{E_3})}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{3}×1}{\frac{1}{3}×1+\frac{1}{3}×0+\frac{1}{3}×\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1, E2 and E3 be the events that boxes I, II and III are chosen.
P (E1) = P (E2) = P (E3) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
let A be the event the coin drawn is of gold.
{tex}p(A|{E_1}) = \frac{2}{2} = 1{/tex}
{tex}P(A|{E_2}) = 0{/tex}
{tex}P(A|{E_3}) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}P({E_1}|A) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A|{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A|{E_1}) + P({E_2}) + P(A|{E_2}) + P({E_3})P(A|{E_3})}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{3}×1}{\frac{1}{3}×1+\frac{1}{3}×0+\frac{1}{3}×\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}  
{tex} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A card from a pack of 52 cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, two cards are drawn and are found to be hearts. Find the probability of the missing card to be a heart.
question_text: A card from a pack of 52 cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, two cards are drawn and are found to be hearts. Find the probability of the missing card to be a heart.
explanation: Consider the following events: E 1 = Missing card is a heart card, E 2 = Missing card is a spade card, E 3 = Missing card is a club card, E 4 = Missing card is a diamond card and, A = Drawing two heart cards from the remaining cards. Therefore,we have, {tex}P\left(E_{1}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{2}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} , {tex}P\left(E_{3}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{4}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} P (A/E 1 ) = Probability of drawing two heart cards given that one heart card is missing {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P\left(A / E_{1}\right)=\frac{ ^{12}{} C_{2}}{51}{/tex} P (A/E 2 ) = Probability of drawing two heart cards given that one spade card is missing {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P\left(A / E_{2}\right)=\frac {{^{13 }}\mathrm{C}_{2}}{51}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}P\left(A / E_{3}\right)=\frac{^{13 }\mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(A / E_{4}\right)=\frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} By Baye's Theorem,we have, Required probability = P (E 1 /A) {tex}=\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)+P\left(E_{3}\right) P\left(A / E_{3}\right)+P\left(E_{4}\right) P\left(A / E_{4}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{12}C_2}{51} \mathrm{}{}}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{12} C_2}{51} +\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{13} C_2}{51}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{13} C_2}{51} +\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{13}C_2}{51} \mathrm{}{}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{66}{66+78+78+78}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{11}{50}{/tex}
solution: Consider the following events: E 1 = Missing card is a heart card, E 2 = Missing card is a spade card, E 3 = Missing card is a club card, E 4 = Missing card is a diamond card and, A = Drawing two heart cards from the remaining cards. Therefore,we have, {tex}P\left(E_{1}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{2}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} , {tex}P\left(E_{3}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{4}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} P (A/E 1 ) = Probability of drawing two heart cards given that one heart card is missing {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P\left(A / E_{1}\right)=\frac{ ^{12}{} C_{2}}{51}{/tex} P (A/E 2 ) = Probability of drawing two heart cards given that one spade card is missing {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P\left(A / E_{2}\right)=\frac {{^{13 }}\mathrm{C}_{2}}{51}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}P\left(A / E_{3}\right)=\frac{^{13 }\mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(A / E_{4}\right)=\frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} By Baye's Theorem,we have, Required probability = P (E 1 /A) {tex}=\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)+P\left(E_{3}\right) P\left(A / E_{3}\right)+P\left(E_{4}\right) P\left(A / E_{4}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{12}C_2}{51} \mathrm{}{}}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{12} C_2}{51} +\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{13} C_2}{51}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{13} C_2}{51} +\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{13}C_2}{51} \mathrm{}{}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{66}{66+78+78+78}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{11}{50}{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider the following events:
E1 = Missing card is a heart card,
E2 = Missing card is a spade card,
E3 = Missing card is a club card,
E4 = Missing card is a diamond card
and, A = Drawing two heart cards from the remaining cards.
Therefore,we have,
{tex}P\left(E_{1}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{2}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}, {tex}P\left(E_{3}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{4}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
P (A/E1) = Probability of drawing two heart cards given that one heart card is missing
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad P\left(A / E_{1}\right)=\frac{ ^{12}{} C_{2}}{51}{/tex}
P (A/E2) = Probability of drawing two heart cards given that one spade card is missing
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad P\left(A / E_{2}\right)=\frac {{^{13 }}\mathrm{C}_{2}}{51}{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}P\left(A / E_{3}\right)=\frac{^{13 }\mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(A / E_{4}\right)=\frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex}
By Baye's Theorem,we have,
Required probability = P (E1/A)
{tex}=\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)+P\left(E_{3}\right) P\left(A / E_{3}\right)+P\left(E_{4}\right) P\left(A / E_{4}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{12}C_2}{51} \mathrm{}{}}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{12} C_2}{51} +\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{13} C_2}{51}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{13} C_2}{51} +\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{13}C_2}{51} \mathrm{}{}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{66}{66+78+78+78}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{11}{50}{/tex}

question_html: In a bolt factory, three machines, A, B, C, manufacture 25%, 35% and 40% of the total production respectively. Of their respective outputs, 5%, 4% and 2% are defective. A bolt is drawn at random from the total product and it is found to be defective. Find the probability that it was manufactured by the machine C.
question_text: In a bolt factory, three machines, A, B, C, manufacture 25%, 35% and 40% of the total production respectively. Of their respective outputs, 5%, 4% and 2% are defective. A bolt is drawn at random from the total product and it is found to be defective. Find the probability that it was manufactured by the machine C.
explanation: Let E 1 , E 2 and E 3 be the events of drawing a bolt produced by machine A, B and C respectively. Therefore,we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{25}{100}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{35}{100}=\frac{7}{20}{/tex} , and P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{40}{100}=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} Let E be the event of drawing a defective bolt. Therefore, {tex}P(\frac E {E_1}){/tex} = probability of drawing a defective bolt, given that it is produced by the machine A = {tex}\frac{5}{100}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac E {E_2}){/tex} = probability of drawing a defective bolt, given that it is produced by the machine B = {tex}\frac{4}{100}=\frac{1}{25}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac E {E_3}){/tex} = probability of drawing a defective bolt, given that it is produced by the machine C = {tex}\frac{2}{100}=\frac{1}{50}{/tex} Therefore, we have, Probability that the bolt drawn is manufactured by C, given that it is defective = {tex}P(\frac {E_3}{E}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(\frac E {E_{3}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{3}\right)}{P\left(\frac E {E_{1}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{1}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{2}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{2}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{3}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{3}\right)}{/tex} [by Bayes's theorem] {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{1}{50} \times \frac{2}{5}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{20} \times \frac{1}{4}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{25} \times \frac{7}{20}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{50} \times \frac{2}{5}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{125} \times \frac{2000}{69}\right)=\frac{16}{69}{/tex} Hence, the required probability is {tex}\frac{16}{69}{/tex} .
solution: Let E 1 , E 2 and E 3 be the events of drawing a bolt produced by machine A, B and C respectively. Therefore,we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{25}{100}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{35}{100}=\frac{7}{20}{/tex} , and P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{40}{100}=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} Let E be the event of drawing a defective bolt. Therefore, {tex}P(\frac E {E_1}){/tex} = probability of drawing a defective bolt, given that it is produced by the machine A = {tex}\frac{5}{100}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac E {E_2}){/tex} = probability of drawing a defective bolt, given that it is produced by the machine B = {tex}\frac{4}{100}=\frac{1}{25}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac E {E_3}){/tex} = probability of drawing a defective bolt, given that it is produced by the machine C = {tex}\frac{2}{100}=\frac{1}{50}{/tex} Therefore, we have, Probability that the bolt drawn is manufactured by C, given that it is defective = {tex}P(\frac {E_3}{E}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(\frac E {E_{3}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{3}\right)}{P\left(\frac E {E_{1}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{1}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{2}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{2}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{3}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{3}\right)}{/tex} [by Bayes's theorem] {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{1}{50} \times \frac{2}{5}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{20} \times \frac{1}{4}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{25} \times \frac{7}{20}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{50} \times \frac{2}{5}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{125} \times \frac{2000}{69}\right)=\frac{16}{69}{/tex} Hence, the required probability is {tex}\frac{16}{69}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let E1, E2 and E3 be the events of drawing a bolt produced by machine A, B and C respectively. Therefore,we have,
P(E1) = {tex}\frac{25}{100}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex}\frac{35}{100}=\frac{7}{20}{/tex}, and P(E3) = {tex}\frac{40}{100}=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} 
Let E be the event of drawing a defective bolt. Therefore,
{tex}P(\frac E {E_1}){/tex} = probability of drawing a defective bolt, given that it is produced by the machine A = {tex}\frac{5}{100}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac E {E_2}){/tex} = probability of drawing a defective bolt, given that it is produced by the machine B = {tex}\frac{4}{100}=\frac{1}{25}{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac E {E_3}){/tex} = probability of drawing a defective bolt, given that it is produced by the machine C = {tex}\frac{2}{100}=\frac{1}{50}{/tex}

Therefore, we have,
Probability that the bolt drawn is manufactured by C, given that it is defective
= {tex}P(\frac {E_3}{E}){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{P\left(\frac E {E_{3}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{3}\right)}{P\left(\frac E {E_{1}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{1}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{2}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{2}\right)+P\left(\frac E {E_{3}}\right) \cdot P\left(E_{3}\right)}{/tex} [by Bayes's theorem]
{tex}\frac{\left(\frac{1}{50} \times \frac{2}{5}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{20} \times \frac{1}{4}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{25} \times \frac{7}{20}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{50} \times \frac{2}{5}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{125} \times \frac{2000}{69}\right)=\frac{16}{69}{/tex}
Hence, the required probability is {tex}\frac{16}{69}{/tex}.

question_html: There are two bags, bag I and bag II. Bag I contains 4 white and 3 red balls while another bag II contains 3 white and 7 red balls. One ball is drawn at random from one of the bags and it is found to be white. Find the probability that it was drawn from bag I.
question_text: There are two bags, bag I and bag II. Bag I contains 4 white and 3 red balls while another bag II contains 3 white and 7 red balls. One ball is drawn at random from one of the bags and it is found to be white. Find the probability that it was drawn from bag I.
explanation: Let E 1 be the event that Bag I is chosen, E 2 be the event that Bag II is chosen, and A be the event that the drawn ball is white. Then, {tex}\quad P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 4 } { 7 }{/tex} and {tex}\quad P(A/{E_{2)}} = \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex} Now, (required probability= P(E 1 /A) {tex}= \frac { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) . P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) . P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) , P \left( A / E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { 4 } { 7 } } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { 4 } { 7 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { 3 } { 10 } } = \frac { \frac { 4 } { 7 } } { \frac { 4 } { 7 } + \frac { 3 } { 10 } } = \frac { \frac { 4 } { 7 } } { \frac { 40 + 21 } { 70 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 7 } \times \frac { 70 } { 61 } = \frac { 40 } { 61 }{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 be the event that Bag I is chosen, E 2 be the event that Bag II is chosen, and A be the event that the drawn ball is white. Then, {tex}\quad P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 4 } { 7 }{/tex} and {tex}\quad P(A/{E_{2)}} = \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex} Now, (required probability= P(E 1 /A) {tex}= \frac { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) . P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) . P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) , P \left( A / E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { 4 } { 7 } } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { 4 } { 7 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { 3 } { 10 } } = \frac { \frac { 4 } { 7 } } { \frac { 4 } { 7 } + \frac { 3 } { 10 } } = \frac { \frac { 4 } { 7 } } { \frac { 40 + 21 } { 70 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 7 } \times \frac { 70 } { 61 } = \frac { 40 } { 61 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 be the event that Bag I is chosen, E2 be the event that Bag II is chosen, and A be the event that the drawn ball is white.
Then, {tex}\quad P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 4 } { 7 }{/tex}
and{tex}\quad P(A/{E_{2)}} = \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex}
Now, (required probability= P(E1/A)
{tex}= \frac { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) . P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) . P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) , P \left( A / E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { 4 } { 7 } } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { 4 } { 7 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { 3 } { 10 } } = \frac { \frac { 4 } { 7 } } { \frac { 4 } { 7 } + \frac { 3 } { 10 } } = \frac { \frac { 4 } { 7 } } { \frac { 40 + 21 } { 70 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 4 } { 7 } \times \frac { 70 } { 61 } = \frac { 40 } { 61 }{/tex}

question_html: In a class, 5% of boys and 10% of girls have an IQ of more than 150. In the class, 60% are boys and rest are girls. If a student is selected at random and found to have an IQ of more than 150, then find the probability that the student is a boy.
question_text: In a class, 5% of boys and 10% of girls have an IQ of more than 150. In the class, 60% are boys and rest are girls. If a student is selected at random and found to have an IQ of more than 150, then find the probability that the student is a boy.
explanation: Let us define the events as E 1 : Boy is selected E 2 : Girl is selected A: The student has an IQ of more than 150 students. Now, P(E 1 )= {tex}60 \% = \frac { 60 } { 100 }{/tex} Now, P(E 2 {tex}= 40 \% = \frac { 40 } { 100 }{/tex} ) [ {tex}\because{/tex} in the class 60% students are boys, so 40% are girls, given] Now, P(A/E 1 ) = Probability that boys has an IQ of more than 150 {tex}= 5 \% = \frac { 5 } { 100 }{/tex} and P(A/E 2 ) = Probability that girls has an IQ of more than {tex}150 = 10 \% = \frac { 10 } { 100 }{/tex} The probability that the selected boy having IQ more than 150 is {tex}P \left( E _ { 1 } / A \right) = \frac { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) P \left( A / E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} [by Baye's theorem] {tex}= \frac { \frac { 60 } { 100 } \times \frac { 5 } { 100 } } { \left( \frac { 60 } { 100 } \times \frac { 5 } { 100 } \right) + \left( \frac { 40 } { 400 } \times \frac { 10 } { 100 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 300 } { 300 + 400 } = \frac { 300 } { 700 } = \frac { 3 } { 7 }{/tex} Hence, the required probability is 3/7
solution: Let us define the events as E 1 : Boy is selected E 2 : Girl is selected A: The student has an IQ of more than 150 students. Now, P(E 1 )= {tex}60 \% = \frac { 60 } { 100 }{/tex} Now, P(E 2 {tex}= 40 \% = \frac { 40 } { 100 }{/tex} ) [ {tex}\because{/tex} in the class 60% students are boys, so 40% are girls, given] Now, P(A/E 1 ) = Probability that boys has an IQ of more than 150 {tex}= 5 \% = \frac { 5 } { 100 }{/tex} and P(A/E 2 ) = Probability that girls has an IQ of more than {tex}150 = 10 \% = \frac { 10 } { 100 }{/tex} The probability that the selected boy having IQ more than 150 is {tex}P \left( E _ { 1 } / A \right) = \frac { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) P \left( A / E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} [by Baye's theorem] {tex}= \frac { \frac { 60 } { 100 } \times \frac { 5 } { 100 } } { \left( \frac { 60 } { 100 } \times \frac { 5 } { 100 } \right) + \left( \frac { 40 } { 400 } \times \frac { 10 } { 100 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 300 } { 300 + 400 } = \frac { 300 } { 700 } = \frac { 3 } { 7 }{/tex} Hence, the required probability is 3/7
solution_html:
Let us define the events as
E1: Boy is selected
E2: Girl is selected
A: The student has an IQ of more than 150 students.
Now, P(E1)={tex}60 \% = \frac { 60 } { 100 }{/tex}
Now, P(E2{tex}= 40 \% = \frac { 40 } { 100 }{/tex}) [{tex}\because{/tex} in the class 60% students are boys, so 40% are girls, given]
Now, P(A/E1) = Probability that boys has an IQ of
 more than 150
{tex}= 5 \% = \frac { 5 } { 100 }{/tex}
and P(A/E2) = Probability that girls has an IQ
of more than{tex}150 = 10 \% = \frac { 10 } { 100 }{/tex}
The probability that the selected boy having IQ more than 150 is
{tex}P \left( E _ { 1 } / A \right) = \frac { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) P \left( A / E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex}
[by Baye's theorem]

{tex}= \frac { \frac { 60 } { 100 } \times \frac { 5 } { 100 } } { \left( \frac { 60 } { 100 } \times \frac { 5 } { 100 } \right) + \left( \frac { 40 } { 400 } \times \frac { 10 } { 100 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 300 } { 300 + 400 } = \frac { 300 } { 700 } = \frac { 3 } { 7 }{/tex}
Hence, the required probability is 3/7

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A couple has two children, find the probability that both children are females, if it is known that the elder child is a female.
question_text: A couple has two children, find the probability that both children are females, if it is known that the elder child is a female.
explanation: A Couple has two children, Let boy be denoted by b & girl be denoted by g Let's define events; C : Both children are female. D : Elder child is female. {tex}\therefore{/tex} C = {(g, g)} P(C) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} And, D = {(g, b), (g, g)} P(D) = {tex}\frac{2}{4}{/tex} {tex}P(C \cap D) = \frac14{/tex} Hence, P(C|D) = {tex}\frac{P(C \cap D)}{P(D)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{4}}{\frac{2}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution: A Couple has two children, Let boy be denoted by b & girl be denoted by g Let's define events; C : Both children are female. D : Elder child is female. {tex}\therefore{/tex} C = {(g, g)} P(C) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} And, D = {(g, b), (g, g)} P(D) = {tex}\frac{2}{4}{/tex} {tex}P(C \cap D) = \frac14{/tex} Hence, P(C|D) = {tex}\frac{P(C \cap D)}{P(D)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{4}}{\frac{2}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
A Couple has two children,
Let boy be denoted by b & girl be denoted by g
Let's define events;
C : Both children are female.
D : Elder child is female.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} C = {(g, g)}
P(C) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
And, D = {(g, b), (g, g)}
P(D) = {tex}\frac{2}{4}{/tex}
{tex}P(C \cap D) = \frac14{/tex}
Hence, P(C|D) = {tex}\frac{P(C \cap D)}{P(D)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{4}}{\frac{2}{4}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

question_html: A bag contains 4 red and 4 black balls, another bag contains 2 red and 6 black balls. One of the two bags is selected at random and a ball is drawn from the bag which is found to be red. Find the probability that the ball is drawn from the first bag.
question_text: A bag contains 4 red and 4 black balls, another bag contains 2 red and 6 black balls. One of the two bags is selected at random and a ball is drawn from the bag which is found to be red. Find the probability that the ball is drawn from the first bag.

options.explanation: Let {tex}{E_1}{/tex} and {tex}{E_2}{/tex} are events of selection of the first and second bag respectively {tex}\therefore P({E_1}) = P({E_2}) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Let A = event of getting a red ball. {tex}\therefore P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 4 } { 8 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} , {tex}P \left( \mathrm { A } / \mathrm { E } _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 2 } { 8 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P \left( E _ { 1 } / \mathrm { A } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } } = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex}

explanation: Let {tex}{E_1}{/tex} and {tex}{E_2}{/tex} are events of selection of the first and second bag respectively {tex}\therefore P({E_1}) = P({E_2}) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Let A = event of getting a red ball. {tex}\therefore P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 4 } { 8 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} , {tex}P \left( \mathrm { A } / \mathrm { E } _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 2 } { 8 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P \left( E _ { 1 } / \mathrm { A } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } } = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: In a school, there are 1000 students, out of which 430 are girls. It is known that out of 430, 10% of the girls study in class XII, what is the probability that a student chosen randomly studies in class XII, given that the chosen student is a girl?
question_text: In a school, there are 1000 students, out of which 430 are girls. It is known that out of 430, 10% of the girls study in class XII, what is the probability that a student chosen randomly studies in class XII, given that the chosen student is a girl?
explanation: Let 'A' be the event that the chosen student studies in class XII and B be the event that the chosen student is a girl. There are 430 girls out of 1000 students So, P(B) = P (Chosen student is girl) {tex}= \frac { 430 } { 1000 } = \frac { 43 } { 100 }{/tex} Since, 10% of the girls studies in class XII So, total number of girls studies in class XII {tex} = \frac { 10 } { 100 } \times 430 = 43{/tex} Then, P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P (Chosen student is a girl of class XII) {tex}= \frac { 43 } { 1000 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required probability = P(A / B) {tex}= \frac { P ( A \cap B ) } { P ( B ) } \quad \left[ \because P ( A / B ) = \frac { P ( A \cap B ) } { P ( B ) } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 43 / 1000 } { 43 / 100 } = \frac { 1 } { 10 }{/tex}
solution: Let 'A' be the event that the chosen student studies in class XII and B be the event that the chosen student is a girl. There are 430 girls out of 1000 students So, P(B) = P (Chosen student is girl) {tex}= \frac { 430 } { 1000 } = \frac { 43 } { 100 }{/tex} Since, 10% of the girls studies in class XII So, total number of girls studies in class XII {tex} = \frac { 10 } { 100 } \times 430 = 43{/tex} Then, P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P (Chosen student is a girl of class XII) {tex}= \frac { 43 } { 1000 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required probability = P(A / B) {tex}= \frac { P ( A \cap B ) } { P ( B ) } \quad \left[ \because P ( A / B ) = \frac { P ( A \cap B ) } { P ( B ) } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 43 / 1000 } { 43 / 100 } = \frac { 1 } { 10 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let 'A' be the event that the chosen student studies in class XII and B be the event that the chosen student is a girl.
There are 430 girls out of 1000 students
So, P(B) = P (Chosen student is girl) {tex}= \frac { 430 } { 1000 } = \frac { 43 } { 100 }{/tex}
Since, 10% of the girls studies in class XII
So, total number of girls studies in class XII
{tex} = \frac { 10 } { 100 } \times 430 = 43{/tex}
Then, P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P (Chosen student is a girl of class XII)
{tex}= \frac { 43 } { 1000 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required probability = P(A / B)
{tex}= \frac { P ( A \cap B ) } { P ( B ) } \quad \left[ \because P ( A / B ) = \frac { P ( A \cap B ) } { P ( B ) } \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 43 / 1000 } { 43 / 100 } = \frac { 1 } { 10 }{/tex}

question_html:
Coloured balls are distributed in three bags as shown in the following table:

Bag Colour of Balls
  Black White Red
I 1 2 3
II 2 4 1
III 4 5 3

A bag is selected at random and then two balls are randomly drawn from selected bag. They happen to be black and red. What is the probability that they come from bag I?
question_text: Coloured balls are distributed in three bags as shown in the following table: Bag Colour of Balls Black White Red I 1 2 3 II 2 4 1 III 4 5 3 A bag is selected at random and then two balls are randomly drawn from selected bag. They happen to be black and red. What is the probability that they come from bag I?
explanation: Coloured balls are distributed in three bags as shown in the following table: Bag Colour of Balls Black White Red I 1 2 3 II 2 4 1 III 4 5 3 A bag is selected at random and then two balls are randomly drawn from selected bag. They happen to be black and red. We have to find hee probability that they come from bag I? Let us define the events as E 1 : Bag I is selected E 2 : Bag II is selected E 3 : Bag III is selected A: Two drawn balls are found to be black and red Since events E 1 , E 2 and E 3 are mutually exclusive and exhaustive events. {tex}\therefore P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) = P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = P \left( E _ { 3 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} Aso, P(A/E 1 ) = Probability that red and black balls are drawn from bag I {tex}= \frac { ^ { 1 } C _ { 1 } \times ^ { 3 } C _ { 1 } } {^ 6 C_2} [ \because \text { bag I contains total }{/tex} 6 balls including 1 black and 3 red balls] {tex}= \frac { 1 \times 3 } { \frac { 6 \times 5 } { 2 \times 1 } } = \frac { 1 \times 3 \times 2 } { 6 \times 5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex} P(A/E 2 ) = Probability that red and black balls are drawn from bag II {tex}= \frac { ^ { 2 } C _ { 1 } \times ^ { 1 } C _ { 1 } } { ^ { 7 } C _ { 2 } } [ \because \text { bag } 11 \text { contains total } 7 \text { balls }{/tex} including 2 black balls and 1 red ball] {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 1 } { \frac { 7 \times 6 } { 2 \times 1 } } = \frac { 2 \times 2 } { 7 \times 6 } = \frac { 2 } { 21 }{/tex} and P(A/E 3 ) = Probability that red and black balls are drawn from bag III= {tex}= \frac{{^4{C_1}{ \times ^3}{C_1}}}{{^{12}{C_2}}}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} bag III contains totall2 balls including 4 black balls and 3 red balls] {tex}= \frac { 4 \times 3 } { \frac { 12! } { 2!10 ! } } = \frac { 4 \times 3 \times 2 ! \times 10 ! } { 12 ! } = \frac { 4 \times 3 \times 2 } { 12 \times 11 } = \frac { 2 } { 11 }{/tex} The probability that the two drawn balls which are black and red, are come from bag I, {tex}P\left( {{E_1}/A} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1}) \cdot P(A/{E_1})}}{{\left[ {P\left( {{E_1}} \right) \cdot P\left( {A/{E_1}} \right) + P\left( {{E_2}} \right) \cdot P\left( {A/{E_2}} \right) + P\left( {{E_3}} \right)P\left( {A/{E_3}} \right)} \right]}}{/tex} [by Baye's theoram] {tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 1 } { 5 } } { \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 1 } { 5 } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 2 } { 21 } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 2 } { 11 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 5 } } { \left( \frac { 1 } { 5 } + \frac { 2 } { 21 } + \frac { 2 } { 11 } \right) } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 5 } } { \frac { 231 + 110 + 210 } { 1155 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1} { 5 } \times \frac { 1155 } { 551 } = \frac { 231 } { 551 }{/tex} Hence, the required probability is {tex}\frac { 231 } { 551 }{/tex} .
solution: Coloured balls are distributed in three bags as shown in the following table: Bag Colour of Balls Black White Red I 1 2 3 II 2 4 1 III 4 5 3 A bag is selected at random and then two balls are randomly drawn from selected bag. They happen to be black and red. We have to find hee probability that they come from bag I? Let us define the events as E 1 : Bag I is selected E 2 : Bag II is selected E 3 : Bag III is selected A: Two drawn balls are found to be black and red Since events E 1 , E 2 and E 3 are mutually exclusive and exhaustive events. {tex}\therefore P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) = P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = P \left( E _ { 3 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} Aso, P(A/E 1 ) = Probability that red and black balls are drawn from bag I {tex}= \frac { ^ { 1 } C _ { 1 } \times ^ { 3 } C _ { 1 } } {^ 6 C_2} [ \because \text { bag I contains total }{/tex} 6 balls including 1 black and 3 red balls] {tex}= \frac { 1 \times 3 } { \frac { 6 \times 5 } { 2 \times 1 } } = \frac { 1 \times 3 \times 2 } { 6 \times 5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex} P(A/E 2 ) = Probability that red and black balls are drawn from bag II {tex}= \frac { ^ { 2 } C _ { 1 } \times ^ { 1 } C _ { 1 } } { ^ { 7 } C _ { 2 } } [ \because \text { bag } 11 \text { contains total } 7 \text { balls }{/tex} including 2 black balls and 1 red ball] {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 1 } { \frac { 7 \times 6 } { 2 \times 1 } } = \frac { 2 \times 2 } { 7 \times 6 } = \frac { 2 } { 21 }{/tex} and P(A/E 3 ) = Probability that red and black balls are drawn from bag III= {tex}= \frac{{^4{C_1}{ \times ^3}{C_1}}}{{^{12}{C_2}}}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} bag III contains totall2 balls including 4 black balls and 3 red balls] {tex}= \frac { 4 \times 3 } { \frac { 12! } { 2!10 ! } } = \frac { 4 \times 3 \times 2 ! \times 10 ! } { 12 ! } = \frac { 4 \times 3 \times 2 } { 12 \times 11 } = \frac { 2 } { 11 }{/tex} The probability that the two drawn balls which are black and red, are come from bag I, {tex}P\left( {{E_1}/A} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1}) \cdot P(A/{E_1})}}{{\left[ {P\left( {{E_1}} \right) \cdot P\left( {A/{E_1}} \right) + P\left( {{E_2}} \right) \cdot P\left( {A/{E_2}} \right) + P\left( {{E_3}} \right)P\left( {A/{E_3}} \right)} \right]}}{/tex} [by Baye's theoram] {tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 1 } { 5 } } { \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 1 } { 5 } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 2 } { 21 } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 2 } { 11 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 5 } } { \left( \frac { 1 } { 5 } + \frac { 2 } { 21 } + \frac { 2 } { 11 } \right) } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 5 } } { \frac { 231 + 110 + 210 } { 1155 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1} { 5 } \times \frac { 1155 } { 551 } = \frac { 231 } { 551 }{/tex} Hence, the required probability is {tex}\frac { 231 } { 551 }{/tex} .
solution_html:
Coloured balls are distributed in three bags as shown in the following table:

Bag Colour of Balls
  Black White Red
I 1 2 3
II 2 4 1
III 4 5 3

A bag is selected at random and then two balls are randomly drawn from selected bag. They happen to be black and red. We have to  find hee probability that they come from bag I?

Let us define the events as
E1: Bag I is selected
E2: Bag II is selected
 E3: Bag III is selected
A: Two drawn balls are found to be black and red
Since events E1 , E2 and E3 are mutually exclusive and exhaustive events.
{tex}\therefore P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) = P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) = P \left( E _ { 3 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
Aso, P(A/E1) = Probability that red and black balls are drawn from bag I
{tex}= \frac { ^ { 1 } C _ { 1 } \times ^ { 3 } C _ { 1 } } {^ 6 C_2} [ \because \text { bag I contains total }{/tex}
6 balls including 1 black and 3 red balls]
{tex}= \frac { 1 \times 3 } { \frac { 6 \times 5 } { 2 \times 1 } } = \frac { 1 \times 3 \times 2 } { 6 \times 5 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad P \left( A / E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex}
P(A/E2) = Probability that red and black balls are drawn from bag II
{tex}= \frac { ^ { 2 } C _ { 1 } \times ^ { 1 } C _ { 1 } } { ^ { 7 } C _ { 2 } } [ \because \text { bag } 11 \text { contains total } 7 \text { balls }{/tex}
including 2 black balls and 1 red ball]
{tex}= \frac { 2 \times 1 } { \frac { 7 \times 6 } { 2 \times 1 } } = \frac { 2 \times 2 } { 7 \times 6 } = \frac { 2 } { 21 }{/tex}
and P(A/E3) = Probability that red and black balls
are drawn from bag III= {tex}= \frac{{^4{C_1}{ \times ^3}{C_1}}}{{^{12}{C_2}}}{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} bag III contains totall2 balls including 4 black balls and 3 red balls]
{tex}= \frac { 4 \times 3 } { \frac { 12! } { 2!10 ! } } = \frac { 4 \times 3 \times 2 ! \times 10 ! } { 12 ! } = \frac { 4 \times 3 \times 2 } { 12 \times 11 } = \frac { 2 } { 11 }{/tex}
The probability that the two drawn balls which are black and red, are come from bag I,
{tex}P\left( {{E_1}/A} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1}) \cdot P(A/{E_1})}}{{\left[ {P\left( {{E_1}} \right) \cdot P\left( {A/{E_1}} \right) + P\left( {{E_2}} \right) \cdot P\left( {A/{E_2}} \right) + P\left( {{E_3}} \right)P\left( {A/{E_3}} \right)} \right]}}{/tex}[by Baye's theoram]
{tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 1 } { 5 } } { \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 1 } { 5 } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 2 } { 21 } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 2 } { 11 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 5 } } { \left( \frac { 1 } { 5 } + \frac { 2 } { 21 } + \frac { 2 } { 11 } \right) } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 5 } } { \frac { 231 + 110 + 210 } { 1155 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1} { 5 } \times \frac { 1155 } { 551 } = \frac { 231 } { 551 }{/tex}
Hence, the required probability is {tex}\frac { 231 } { 551 }{/tex}.

question_html: A doctor is to visit a patient. From the past experience, it is known that the probabilities that he will come by train, bus, scooter or by other means of transport are respectively {tex}\frac{3}{{10}},\frac{1}{5},\frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex}. The probabilities that he will be late are {tex}\frac{1}{4},\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{{12}}{/tex}, if he comes by train, bus and scooter respectively, but if he comes by other means of transport, that he will not be late. When he arrives, he is late. What is the probability that he comes by train?
question_text: A doctor is to visit a patient. From the past experience, it is known that the probabilities that he will come by train, bus, scooter or by other means of transport are respectively {tex}\frac{3}{{10}},\frac{1}{5},\frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} . The probabilities that he will be late are {tex}\frac{1}{4},\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{{12}}{/tex} , if he comes by train, bus and scooter respectively, but if he comes by other means of transport, that he will not be late. When he arrives, he is late. What is the probability that he comes by train?
explanation: Let E be the event that the doctor visits the patient late and let T 1 , T 2 , T 3 , T 4 , be the event that the doctor comes by train, bus, scooter and other means of Transport respectively. P(T 1 ) = {tex} \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex} , P(T 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} , P(T 3 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} , P(T 4 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} P( {tex}\frac {E}{T_1}{/tex} ) = probability that the doctor arriving late comes by train {tex}= \frac{1}{4}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}P(\frac {E}{T_2}) = \frac{1}{3},P(\frac {E}{T_3}) = \frac{1}{{12}},P(\frac {E}{T_4}) = 0{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {T_1}{E}) {/tex} = {tex} \frac{{P({T_1})P(E/{T_1})}}{{P({T_1})P(E/{T_1}) + P({T_2})P(E/{T_2}) + P({T_3})P(E/{T_3}) + P({T_4})P(E/{T_4})}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{3}{10}\times \frac{1}{4}}{\frac{3}{10}\times\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{5}\times\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{10}\times\frac{1}{12}+\frac{2}{5}\times 0}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution: Let E be the event that the doctor visits the patient late and let T 1 , T 2 , T 3 , T 4 , be the event that the doctor comes by train, bus, scooter and other means of Transport respectively. P(T 1 ) = {tex} \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex} , P(T 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} , P(T 3 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} , P(T 4 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} P( {tex}\frac {E}{T_1}{/tex} ) = probability that the doctor arriving late comes by train {tex}= \frac{1}{4}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}P(\frac {E}{T_2}) = \frac{1}{3},P(\frac {E}{T_3}) = \frac{1}{{12}},P(\frac {E}{T_4}) = 0{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {T_1}{E}) {/tex} = {tex} \frac{{P({T_1})P(E/{T_1})}}{{P({T_1})P(E/{T_1}) + P({T_2})P(E/{T_2}) + P({T_3})P(E/{T_3}) + P({T_4})P(E/{T_4})}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{3}{10}\times \frac{1}{4}}{\frac{3}{10}\times\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{5}\times\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{10}\times\frac{1}{12}+\frac{2}{5}\times 0}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E be the event that the doctor visits the patient late and let T1, T2, T3, T4, be the event that the doctor comes by train, bus, scooter and other means of Transport respectively.
P(T1) = {tex} \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex}, P(T2) = {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}, P(T3) = {tex}\frac{1}{{10}}{/tex}, P(T4) = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
P({tex}\frac {E}{T_1}{/tex}) = probability that the doctor arriving late comes by train {tex}= \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}P(\frac {E}{T_2}) = \frac{1}{3},P(\frac {E}{T_3}) = \frac{1}{{12}},P(\frac {E}{T_4}) = 0{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac {T_1}{E}) {/tex} = {tex} \frac{{P({T_1})P(E/{T_1})}}{{P({T_1})P(E/{T_1}) + P({T_2})P(E/{T_2}) + P({T_3})P(E/{T_3}) + P({T_4})P(E/{T_4})}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{3}{10}\times \frac{1}{4}}{\frac{3}{10}\times\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{5}\times\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{10}\times\frac{1}{12}+\frac{2}{5}\times 0}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A car manufacturing factory has two plants, X and Y. Plant X manufactures 70% of cars and plant Y manufactures 30%. 80% of the cars at plant X and 90% of the cars at plant Y are rated of standard quality. A car is chosen at random and is found to be of standard quality. What is the probability that it has come from plant X?
question_text: A car manufacturing factory has two plants, X and Y. Plant X manufactures 70% of cars and plant Y manufactures 30%. 80% of the cars at plant X and 90% of the cars at plant Y are rated of standard quality. A car is chosen at random and is found to be of standard quality. What is the probability that it has come from plant X?
explanation: Let E be the event that the car is of standard quality. Let B 1 and B 2 be the events that the car is manufactured in plants X and Y, respectively. Now {tex}P({B_1}) = \frac{{70}}{{100}} = \frac{7}{{10}},P({B_2}) = \frac{{30}}{{100}} = \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}P(E|{B_1}){/tex} = Probability that a standard quality car is manufactured in plant X {tex} = \frac{{80}}{{100}} = \frac{8}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}P(E|{B_2}) = \frac{{90}}{{100}} = \frac{9}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}P({B_1}|E){/tex} = Probability that a standard quality car has come from plant X {tex} = \frac{{P({B_1}) \times P(E|{B_1})}}{{P({B_1}).P(E|{B_1}) + P({B_2}).P(E|{B_2})}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{7}{{10}} \times \frac{8}{{10}}}}{{\frac{7}{{10}} \times \frac{8}{{10}} + \frac{3}{{10}} \times \frac{9}{{10}}}} = \frac{{56}}{{83}}{/tex} Hence the required probability is {tex}\frac{{56}}{{83}}{/tex} .
solution: Let E be the event that the car is of standard quality. Let B 1 and B 2 be the events that the car is manufactured in plants X and Y, respectively. Now {tex}P({B_1}) = \frac{{70}}{{100}} = \frac{7}{{10}},P({B_2}) = \frac{{30}}{{100}} = \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}P(E|{B_1}){/tex} = Probability that a standard quality car is manufactured in plant X {tex} = \frac{{80}}{{100}} = \frac{8}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}P(E|{B_2}) = \frac{{90}}{{100}} = \frac{9}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}P({B_1}|E){/tex} = Probability that a standard quality car has come from plant X {tex} = \frac{{P({B_1}) \times P(E|{B_1})}}{{P({B_1}).P(E|{B_1}) + P({B_2}).P(E|{B_2})}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{7}{{10}} \times \frac{8}{{10}}}}{{\frac{7}{{10}} \times \frac{8}{{10}} + \frac{3}{{10}} \times \frac{9}{{10}}}} = \frac{{56}}{{83}}{/tex} Hence the required probability is {tex}\frac{{56}}{{83}}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let E be the event that the car is of standard quality. Let B1 and B2 be the events that the car is manufactured in plants X and Y, respectively. Now
{tex}P({B_1}) = \frac{{70}}{{100}} = \frac{7}{{10}},P({B_2}) = \frac{{30}}{{100}} = \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex}
{tex}P(E|{B_1}){/tex} = Probability that a standard quality car is manufactured in plant X
{tex} = \frac{{80}}{{100}} = \frac{8}{{10}}{/tex}
{tex}P(E|{B_2}) = \frac{{90}}{{100}} = \frac{9}{{10}}{/tex}
{tex}{/tex}
{tex}{/tex}

{tex}P({B_1}|E){/tex} = Probability that a standard quality car has come from plant X
{tex} = \frac{{P({B_1}) \times P(E|{B_1})}}{{P({B_1}).P(E|{B_1}) + P({B_2}).P(E|{B_2})}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\frac{7}{{10}} \times \frac{8}{{10}}}}{{\frac{7}{{10}} \times \frac{8}{{10}} + \frac{3}{{10}} \times \frac{9}{{10}}}} = \frac{{56}}{{83}}{/tex}
Hence the required probability is {tex}\frac{{56}}{{83}}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A box contains 2 gold and 3 silver coins. Another box contains 3 gold and 3 silver coins. A box is chosen at random, and a coin is drawn from it. If the selected coin is a gold coin, find the probability that it was drawn from the second box.
question_text: A box contains 2 gold and 3 silver coins. Another box contains 3 gold and 3 silver coins. A box is chosen at random, and a coin is drawn from it. If the selected coin is a gold coin, find the probability that it was drawn from the second box.
explanation: Let G: Gold coins S: Silver coins A : 1 st box B : 2 nd box Assuming, selecting bags is of equal probability i.e. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} We want to find {tex}P(\frac B G){/tex} , i.e. probability of selected gold coin is from the 2 nd box. Therefore,by Baye's theorm,we have, {tex}P(\frac B G){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B}) \cdot \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G} | \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A}) \cdot \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G} | \mathrm{A})+\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B}) \cdot \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G} | \mathrm{B})}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{3}{6}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+\left(\frac{3}{6}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex} Conclusion: Therefore, the probability of selected gold coin is from the 2 nd box is {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
solution: Let G: Gold coins S: Silver coins A : 1 st box B : 2 nd box Assuming, selecting bags is of equal probability i.e. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} We want to find {tex}P(\frac B G){/tex} , i.e. probability of selected gold coin is from the 2 nd box. Therefore,by Baye's theorm,we have, {tex}P(\frac B G){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B}) \cdot \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G} | \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A}) \cdot \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G} | \mathrm{A})+\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B}) \cdot \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G} | \mathrm{B})}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{3}{6}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+\left(\frac{3}{6}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex} Conclusion: Therefore, the probability of selected gold coin is from the 2 nd box is {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let G: Gold coins
S: Silver coins
A : 1st box
B : 2nd box
Assuming, selecting bags is of equal probability i.e. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
We want to find {tex}P(\frac B G){/tex}, i.e. probability of selected gold coin is from the 2nd box.

Therefore,by Baye's theorm,we have,
{tex}P(\frac B G){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B}) \cdot \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G} | \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A}) \cdot \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G} | \mathrm{A})+\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B}) \cdot \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G} | \mathrm{B})}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{3}{6}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+\left(\frac{3}{6}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
Conclusion: Therefore, the probability of selected gold coin is from the 2nd box is {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Ramesh appears for an interview for two posts, A and B, for which the selection is independent. The probability for his selection for Post A is (1/6) and for Post B, it is (1/7). Find the probability that Ramesh is selected for at least one post.
question_text: Ramesh appears for an interview for two posts, A and B, for which the selection is independent. The probability for his selection for Post A is (1/6) and for Post B, it is (1/7). Find the probability that Ramesh is selected for at least one post.
explanation: Let E 1 = event that Ramesh is selected for the post A, and E 2 = event that Ramesh is selected for the post B. Therefore,we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} and P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{7}{/tex} Clearly, E 1 and E 2 are independent events. {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E 1 {tex}\cap{/tex} E 2 ) = P(E 1 ) {tex}\times{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{7}\right)=\frac{1}{42}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(Ramesh is selected for at least one post) = P(E 1 {tex}\cup{/tex} E 2 ) = P(E 1 ) + P(E 2 ) - P(E 1 {tex}\cap{/tex} E 2 ) {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{7}-\frac{1}{42}\right)=\frac{12}{42}=\frac{2}{7}{/tex} {tex}{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 = event that Ramesh is selected for the post A, and E 2 = event that Ramesh is selected for the post B. Therefore,we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} and P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{7}{/tex} Clearly, E 1 and E 2 are independent events. {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E 1 {tex}\cap{/tex} E 2 ) = P(E 1 ) {tex}\times{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{7}\right)=\frac{1}{42}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(Ramesh is selected for at least one post) = P(E 1 {tex}\cup{/tex} E 2 ) = P(E 1 ) + P(E 2 ) - P(E 1 {tex}\cap{/tex} E 2 ) {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{7}-\frac{1}{42}\right)=\frac{12}{42}=\frac{2}{7}{/tex} {tex}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 = event that Ramesh is selected for the post A, and E2 = event that Ramesh is selected for the post B.

Therefore,we have,
 P(E1) = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} and P(E2) = {tex}\frac{1}{7}{/tex}
Clearly, E1 and E2 are independent events.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E1 {tex}\cap{/tex} E2) = P(E1) {tex}\times{/tex} P(E2) = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{7}\right)=\frac{1}{42}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P(Ramesh is selected for at least one post)
= P(E1 {tex}\cup{/tex} E2)
= P(E1) + P(E2) - P(E1 {tex}\cap{/tex} E2)
{tex}=\left(\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{7}-\frac{1}{42}\right)=\frac{12}{42}=\frac{2}{7}{/tex} {tex}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A problem in mathematics is given to three students whose chances of solving it correctly are 1/ 2, 1/ 3 and 1/4 respectively. What is the probability that only one of them solves it correctly?
question_text: A problem in mathematics is given to three students whose chances of solving it correctly are 1/ 2, 1/ 3 and 1/4 respectively. What is the probability that only one of them solves it correctly?
explanation: Let A, B, C be the given students and let E 1 , E 2 and E 3 be the events that the problem is solved by A, B, C respectively. Then, {tex}\bar{E}_{1}{/tex} , {tex}\bar{E}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\bar{E}_{3}{/tex} are the events that the given problem is not solved by A, B, C respectively. Therefore, we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ; P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} ; P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} ; {tex}P\left(\bar{E}_{1}\right)=\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2} ; P\left(\bar{E}_{2}\right)=\left(1-\frac{1}{3}\right)=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(\bar{E}_{3}\right)=\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} P(exactly one of them solves the problem) {tex}=P\left[\left(E_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right) \text { or }\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap E_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right) \text { or }\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap E_{3}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=P\left(E_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right)+P\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap E_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right)+P\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap E_{3}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left\{P\left(E_{1}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{2}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{3}\right)\right\}+\left[P\left(\bar{E}_{1}\right) \times P\left(E_{2}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{3}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}+\left[P\left(\bar{E}_{1}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{2}\right) \times P\left(E_{3}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{12}\right)=\frac{11}{24}{/tex} {tex}{/tex}
solution: Let A, B, C be the given students and let E 1 , E 2 and E 3 be the events that the problem is solved by A, B, C respectively. Then, {tex}\bar{E}_{1}{/tex} , {tex}\bar{E}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\bar{E}_{3}{/tex} are the events that the given problem is not solved by A, B, C respectively. Therefore, we have, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ; P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} ; P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} ; {tex}P\left(\bar{E}_{1}\right)=\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2} ; P\left(\bar{E}_{2}\right)=\left(1-\frac{1}{3}\right)=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(\bar{E}_{3}\right)=\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} P(exactly one of them solves the problem) {tex}=P\left[\left(E_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right) \text { or }\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap E_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right) \text { or }\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap E_{3}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=P\left(E_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right)+P\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap E_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right)+P\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap E_{3}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left\{P\left(E_{1}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{2}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{3}\right)\right\}+\left[P\left(\bar{E}_{1}\right) \times P\left(E_{2}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{3}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}+\left[P\left(\bar{E}_{1}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{2}\right) \times P\left(E_{3}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{12}\right)=\frac{11}{24}{/tex} {tex}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A, B, C be the given students and let E1, E2 and E3 be the events that the problem is solved by A, B, C respectively. Then, {tex}\bar{E}_{1}{/tex}, {tex}\bar{E}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\bar{E}_{3}{/tex} are the events that the given problem is not solved by A, B, C respectively.

Therefore, we have,

 P(E1) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}; P(E2) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}; P(E3) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex};
{tex}P\left(\bar{E}_{1}\right)=\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2} ; P\left(\bar{E}_{2}\right)=\left(1-\frac{1}{3}\right)=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(\bar{E}_{3}\right)=\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
P(exactly one of them solves the problem)
{tex}=P\left[\left(E_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right) \text { or }\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap E_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right) \text { or }\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap E_{3}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=P\left(E_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right)+P\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap E_{2} \cap \bar{E}_{3}\right)+P\left(\bar{E}_{1} \cap \bar{E}_{2} \cap E_{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left\{P\left(E_{1}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{2}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{3}\right)\right\}+\left[P\left(\bar{E}_{1}\right) \times P\left(E_{2}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{3}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}+\left[P\left(\bar{E}_{1}\right) \times P\left(\bar{E}_{2}\right) \times P\left(E_{3}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{12}\right)=\frac{11}{24}{/tex} {tex}{/tex}
